title: Diabetic after pregnancy
post text:
Hi everyone! Just looking to see if anyone else had the same experience as me. My endocrinologist said he has seen very few cases like mine, so just trying to see if another mom went through the same.

I got pregnant with my first child, I was a little bit overweight (BMI 27) but I was not insulin resistant or diabetic (insulin levels & blood sugar was tested a month or so before getting pregnant). My OB was very thorough in making sure my blood work was done before trying to get pregnant. I had no risk factors except the BMI, no family history either. 

I got diagnosed at 26 weeks with gestational diabetes. Diet controlled and it never went away after birth. I was tested for type 1, LADA, MODY, they even ran genetic tests and nothing came out. 

My diabetes has been controlled (4.8-5 A1C) without medication for the last 3 years (since I was diagnosed with GDM), so that’s not my concern. 

I guess I’m just curious to see if other moms just went diabetic after one pregnancy as well even if they had no issues before. My doctors all told me it’s very rare but possible that one pregnancy was enough for my body to develop diabetes. 

Anyone else have a similar story? Just looking to relate to other women who might have gone through the same thing. Thank you!
label: no stigma

title: When I started this journey a few days ago
post text: My blood sugar was reading anywhere between 13-18 with maybe one or two lower numbers. After fully amending my diet, beginning yesterday, and allowing myself a few carbs a day, my blood sugars have been at a steady 10 today. This of course is still high but it's a significant drop instead of seeing it read 18 everytime I took it! I think I'm heading in the right direction. I am also currently going through treatment for bulimia which is comprised of episodes of binging and purging and it's more than likely the case that the binging is what got me here. I am a Cereal lover and yesterday evening at about 9pm I had three oatibix with some semi skimmed milk and was so pleased when I wasn't showing a very high number when I took my reading this morning. This tells me that even though it's a decent amount of carbs in the Cereal, it's obviously drastically lower than what I was having. For my morning meal I had seeded bread (toasted) with some cottage cheese. My morning reading was 10.1 and my reading before lunch was 10.2 so I know that I'm safe with my choice of bread. For lunch I opted for 5 slices of cucumber, 3 small breaded chicken burgers (i checked the carbs), 2 boiled eggs and some spring onions. This is a drastic change from the very carb heavy, starchy meals I was eating previously. This is a success for me and I now know that I can still eat well and eat things I enjoy without it affecting my blood sugar level :)

title: Metformin/Insulin

Hi, I’m just a bit confused as to what’s happening so I’m just coming on here to share my experience. I’m 20 years old and around 185 pounds. In just 1 year I gained 40+ pounds, having my blood pressure skyrocket and led to all these other health issues, I felt like I was going to faint every other day but I kept eating junk thinking I just needed food. Two years ago I met with a nutritionist specifically for Ed and worked with her keeping a strict meal plan and eating times which at first was the hardest thing to do ever and even sneaked food to my room in the middle of the night because I felt hungry. My endocrinologist says it’s because some people are more sensitive to changes in blood sugar and since I lost so much weight my body is not used to this insulin production? He told me two years ago I was insulin resistant and then put me on 500mg of Metformin, he says I have insulin sensitivity and wants me to up my dose and start 2 500mg Metformin every day now and let him know how it goes in 3 weeks. title: First time with fluctuating glucose
post text: So I’m a newly diagnosed t2 diabetic within the last year and I’ve been going through a journey with my pcp trying to figure out a medicine/insulin treatment that works for my body and diabetes and so far we figured everything out and since April of 2024 I have lowered my A1C from 12 point something to like 6.2 and my doctor was super proud of me and I’m proud of myself too, but today for the first time I experienced an intense fluctuating blood sugar? I think as at work today and I felt fine all day but then all of a sudden I started feeling sick (normal symptoms I get when my sugar starts dropping) so I figured I was either having a low blood sugar or that I was having a blood sugar plummet and needed glucose tablets ASAP type thing. But when I checked my glucose it was only 109? And I’ve had normal blood sugar ranges for months now so for me to feel sick at 109 is not normal. And 20 minutes later I was at 74 so I knew I was dropping fast. I took the regular serving size of 4 glucose tablets and waited 15 minutes all for my levels to only go up to 86 and I was STILL feeling sick. So I went back to work cuz at first I felt a little better but I was told to try eating and see if that would help (I hadn’t taken any more glucose tablets after the 4 I originally took) so I ate 4 SMALL turkey and cheese with lettuce pinwheels and drank like 4 ounces of orange juice and felt fine but 10 minutes later I checked my blood sugars and they were at 166!!! My jaw DROPPED because my sugars hadn’t been that high in a LONG time (I’m talking like MONTHS ago) and I was super confused cuz at first I felt fine? Then 15 minutes go by and I start feeling sick again so I went home early cuz I didn’t have insulin on me (because I normally watch my carbs during lunch hour so I don’t have to need insulin). When I get home I check my blood sugar again and now it’s at 119 only maybe 30 minutes after it was checked and showing 166 but I’m still feeling kinda icky and I’m so confused on why my blood sugars are being so up and down. This is the first time this has happened to me so idk why it’s being like this. Has anyone else dealt with this? Please someone give me some advice on what to do for it this happens again.

title: My CGM Said 21, Fingerstick Said 12—WTF? Anyone Else Had This Happen?
post text:
Hey everyone,
 
 I had a crazy (and honestly scary) experience today that I feel like some of you can probably relate to. My CGM was showing 20-21 mmol/L, so I naturally thought, “Okay, this is bad, I need to correct.” I had already taken 7 units of insulin, then another 3 units, and was about to inject even more when I decided to double-check with a regular fingerstick test.
 
 Guess what? My actual blood sugar was only 12 mmol/L. Like, what the hell?! If I had kept trusting the CGM and kept injecting, I could’ve crashed hard. And this isn’t even the first time this has happened.
 
 I get that CGMs measure interstitial fluid and there’s sometimes a lag, but a 9 mmol/L difference is just insane. It really makes me wonder how many times I’ve overcorrected because of bad CGM readings, leading to unnecessary lows.
 
 So now, I’m making it a rule: if my CGM says anything above 15 mmol/L, I’m double-checking with a fingerstick before injecting. I’d rather take a few extra seconds than risk a massive hypo because of a faulty reading.
 
 Has anyone else dealt with this? How do you avoid overcorrecting when CGM readings seem off?

title: Troubles getting Pregabalin prescription filled, what should I have done differently?
post text:
I'm in the US and have diabetic peripheral neuropathy. I take pregabalin, it's the only thing that has helped with the pain.

This last week I went to a twice postponed appt with my pain management provider. He is difficult to schedule with. At the appt, I was given my 2nd Qutenza (capsaicin) treatment (it doesn't help much with the pain). I also talked with my doctor briefly and told him I was taking more than the prescribed pregabalin to manage my pain. I asked him for an increase in the dosage, including 165 mg extended-release (ER) sent to a Vons pharmacy (associated with Albertsons, Sav-On, etc). All seemed right with the world and I was hopeful I'd get my medication.

I called the pharmacy the first time later that day and was informed that they couldn't get the ER dose, it was on backorder. In the US, federal law makes it difficult to deal with controlled substances like opioids and narcotics, and pregabalin is classified as a controlled substance. This means:

* Prescriptions may not be transferred from pharmacy to pharmacy.
* provider needs to call in the same prescription to a different pharmacy or a different prescription to the same pharmacy.
* prescription information is available to all pharmacists (I guess to prevent duplicate, Dr. and pharmacy shopping on strong pain meds.

Also, in the US, pharmacy wholesale providers like McKesson and AmerisourceBergen will not inform a pharmacy beforehand if they can obtain a medication dosage in the overnight order. So pharmacies have to place an order and wait and see the next day if the meds come in for the patient.

So, I waited until mid-day the next day, contacted the pharmacy a 2nd time and was told it didn't come in. I asked the pharmacy (3rd contact) to contact the Dr. to get a dose they did have in stock. I also messaged the Dr. (1st time) the same. The 3rd day I found out (4th contact) the pharmacy still didn't have any dose. I contacted the Dr. office (2nd time) and was informed the Dr. was out of office until Monday with no covering provider. The receptionist also talked with my pharmacist and together they concluded that it was too early for me to have medication, despite having a Dr's prescription (3rd and 4th time comm with Dr. office).

I went to the pharmacist (5th contact) and she told me she checked on my previous prescriptions and it was my fault I ran out, I didn't take them as prescribed. I explained to her that was thoroughly discussed with my Dr. and that's why a new prescription was issued. She said she heard that it was a 'replacement' prescription and thus subject to the govt timelines but couldn't produce any documentation to back this up. I left and with no word from my Dr. office I went to urgent care and finally obtained a temporary prescription and had it filled at a different pharmacy. In the end I was in misery from pain and withdrawal to a bad point.

What should I have done differently to avoid the delays?

title: Good news
post text:
Good news!

Good evening, 
I feel I wanted to share some hope and good news here as people going through same struggles as me will understand me more and what it means more than the people around me.
Back in September, I found out I was 3 months pregnant! For someone who has PCOS and type 2 diabetes (currently awaiting genetics test) to confirm if its type 2 or not and other internal complications.. never had one done since I was diagnosed at age 18 and I'm now 34. I went into the pregnancy with extremely high HbA1c of 120, which they automatically added me on insulin on top of the meds I was already taking! This number has been going up from normal to this high with my normal doctor ignoring it and not doing anything about it... we are now in December and I got a call today telling me my HbA1c is 44!!! Effing 44 I'm beyond happy... and the pregnancy diabetes doctor I'm seeing had the audacity to tell me back in September that I'll now be on insulin for life... I managed to come off insulin by myself in my 20's and I'm aiming to come back off insulin again after I give birth. It takes hard work, dedication and alot of sacrifice in food!!! But all is possible.

title: Fries
post text:
I've been in remission since I changed my diet and was able to get my levels down. I've drastically reduced the carbs and added loads of veggies and protein to what I eat. 

I then got a Libre 2 to test different foods and what they do to me. I had a post the other day about a Chinese takeaway that scared me with ups and downs but yesterday I went to a concert and didn't have many options to eat. 

Ended up eating a large portion of fries and the chicken it came with was breaded (I didn't know) so I removed the outside and only had the chicken part (but it was obviously fried anyway). The spike was not as high as I had with other things before, but for the first time it was still high and going up 2 hours after the meal, while previously it would always come down by the time I got to 2 hours. It was a bit scary so I did a power walk for 15 minutes and got it down - it went up again when I stopped exercising, but it didn't reach the spike I had for the 3 hours after the meal (which was going over the ideal target).

I guess it was a combination of a lot of carbs with a high fat meal. My question is - any advice of what I could pair with fries in the future to help controlling the sugar? Obviously it would have to be a smaller portion anyway but any other ideas? Or should I just give up on fries all together?

title: Just diagnosed: Diabetes Type 2
post text:
Hi all,

I am from Australia and wanted to share and seek advice with this community and folks who are also T2.

Post-covid I stopped exercising and going to the gym, eating junk food and basically got overweight (however if there was a zombie apocalypse I think I am just about fit enough to get away). I am 42 and recently within the last few weeks start having issues with my eyesight with nearsightedness. I can still read off my mobile, laptop, monitor, books but objects far away are blurry (especially faces). Prior to this I was was thirsty and drinking a lot especially at night and most of the time I felt and still feel tired.

Just got my blood test results today and doctor confirmed I am diabetic t2 + high blood pressure + high cholesterol :(   
  
I got prescribed:

1. Mizart - for high blood pressure

2. Metformin - for diabetes

\+ I got that BG Monitoring System (the thought on pricking myself doesn't sound fun either)

Good news:

I used to be a gym junkie pre-covid and I was very strict with my diet (I didn't eat anything with sugar for 2 x years) so I have a plan kick start a healthy lifestyle. My sister is also an optometrist so I booked an appointment to see her this weekend, hopefully there are no bigger issues (that part scares me a bit).

Questions:

1. Have had any issues with your eyesight? Were you nearsighted? Did it go away?

2. What lifestyle changes did you make?

3. Doctor said I might be on medication for a long while, anyone ever come off medication?

4. Any advice or tip are welcome.

  
I'll share more as I go through the weeks.

title: Let's talk about Adaptogenic Mushrooms plz.
post text:
Okay so all of those ads for Adaptogenic Mushroom teas and supplements. Ya know, Ryze and stuff. So my manager had a bag out on her desk at work. I mentioned that I had seen the ads but I could not justify purchasing it because it's expensive and also screwed over a few people in my department who would tried it (I work in office jobs so there are literally about 10 diabetics (t1&2) and several pre-diabetics) and the auto subscription screwed them but to my surprise she gifted me a bag and a half.

 I don't usually entertain hype stuff. I'm also not an influencer or bougie or anything so I didn't bother. When she gave it to me the taste was AWFUL. However I'm the kind of person that will totally consume something that tastes nasty for health benefits so I gave it a go and even though at first the taste was making me want to throw up, I stuck with it. 

After 1 week of use I noticed a significance difference in my blood glucose readings. Honestly it was the thing that got me down to a hundred. Keeps it under 200 easily. I haven't been able to afford insulin so I've been rolling the dice on that, but this tea has been working almost better than insulin (in my opinion for myself because we are all different) on my readings, taken with my other medications. (Granted for the last 6 months I have been on 2000 mg of metformin a day, 1 Pioglitazone 45 mg [I am still not sure what thisef does but after my last Dr visit he gave it to me and I wasn't asking more questions], 20g of Glipizide a day and an 80mg of Atorvastatin for cholesterol) I know it's a hefty handful of meds but the mushroom tea has still kept me LOW on glucose readings. 

I am okay with no sugar but I do struggle and wrestle with the carbs daily. I feel like my jaw hits the floor with my constant lower readings. Has anyone else tried these things and how do you feel about it? I feel bad because I just don't know how to process having such a low blood sugar reading consistently when a stupid hyped up overpriced mushroom tea has actually done what the ad say they do and kind of eliminates my neuropathy inflammation and blood sugar reads. Anyone else have feedback for me that doesn't make me feel crazy?
label: no stigma

title: Wife just got diagnosed at 23 years old.
post text:
I have a bunch of questions, i hardly know where to start, i just want to help her, but im both confused and scared,  firstly: How unlikely is it that she is diabetic at 23? her bmi is high, and she has been eating a diet high in sugars for a while now, but at her age it still took me by surprise.

Her blood test at the gp showed she was now diabetic at a number of 55, i don't recall the name of the test, but it's the one where they are able  check back 2 two 3 months worth of glucose level in the blood.

Then yesterday we used an at home blood test kit and it said her bloods were an mmol level of 20 after eating, which every website said was super high, i contacted 111 in the UK, and they got back to me hours later, all the while i tested her bloods and they gradually went down to 17, then many hours later to 13. The next day we went to an out of hours doctor referred from the 111 services , her bloods were back up to 16 (without eating since), the doctor did a urine test and ruled out type 1 diabetes, was confused at her having type 2 at her age but said it was likely due to weight gain, and gave us metformin to start with. She's about 280 pounds at 5ft 9. Her ketones were none existent and so we were discharged for now with a gp  appointment due in 5 days for a follow up

Basically, im worried and in need of advice/ support, will she be okay? Could it be something more sinister like cancer?

  
She has no other symptoms related to diabetes/ hyperglycemia  that i can tell.

title: Success story (so far)!
post text:
Diagnosed late July, 12.9 A1c. Had lost almost 40lbs in about 8 months, extreme thirst and urination were the symptoms I had before diagnosis. Went on Metformin and Ozempic. Using a Dexcom G7 to keep track. Also started TRT, low number found during same Dr visit. And slightly high cholesterol too, 209.

About 2 months after diagnosis and beginning of medication, A1c down to 7.3, and cholesterol down to 120 (didn't do any medication for it). Also exercising more and putting some muscle mass back on that had been lost. 

Just got back blood work after 3 months of being medicated and changing lifestyle around for the better: A1c of 5.7!! Feeling good about how things have gone and thanks to all in this group for advice and motivation! 

Recently been treating for a slightly high TSH (thyroid) number. After 6 weeks of medication it didn't get too much better but will be following up with doc soon.

title: Choosing a first insulin pump (Omnipod or T-slim x2)
post text:
Hi!
I am a type 1 diabetic for 1 year. I currently use the MDI method for injections. I need between 1.5 and 3.5 units of rapid insulin (Fiasp) per meal and 9.5 units of slow insulin per day.

I eat foods that are very similar, but that was the case before I discovered my diabetes.

A typical meal looks like:
70 to 120 grams of carbohydrates (usually brown rice)
70 to 150 grams of legumes (I always vary)
100 to 250 grams of meat (most often chicken fillet or ground beef because it is quick to prepare on a daily basis)
Lots of vegetables (preferably seasonal)

I eat 4 meals like this a day.

All the food is fresh and home cooked. I never wanted processed foods in my diet. I rarely eat desserts: it’s purely for pleasure. I always thought that it brought nothing but bad habits, so it remains a rare pleasure. My breakfasts are salty, I never eat sweet things in the morning. It’s not filling, nor is it good for my body.

I have an active lifestyle. I cycle to work, about 20 kilometres a day, rain, or shine. I walk about 25 kilometres a day and often carry loads to work. I do street workout 4 times a week for about 1 hour, which is enough time to do 120 pull-ups, 80 dips and 100 push-ups.

I do not take any dietary supplements and do not intend to use any.

We don't have a motorized vehicle and don't need one, so we do everything with our legs, even shopping.

Of course, I also like to relax. I enjoy my sofa, I like to sit on my IKEA Poang or I like to sit at my desk and play.

I try to get enough sleep, about 8 hours a night. I rarely need more, even without an alarm clock. Sometimes I sleep poorly due to poor blood sugar.

Yesterday was the day of my appointment at the hospital to discuss the installation of insulin pumps.

The choice was reduced to 2 models: T-slim x2 and Omnipod 5. At first, during the learning, it will not be a closed loop. The closed loop will be offered about 6 months later.
Since it seems that the Omnipod has no real disadvantage compared to the T-slim x2, I am turning to this solution: no wire and an active lifestyle seem to me to be the most important advantage.

I still have some questions. I am slim, all the abdominal, back and arm muscles are prominent, muscular and I have very little fat.

I wonder if it will be easy to alternate the installation areas of the Omnipod or if, ultimately, the T-slim x2 would not be the right choice, because it is easier to insert the pipes in different areas.

Do you have any advice or experience to share that will help me decide? Please know that there will be no difference in cost whether I choose one system or the other. So it doesn't weigh in the balance.

Thanks for reading. I will update the post when I have decided. I will probably give feedback after use too.

Edit : I understand there is no best choice between those 2 pumps: both are great.

Wireless remains the main benefit, so I will go with Omnipod. It works well during sport or on slim body, so my fear wasn’t justified.

Thanks to everyone that helped me and give me insight.
All the best to you :)

title: Diabetes medicines interacting with Antibiotics
post text:
Diabetic drug interactions with antibiotics

I am not a doctor, so this is NOT medical advice.  I am just a patient that had to learn a lot about drug interactions in a hurry after a major operation.  this is more of a "heads up" warning.

i have been on various antibiotics for the past 2 1/2 month.  but the last 6 weeks i have been on Linezolid (the antibiotic of last resort).  If you ever are unfortunate enough to get MRSA infection, this is a likely drug they will give you.   Despite its powerfulness, i have not seen a lot of side effects.  but i attribute that to going online and learning all i could about it and changing my behavior.

I am also on Novolog 70/30 mix morning and night, Metformin 5x500mg a day, and Trulicity 3 mg dose once a week.

LINEZOLID and INSULIN

the First interesting interaction was about a week or two in, i was getting a LOT of low blood sugar events happening.  Sometimes 2 a day.  Online research stated that Linezolid can super amplify the effect of Insulin.  Its something to do with the linezolid acting like an MAOI inhibitor.  And that is exactly what was happening, my normal dose of novolog was way too much.  Under the doctor's advice, i lowered it to 70% of the amount i was using previously.  I also changed my diet that was mostly a carnivore/Mediterranean diet, and added in some small amount of simple carbs at every meal.  such as 1/2 an English muffin at breakfast, bread at lunch, potato or rice at dinner--not too much, but enough to give my insulin something to go take a bite out of.

LINEZOLID AND GUT BACTERIA

Linezolid, like many antibiotics, tends to kill your gut bacteria.  If the gut bacteria all die off, then a nasty strain called C. Diff. can take over.  You do not want it.  So in researching, there is a Yeast that is recommended that does not die off from this one antibiotic: saccharomyces boulardii.  And it seems to help with having long term gut problems.

METFORMIN AND LINEZOLID

I also take metformin, and there is a potential interaction between Metformin and Linezolid: Lactic Acidosis.  Apparently is happens suddenly, but takes some time to hit.  So i am watching it carefully.  If i end up tired, and my whole body starts to ache, headaches i am supposed to call the doctor right away.  it is a serious complication IF it happens.  I also bought a Lactate blood meter to check on it over the following weeks.  taking Vitamin C is supposed to help reduce the lactic acid

LINEZOLID AND EATING FOOD WITH TYRAMINE

Another thing to watch with Linezolid is the problem of not being able to handle Tyramine.  Tyramine is present in lots of foods, such as anchovies, smoked meats, tomato, kimchi, sauerkraut, and so on.  The tyramine normally can be handled in your body, but since Linezolid acts like an MAOI inhibitor, your body suddenly can not handle it.  So there is a list of foods your should avoid.  find the "Migraine Headache" diet online to recommend foods low in tyramine.  Too much tyramine and you can end up with VERY high blood pressure.  An at home blood pressure machine is useful here.  Also there is a probiotic called Lactobacilus Rhamnosous that supposedly reduces the tyramine level in your gut from food you ate, might be of use.  the problem i ran into here is, a lot of the banned foods are foods that are my go too foods to not raise blood sugar as snacks.  SO it is an real adjustment for me.  Supposedly you have to stay off these high tyramine foods for 3 weeks after stopping using the antibiotic.

INSULIN AND KAOPECTATE

And if you do get diarrhea, be aware that typical antidiarrheals are bad for use while taking insulin.  Taking kaopectate can result in a sudden drop in blood sugar.  Also, Imodium is not supposed to be used with the Linezolid.

Other antibiotics will have similar side effects, so research online carefully.
label: no stigma

title: Looking for some general insights - Metformin question
post text:
I was diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes a year ago with an A1C of 6.5%. I lost some weight but didn’t make significant changes otherwise. At my recent checkup, my A1C had increased to 7.0%. My doctor prescribed a Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) and Metformin 1000mg.

I started using the CGM immediately, but there was a delay of about two weeks in getting the Metformin. During those two weeks, I made radical changes to my diet. The results were promising—I could see improvements in my CGM data, which, while reading slightly low, was stable. By the end of those two weeks, my estimated GMI had dropped to 5.8%.

When I finally started Metformin, it caused me significant side effects, including nausea and "very intense dreams." I’m sensitive to certain medications that trigger vivid dreams, and this seemed to be one of them. After four days on Metformin, my GMI average rose to 6.3%. While it did seem to smooth out my glucose levels, my readings never dropped below 110-120.

Frustrated, I stopped taking Metformin, and within a few days, my average went back down to 6.0%. I suspect the elevated levels could have been related to stress or cortisol caused by the intense dreams and nausea.

I also deal with the dawn phenomenon, where my glucose stays around 120 throughout the morning. I chalk this up to a long-standing routine of only sleeping 4-6 hours per night and skipping breakfast for the past 30 years—my body seems “used to it.”

At this point, I’m uncertain what to do about the Metformin. I haven’t brought this up with my doctor yet, though I’ve previously tried to explain my sensitivity to certain medications. Unfortunately, my concerns didn’t seem to be taken seriously. Now that my levels are relatively stable after 1.5 months on the CGM (average ~6.0%), I’m considering giving Metformin another try—but I don’t want to take something that causes nausea and intense dreams.

Any insights or advice would be greatly appreciated.

**edit with AI for clarity :(

title: Newbie Diabetic Trying to Figure Out This New Life
post text:
Hi All! Have been lurking in here for a few months and figured I would do a post. After not seeing a doctor and getting a physical for 5 years, I went in August and my A1C was 8.9. I was immediately put on Jardiance. Then I lost my job (and my good insurance) and am now on unemployment (a lousy $314/week) and Medicaid, so I had to find a new doctor. He  added Metformin and a blood pressure medication to my daily meds. When we did blood labs, my A1C was down to 6.6, so I am headed in the right direction. I do not take insulin, have just been trying to eat better and do an hour on my exercise bike 5 days a week for a hour per session. I am going to be buying an adjustable weight set soon to work on muscle building because I lost about 30 pounds in 3 months from my diet (mostly as much cutting carbs and sugar as much as possible) and exercise.

I have been able to keep my blood sugar below 180 2 hours after eating, but keep reading that 140 should be the goal to get the A1C down quicker. One question - is it okay if it goes above 180 for a short amount of time and then comes back down within 2 hours?

My new doctor hasn't been the most helpful with telling me what my targets should be, so I think I am going to see a dietition as I have been considering a plant-based diet (not fun). Eating healthy is expensive and it's hard to find time to make dinner from scratch everyday. I also don't really eat breakfast or snack much, so it's been about 2 meals per day with maybe an apple, carrot sticks, or handful of nuts in between.

TLDR - am I doing this right? Or is my BC going to get more difficult to keep under control as time goes on?  I feel lost....Any advice would be greatly appreciated as I am trying to figure out this new life. Thanks!

title: Thoughts on if I should contact ADA?
post text:
Okay so this mights be a long one
I’m a case manager and I had to take my client to the district court this morning. We get there I bring my water bottle that has liquid IV and a water soluble medication that I take, and my glucose tabs that I’m actively eating because my blood sugar is low. The security guard tells me to go outside if I’m eating. I explain that they were for my medical condition and I was finished anyway, then they told me to take my water bottle out of the building. Again I explain I have a medical conditions and my medication is in my water and it needed to be finished. The security guard asked me “what medications are you on?” I told them it’s illegal for him to ask me this. The security guard said “I don’t care” and told me to just leave my water bottle with them, I told them I didn’t feel comfortable doing that. So they told me to leave. I just gave in and left my water. NEXT they tell me ( also it’s like 5 men ganging up on me all using their intimidating man voices) that I need to go through the metal detector. I tell them that I cannot go through metal detectors per my doctor. They told me “whatever you can go through” So then they ask me “what medical conditions do you have” I told them that’s also illegal for them to ask. I was also with my client who I don’t want to disclose all this information in front of. Anyway, the guard said “go through the metal detector or leave.” So I give in and go through the metal detector, once I walk through the other guard say “you beeped you have to go back through” I’m about to LOSE it at the point and so if my client. So I tell them it’s my medical device and show them like okay I get that BUT then they ask me to turn it on for them to prove it was an insulin pump!  Like what else would it be?? It baffled me. As me and my client were going to the court room, we can hear the security making fun of me then yelled at me and said “the door literally says no food or drink!” I have never been treated so poorly regarding my medical conditions, those men were on an absolute power trip, purposely trying to intimidate me by raising their voices at me when they are 1-2 feet away from me. I have been in many courts and federal building that say no food or drink and I’ve had no problem with bringing in food and drink because of my medical conditions. I was told to contact ADA and report them but is that blowing it out of proportion? I’m just worried that reporting to the ADA is too much. Let me know your thoughts!!

title: Only sort of 'hardwork', A1c down
post text:
I'm 35F. was diagnosed in late last August with an HbA1c of 7.6. Diabetes runs in my dad's side of the family for generations and a tendency for fatty liver on my mother's side. So I was screwed. I weighed 72 kgs at the time. 

Doc prescribed 500mg metformin twice a day, along with diet and exercise.

Honestly, the metformin has been amazing. Cuts out food noise, cuts out cravings, makes eating less and exercising more that much easier. 

My dietary guidelines recommended eating frequently (small snacks of fruits/nuts between meals). I had dinner early(by Indian standards) and controlled my portion sizes but didn't do anything hugely restrictive other than cut out food made from white flour and avoided chips/fried snacks(my weakness).

I thought I was doing really well, the weight was falling off, the exercise was fun. I aimed for 6 days a week, but as long as I managed 3 days, I wasn't too hard on myself. At 6 weeks after diagnosis A1c was 6.5. Doc was happy, wanted to after more weight loss. Told me to keep going as I was.

That was till I found this sub in December. Nothing has made my more anxious than the posts here. All the posts about restrictive diets, the measuring of food, the carb counting and CGMs (Doc never asked to keep ongoing checks on anything other than blood pressure). I really spiralled, thinking that controlling diabetes had to 'hardwork' and 'miserable' and to be done without meds (The more science focused posts were very helpful though).

Got my blood tests done yesterday. A1c 5.7. Fasting was 85, post prandial 79. Weight down by 12 kg. And I had fun doing it. Explored more recipes, ran a 5K along the way. Ate some ramen, some potatoes, smaller servings of rice fairly frequently. I absolutely have not been 'super strict' on myself, but what I am doing now is sustainable for me.

It's possibly age, possibly the weight, possibly the metformin. (Maybe it could have happened faster?)

Before the diagnosis, I felt the worst I had ever felt in years, lethargic and depressive, falling back into bad eating habits. After has been much better from a mental health perspective. Is this a silver lining?

I have not been as consistent as I should be about improving my diet, but I eat broccoli, cauliflower, or carrots almost every day, mix ground flaxseed into my yogurt, and drink tons of water.

I also recently started fish oil supplements and milk thistle for my liver.

Senna laxatives and Ex-Lax barely do anything. Next up is milk of magnesia.

I am of course going to follow up with my doctor, but has anybody experienced this with any of the above meds? It's crazy-making. I have gained weight also.

title: Uh-oh.  A1C up to 6.1
post text:
I know I’m still pre-diabetic but I’m worried.

I can’t find my trend graph on my patient portal, but I’m pretty sure my last A1C in September was 5.7.  It’s been around 5.4 - 5.9 most of the time, highest was 6.2 in 2022 (also my highest weight - I have lost about 20-25 pounds since then).

The thing that doesn’t make sense is that I have been seeing an eating disorders therapist and dietitian weekly for the past few months.  I’m 30 days binge-free.  I still do eat things I shouldn’t, but not in massive quantities anymore (usually).

I have been tracking my food just in text on an eating disorders app.  But just today I downloaded MyFitnessPal again so I can pay more attention to serving sizes and carbs.  Hopefully without triggering me to binge or restrict.

EDIT: I am on 500mg of Metformin ER twice a day (so 1,000 total) and also 5mg of Farxiga.  

I was previously on 2,000mg total of Metformin ER but had severe diarrhea so they took me off it completely, and added Farxiga (also for protection of my kidneys because of mental health meds).   Ended up adding back Metformin at a smaller dose.

title: Question for everyone about cost of your meds.
post text:
Hi, I just saw a post about some people either not getting covered for ozempic, or paying $700+ for it. 

I’m flabbergasted!

I just got my Rx for ozempic filled today, and it was $0, prior to this my insulin, metformin, needles testing kit all completely covered. 

I know my companies insurance is said to be good, but I can’t imagine what I would do if I was paying even $50 for any of these. 

What are you paying? what insurance do you have? Have any meds been denied?

Like I said previously, I’ve paid nothing for any of my meds, I have BCBS and even though my numbers were good, my doctor insisted I try ozempic, to try and eliminate my other meds and help weightloss.

Edit: Thank you to everyone who has shared. 

I have had my insurance for over a decade and prior to that had tricare through the military. So I’ve not paid for almost any medical need for almost 20 years. 

It’s really surprising how much some of you pay for your meds, and I am truly sorry that it is that much. I doubt our healthcare system will ever be fixed, but god for all our sakes I hope it is.
label: yes stigma

title: a1c from july-jan
post text: started ozempic in august. was suuuuper strict the first few months. abt 30-50gcarbs a day. after a1c went down to 6.9 i allowed myself around 100g a day. christmas and thanksgiving i ate in moderation but still allowed myself to live. yesterday i went to the dr and im at 5.9!!!!! almost out of pre diabetic range!! not to mention im 60 pounds down. i know that it’s not cured or even in remission (until im off medication ofc). the biggest victory is i was able to eat chickpea pasta tonight with no spike!!  i have been so desperate for pasta and missing it SO BAD. im at 120 an hour after a serving with low carb pasta sauce, cottage cheese, mozzarella cheese and ground beef. i didn’t believe ppl abt the banza chickpea pasta but, this has been the best hack lol. for anyone looking for a good dessert, the kind ice cream bars are the best. 16g of carbs and 7g of fiber. they taste like a snickers and don’t ever spike me :)

title: Doctor will not prescribe Ozempic even with high readings
post text:
Hello! I  (F 51) was diagnosed with T2 5 years ago with a 7.0 AIC.  Did not take things seriously and in 2022, I hit a 9.4 AIC.  I tried Metformin later in 2022 and I had the usual stomach issues and quit taking it 7 months later.  Without meds, I am down to 6.4, mostly from diet.  I have high readings throughout the day. Some examples:   I had 4 oz of broiled chicken breast with half a cup of brown rice and an hour later I was 245.  Several times a week, my morning fasting numbers are between 160 and 180 and never under 140. (Note, I don't usually do brown rice or anything super carby for that matter)

  
My doctor said that because it seems I'm doing "wonderful" on my own, he doesn't think I need any medication. I should also add that my BMI is 43, down from 46.  I feel like even though my AIC may seem "normal", my numbers throughout the day are not. 

Are these numbers damaging my body? Is there anything I can do to convince my doctor that I need help? Do I need a new doctor?

title: Blood Sugar On Work Days vs Weekends
post text:
Hello All!
 
 Out of curiosity, has anyone noticed that their blood sugar runs higher on days when they are at work vs when they’re off work?
 
 Every day, wake up at the same time. Fasting blood sugar is right around 98-110. Eat the same exact thing for breakfast everyday and take the same exact insulin dosages (40u Long, 35u Fast) for 20g of carbs. On a day off, my numbers go up to around 105 and then stay pretty level around 98-100. On a work day, two hours later, my sugar shoots up to 150-160. I have to take an additional 15u to get it to come back down to 98-100 range. 
 
 Does anyone else notice this? How do you manage this? My in-person days are worse - I’ve had it jump up to 180-190 if I don’t have time to take another fast acting dose before walking in the door at work two hours-post injection. 
 
 Thanks!

title: Neuropathy at a young age?
post text:
Hi, I (19F) have suffered from chronic UTIs my entire life. It was even how I was diagnosed with T1D at age 6.

Over the past couple of months after a UTI I noticed that the urge to pee never went away, even though the bacteria had been confirmed to be gone and no culture/white blood cells were present upon frequent retests.

I have done multiple other tests, like a renal ultrasound, vaginal swabs for issues like thrush, bacterial vaginosis, etc. I have been screened for STIs despite having one commited partner my entire life.

The irritation/pain is worse at night (seriously, I've been sleeping 5 hours max and waking up every hour, or 2 hours max on a bad night. I fear I am actually dying of sleep deprivation).

It looks like the only remaining cause is neuropathy. I had a few years of bad control, apparently due to fluctuating hormones and an insulin resistance that I didn't know I had. My HBa1C was at it's highest maybe 8.5% for a few quarterly checkups.

I am wondering if nerve damage is the cause. I feel like it's the only thing left that it could be. If it is, I have no idea what to do about it.

Any advice is much appreciated
label: no stigma

title: Just diagnosed, I feel like a hot pile of crap, can't seem to calm down. Glucose fasting: 7.5, A1C: 6.5.
post text:
TLDR: I got diagnosed with diabetes and I was given metformin and crestor. I don't want to take it and try to lower it in 6 months (lifestyle +exercise)but my doctor wants me to start taking the medication right away what do I do?

Hi everyone. First post here. Maybe a rant. Maybe a rabbit hole I don't know. I'm a 34/F, 200lbs/165 cm/5'5 south Asian decent with family history of diabetes on my dad's side. Glucose fasting: 7.5, A1C: 6.5.

I had gestational diabetes with my two pregnancies and I knew I was at risk to become diabetic if I didn't care but I didn't know that it would happen so fast. I am honestly so shook right now and so scared because my dad did pass away horribly with complications due to unmanage diabetes and I do not want that kind of future for my kids and my family. Long story short my glucose fasting came up as high as a 7.3 mmol/l and my hemoglobin a1c came up as high which is a 6.5% and apparently that is diagnosis for diabetes mellitus in canada.

 My doctor prescribed me metformin and crestor and I had asked her for time for 6 months just so that I can not reverse or put my diabetes in your mission but get my diet and exercise on track so that I can I don't know I no longer have to be on medication but she does not want to give me that option.

I want to not take the medication and start the diet exercise / weight loss thing and go out of that way but again I don't know if that's a good idea. So please screws of Reddit please let me know if I should start the medication and then stop at 6 months later or if I should take the medication while I'm still doing all of my lifestyle changes? 

What I'm scared of is that if I start the medication and I start to lose weight, I will regain it back. Previously I had to take metformin from my gestational diabetes and I did end up losing a significant amount of weight and my fear is that if I stop the medication 6 months down the road I will regain that way back and I my cholesterol will all come back so I don't know what to do.

title: Introduction
post text:
Hello T2D friends!

I just got diagnosed with type 2 Diabetes. It was a long time coming (risk factors including: obesity, family history on both sides, gestational diabetes and prediabetes).

I ignored my health for a really long time. I have mental health issues that get in the way sometimes. I’m trying to pay more attention to my health now as I am transitioning to a slower pace. 

I’ve been informed about diabetes for a long time, as my mom is a medical professional living with type 2 diabetes. I’ve been on metformin at different times in my journey, as well as fingerpicks with a glucometer. I have a fear of needles 

Anyway, I’m here to find some like-minded people that are going through something similar. I’m currently on round 2 (just a few hours ago) of Ozempic. I have a CGM (Libre 3) and my sugars don’t look too awful currently. I only have 7 days of data so far.

I’m on other medication that suppresses my appetite so my caloric intake has been really low (600-1k per day). Another large contributing factor is that I have severe dental issues and can’t eat normally. I’m very close to needing to switch to a liquid diet which I’d rather avoid if possible. I’m figuring out the dental thing as I go.

Wondering if anyone is in a similar boat, or has been? Sometimes the complications make it so much harder to manage…

Thankful this place exists and good luck to us all

title: Weird sudden drop in blood sugar.
post text:
Checked my BS at 9:13pm - 2 hours after dinner. It was 149, which is higher than I like. So I hit the rowing machine for 15 minutes and did some stretching. Felt a little dizzy and checked my BS again at 9:44pm. It had dropped to 58. Thought it was an error so I checked a different finger and again 57. Went to the kitchen to get some sugar in me and almost passed out. I was really dizzy and couldn’t keep my eyes open. Luckily my wife was nearby so she got me some juice quickly. 

I was diagnosed with type 2 in September. I’m not on any diabetes medication (I take hypertension med and statin). I’ve NEVER seen my BS below 85. I use the rowing machine almost daily and never experienced something like this before. 

This was a 90 point drop in 30 minutes while on no medication. I didn’t even know this was possible. What the heck happened to me tonight?

title: How to carb count: a little example for those who need it
post text:
Yesterday my mom made a cake, I love it it's called "migliaccio", to enjoy every slice without carb guessing I carb counted to the milligram, so here's a little "guide" for those who may need it. Carb counting isn't lame or psycho.

First we have the ingredients:
3 eggs (150 gr)
200gr ricotta
200 sugar
200 flour
½lt milk

1 pan

You sum up all the ingredients to come up with the weight of the mix. So = 1250gr to which I added the weight of the pan (700gr). = 1950

Now I carb count using the back of the packaging of each ingredient that has carbs. I'll show all the passages.

I know that I don't need to do math for sugar, that will be 200 carbs.
But the flour I used has 72.5 carbs for 100 grams SO what you want to do is: 72.5% (which is litterally doable on a calculator) that's 0.725 that I'll multiply for the amount I used= 0.725x200= 145.
I'll do the same for the milk: 4.9%=0.49x500= 24.5
Add it all up: 369.5 carbs total

Now what I do is weight it all cooked, I put the whole pan on the scale: 1128-700 (the pan)=427

Now you take the 369.5 carbs and divide them for the 427 (cause you gotta see how these carbs are divided in the new weight).
369.5÷428= 0.86

So now I know that if I want a slice of 70 grams I'll do 0.86x70= 60 carbs!

I'll dose for that and maybe add a bit later for fats!

I hope this was clear and useful

title: Hypo after a hefty meal?
post text:
So my GP (doctor) asked me to cut Metformin from 2x2 (2000mg/day) to 1x2 (1000mg/day) following my A1C of 4.9% (30). Well, 3 days in, and I just had pasta with ragu and meatballs + 1 tablet. My blood sugar 1 hour in was the lowest I've ever seen - 3.6mmol/l (65mg/dl), and 2 hours in 4mmol/l (72mg/dl). 3 hours in it's at 4.2mmol/l (75mg/dl).

What the hell? The pasta was wholewheat if that makes any difference, but having previously had it, my sugar would spike up to 6mmol/l (106mg/dl) and today for some mysterious reason it seems to have... dropped instead of going up. "Common sense" dictates that reducing metformin dosage should increase my blood glucose, but it seems to be the other way round. I am baffled and concerned.
label: no stigma

title: Anyone here transition back to MDI from their pump?
post text:
I'm considering going back to MDI after my current batch of supplies runs out. I'm just flat out tired of trying to keep up with my X2, finding good sites and the extra annoyances it provides with alerts. I would probably be just as successful on MDI as I have been on the pump. But lately, my body has been a POS with insulin resistance.

Up until last week, I had great numbers. My 3 day average was less than 120 and I could get away with eating something sweet. In fact, I was eating a lot of sweets due to frequent crashes. But starting last week, I just cannot get my numbers where they need to be. I get these long, drawn out spikes that last for hours. Doesn't matter if I crank up the basal rate, the number don't budge. This, of course, causes me to fingerstick more often to make sure my CGM isn't off.

title: New member, long-time with T2
post text:
Hello all! I am getting back on Reddit to have access to advice and tips. I’ve been T2 since around 2015-2016. I have unfortunately never taken it as seriously as I should. I relied on meds (Trulicity until 2023, the Ozempic) to regulate my A1C while I ate like total shit. So now, at age 48, my A1C is 11 and I just was in hospital for a few days with DKA. (I went from 1 mg Ozempic to 2 mg, and when that made me sick, I didn’t eat or hydrate enough.)

So, I’m committed to FINALLY making the diet and exercise changes I should’ve done years ago. My doctor took me off of Oz for now. I’ll be on Metformin (500 mg ER twice daily) and Jardiance (25 mg) until March when we revisit and see how it’s going. I think I’ll get a CGM because I don’t always remember to poke my fingers when I should (and I hate doing it). 

I’m so hopeful that once the Oz is out of my system - and the Metformin is doing its “dirty work” - my gastric emptying will go back to normal and I can eat more fiber. Currently, it’s about a 2-day delay for roughage to exit the building. Once I can eat more, I’m so excited to eat salads all the time!

Thanks for reading, and thanks in advance for listening to my updates and questions. I’m so happy to have a community that understands the special balances that T2 requires!
label: no stigma

title: Struggling with my diabetes
post text:
Hi everyone 

I have had a diagnosis of Type 2 diabetes since 2019.

I managed quite well at first with only a bit of medication.

Although I have lost weight & cut out full fat drinks etc, I have not been doing so well over the last year. My Hba1c has been steadily climbing and is sitting at 76. This is despite taking Empoglifozin & Glicazide. I have been put into Semeglutide tablets, however that is causing a lot of nausea and bloating despite been on metoclopromide for it. At my last hospital appt, I was told the next step would be insulin. I know I am not doing enough to help myself. I struggle with mental health issues including depression which causes self neglect. I am also in recovery from substance abuse & have had a lot of things going on in my personal life (I lost my mum to cancer last year- she was 60). My dad died at 47 in 2007.

I am worried about my health but I am a bit stuck at  the moment.

I’m not sure why I’ve wrote all this down, I guess it helps to get off my chest. If you have read this far: many thanks. 

I’m not sure what I’m looking for by posting. I know what needs to be done but how can you get diabetes under control when you don’t care much for yourself in general and your mental health is so rubbish (despite having counselling and help in the past) that you feel you deserve to suffer some days?
label: yes stigma

title: Wanted to share a recipe that scratches my nutella itch
post text:
I love Nutella. I love everything hazelnut-chocolate flavored (Ferrero Rochers, hazelnut praline, etc.). 

But ever since adopting a low-carb lifestyle for my T2, I’ve missed Nutella so much. I needed a low-carb replacement STAT!

I came across this recipe for cocoa-dusted hazelnuts  It really scratches my Nutella itch without raising my blood glucose (and I use a CGM). 

I make half-batches at a time using hazelnuts from Trader Joes (I bake for only 15 minutes since the hazelnuts are already roasted). The recipe does include a small amount of honey to mask the odd taste of Stevia, but it doesn’t impact my glycemic response. 

I hope other Nutella lovers find this recipe helpful!

title: Fasting is "pre", but A1c is 6.5
post text:
I have received some really fantastic responses about my freak-out at having been diagnosed yesterday -- lots of realism ("get in there and deal with it -- take it seriously!") and lots of comforting ("you got this! It's not too bad, and you can take this as an opportunity to get healthier than ever!"). With a shoulder to cry on last night, I have now moved past the "freak-out" and feel GREAT about the future! Thanks to everyone for the support; I am ready to go.

In looking over my labs, however, I am wondering what all of you experts see. I mean, obviously, I'm diabetic & must take action NOW (and VERY lucky to have been dx'd at 6.5), but perhaps there's some insight to be found in these numbers that I'm not aware of.

2016: FBG 101; FIRST A1c ever measured 5.7 \[no red flags or mention from doc, and I didn't know any better, so I thought, "all good"\]

2017: FBG 101; (no A1c) \[again, no flag or mention from doc, and I still didn't know any better\]

2018: FBG 99; A1c 5.9 \[this is when I said oh crap (got educated), even though docs still hadn't said anything. I joined a "prevent T2" class and lost 45 pounds\]

2019: FBG 97; A1c 5.7

2020: FBG 103; A1c 5.5

2021: FBG 100; (no A1c) \[stress & complacency took over; I became a potato with a bag of cookies & began gaining 50 pounds\]

2022: FBG 130; A1c 6.0 \[FBG clearly diabetic, A1c "pre" diabetic\]

2023: no labs, but in-office A1c 5.9

2024: (2 days ago) FBG 124; A1c 6.5 \[FBG not \*QUITE\* diabetic; A1c at cut-off\]

title: Anyone else have trouble with treats?
post text:
Newbie here, 61F, T2, no meds. For about 5-6 months post dx I was really strict about no dessert or bread type treats. One big benefit of this was that all food cravings and food noise disappeared after about 2 weeks of discomfort. The 2 weeks flew by since I was scared and researching like a madwoman for months.

A1C 6.5 at dx, 6.2 at 3 months. I am a bit overdue for my 6 month check. Thanksgiving was wonderful, but unfortunately was a super spreader event for influenza A. I dealt with that for over 3 weeks, then had a 10 day personal lockdown because my husband got Covid. Loads of fun!

Anyway, around Thanksgiving I started to do occasional small treats, looking towards sustaining diet changes over the long haul. I was still averaging around 75g carbs per day, and continued with intermittent fasting. My BS spiked a bit more than normal, but mostly to 150-160ish and never above 180, still 100% in range on my CGM. But wow the cravings and food noise snuck back in!

I guess my body isn't ready for any treats yet. Has anyone else here had similar experiences?

Oh btw, thanks to everyone here! When I googled hundreds of questions post dx, I found so much great information here, and tons of kindness.

title: I'm glad I'm diabetic and low T
post text:
Anybody else glad to be diagnosed? I know, that sounds weird. Lol. But, I have felt like garbage for years. I have no energy, struggle to make it through the day, come home from work and just crawl in the bed. I thought it was just because I was getting old (I turn 50 in May) or a part of my depression.

I was so relieved when I got the diagnosis (7.5 A1C, 246 testosterone) that it felt like a burden had been lifted. I can now point to something tangible and say "This is why I feel like crap!" and I know it can be treated. I don't have to live the rest of my life exhausted, and I'm excited to live a normal life and be active again! 

I know I'll miss pizza and pasta, rice and fresh baked bread, but I'm confident that this diagnosis will be the impetus I've needed to really watch my diet, get some excercise, and get healthy. I've been about 75lbs overweight for a decade and I'm ready to get back down to a healthy weight, be more active, look better, feel better, and be better. 

I'll be starting Farxiga and TRT injections in a few days and I'm hoping it won't take long to feel some improvement. I've been reading through a lot of old posts here and they've been very helpful. I'll post some updates in a month or two just in case it might help some other newly diagnosed people in the future. Thanks, y'all. Glad we're all here to support each other.

title: R/diabetes_t2
post text:
Diagnosed with Diabetes at 34: Feeling Overwhelmed and Seeking Hope

Hi everyone,

Two weeks ago, my life changed. It all started when I began experiencing dizziness after meals. Out of curiosity and concern, I borrowed my stepdad’s glucometer and tested my blood sugar—it read 6.8 mmol/L (122 mg/dL). Panic set in, and I quickly scheduled an appointment with my family doctor.

She ordered comprehensive blood tests. Just 10 months ago, my fasting blood sugar was 5.4 mmol/L (97 mg/dL), so I was optimistic this would be just a scare. But when the results came back three days later, I was shocked. My fasting glucose had risen to 6.2 mmol/L (112 mg/dL), and my HbA1c was 5.7%. However, the most devastating news came from the OGTT (oral glucose tolerance test). After drinking the 75g glucose solution, my blood sugar spiked to 14 mmol/L (252 mg/dL) and took 5 hours to return to 6.3 mmol/L (113 mg/dL).

My doctor confirmed what I dreaded: at just 34 years old, I have diabetes. What makes this even harder is that I’m about to welcome my first child into the world.

Reading about the potential complications terrifies me. I want to be healthy and present for my family for as long as I can. I’ve started following Professor Taylor’s method for weight loss and lifestyle changes, hoping to make a difference.

But I’m scared, and I need advice. Has anyone here lived many years with diabetes without developing complications? If so, what helped you maintain your health?

Any tips, encouragement, or success stories would mean the world to me right now.

Thank you so much for reading.
label: no stigma

title: Progress, but also other things to be concerned about
post text:
Hey DT2 community!

I posted about my diabetes diagnosis 3 months ago. I started with extremely high numbers (a1c of 12.6) and I'm happy to report that after the first three months that number has gone down to 7.7! It's definitely been a struggle, but I'm trying my best and working hard (also taking my meds) and I'll keep on working to get the numbers down.

With all that being said, I'm also about concerned about kidney disease. For context, my first efgr was of 41 and my Urine Albumin-Creatinine Ratio was of 3.5. After three months efgr is 49 and Urine Albumin-Creatinine Ratio is down to 1.1.  
I'm obviously concerned, but my Dr. says that we need to wait another 3 months before taking any decisions as we need more results. I haven't had a lot of symptoms (thankfully), except for getting cold very easily and even then, that started happening when I started getting my diabetes under control.

title: Managing used glucose meter so supplies
post text:
I'm about a month into my Type 2 journey (A1C was 11%). One of the things I wanted to figure out was the best, most hygienic, way to check my sugar in public (reasonably). Here's what I came up with, and I'm interested any comments y'all might have. 

So far, I've only been in places with paper napkins. I stack and fold (don't want to waste) the napkins until they're thick enough that the blood won't soak through, usually about 8 layers thick.  Then, while the meter is testing the sample, I dab my finger onto the napkin. After the reading, the strip goes onto the napkin. I fold an edge of the napkin to be thicker than the needle, then eject the lancet into that edge. I roll the supplies up. Then, all the blood is separated nice and safe.
label: no stigma

title: For those diagnosed as an adult, what specific things did doctors look for to differentiate T1 and T2?

Hi all, so some background: I am diagnosed with MCTD with secondary Sjogrens, which are both systemic  connective tissue autoimmune diseases. I have a high family history of autoimmune diseases (RA, PsA, Graves, Hashimotos, Celiac, and probably some others I’m forgetting). I also have a high family history of T2 diabetes. My dad (who has type 2) thinks I might be insulin resistant and potentially pre-diabetic. I have always been a healthy weight/body fat, don’t eat much sugar (have to track it for my MCTD because sugar can cause a flair), don’t eat too many carbs (not tracked) because I’m lactose and gluten intolerant, eat a high protein diet, and am in my mid-20s. 

I feel like since I have both the family and personal history of autoimmune diseases, as well as a family history of T2 diabetes, I feel like if I was diabetic (or becoming diabetic), it could go either way between T1 and T2, so I’m curious how they were able to differentiate between T1 and T2 for you and what the diagnosis process looked like for you?

title: New T2. Would a Freestyle CGM help my mental model of what/when to eat?
post text:
Relatively new T2 here trying to eat for the long term here. I got down from A1c above 11 to 6.1, but have slipped a couple tenths of points last 2 visits. 
 
 I really like the idea of "eat to your meter." Would getting a Freestyle CGM monitor be helpful for me to build a mental model of what food does to my blood sugar levels? 
 
 I'm not on insulin, just Metformin. BCBS Insurance won't pay for a CGM like a freestyle, but I think I could get my doc to prescribe one. 
 
  
 I need more immediate feedback on my blood sugar levels, and how they are affected by what/when I eat and exercise. Finger pricks are fine, but I need more granular info. How bad is this banana to me? If I eat vegetables and protein before starch, does that help? Does drinking water lower my blood sugar? How does exercise affect? title: How can I achieve diabetes remission/improve heart failure?
post text:
60 y/o male. 

  
June 2024 - was 156 KG and was hospitalised due to some form of fungal infection on my leg that was linked to my diabetes. Was very sick. Prior to hospitalisation, I had not taken the metformin I was prescribed in around 2019. 

  
Whilst in hospital, I was put on metformin and mounjaro. I have been taking both strictly since as well as been on a very controlled diet cutting carbs (still have carb-y vegetables except potatoes; and have a wrap bread about once every two weeks).

  
I am now on 132 KG and just had an A1C test which showed 39 mmol/mol \[5.7%\] which my app says is in the normal range.



Past A1C readings -  94 mmol/mol in June 2024, and between 90-50 mmol/mol from 2019- 2023. 

  
I have separately found via a test done last month that out that I have an heart ejection fraction of 45% (rises to 65% during exercise). My EF was 45% in 2021 - so this hasn't improved.



My question is - am I on the way to diabetes remission (bearing in mind I achieved these results through mounjaro and metformin)? Why hasn't my EF improved and how could I make it improve? Any other tips?

  
RE exercise - I walk at least 4,000 steps a day and need to improve in this area

  
Many thanks for your time

title: Is low blood sugar a problem for t2?
post text:
Back in October there was an incident where I stood up, walked to a storage closet and out of nowhere I felt very light headed and I had to hurry into a public area to sit down because I felt I was going to faint. It was scary especially because not many people have access or reason to go to that closet and if I fainted there, it would be a while before anyone would notice and get me medical attention.

It happened today again but the episode was shorter and less intense. My blood glucose was 70. I've never been this low before. Being on the spectrum, I forget things easily so I've been missing one of my 2 doses of metformin about 3-4 times a week, and doing so has made me feel better.

I made a lot of healthy dietary changes so I don't consume as much sugar or starchy foods anymore. So far, all signs seem to point to low blood sugar being the issue.

I don't have insurance at the moment and I know I will need to see a doctor eventually. I just want to know if this is a low blood sugar problem that I accidently solved by lowering my metformin intake. My prescription is 500mg twice a day, been taking 1 most days and my sugar has been between 100-120. Just looking for advice from someone more experienced with t2. I was diagnosed less than 6 months ago.

Edit: That's 70  after dinner

Scrambled Eggs with Avocado

title: Metformin constipation & dose questions
post text:
Due to my A1C my weight loss dr has prescribed metformin 1700mg. I've made appropriate changes to my diet. Taking it along with topamax which was prescribed by my neurologist for my migraines I have seen benefits with a decrease in appetite, which is helpful to maintain a calorie deficit. So I'm happy with all of those things. 

When I initially stated at 1000mg I had no issues. The bump up to 1700 has caused me some serious constipation, gas,  and bloating daily. As an IBS-C sufferer I hearing most people experience the opposite on metformin was music to my ears lol. I was shocked to be experiencing constipation like I am. 

Just wondering is this anyone's else experience? Are there OTC methods you use to get through them that are ok for daily use? I take 850 2x a day morning and night. Is there a way I can take my doses to ease the side effects. I obviously will consult with the pharmacist or Dr before officially making any changes. Just a curious question if I can get through this as I'm benefiting from the med just struggling a little with side effects.

title: Let’s have a serious talk
post text:
Did y’all know (most probably already do.) that taking a 30 minute walk (3-6 minutes long songs!) can lower your blood sugar? Dancing, exercise, even fidgeting like a lot of leg movements and body movement. Anything that gets you moving can lower your blood sugar.

I say this because I just saw someone with type 1 diabetes and myself, a type two diabetic, have had ranges between 400+. And that’s a shame on our parts considering that food, lack of exercise, stress, and many other issues contribute to a high blood sugar. So after or before you eat, take a walk. Move around a little. Be ACTIVE! Cause it can really save our lives

title: Eversense 365 inserted 11/21

Eversense 365 inserted 11/17

Eversense 365 inserted 11/21

So I’ve continued on my transmitter attachment journey.

I tried arm bands, an arm strap from Etsy, film ordered from Amazon and finally using Flonase before attaching the transmitter.  This was the only solution that worked for me since I’m allergic to most medical adhesives.

Now it’s attached with only the Eversense patch.  I apply the Flonase to my arm, let it dry and attach the transmitter.

Still very accurate, I did not use my Libre 2 for dosing decisions but I have been using the Eversense for dosing and it’s worked well.

title: What is going on?
post text:
Hello all, I’m a male, early 30’s, 6ft weighing 164 pounds with diabetes LADA(1.5). My A1C in August was 6.5. Since then, my diet has not changed, I eat a low carb diet. My family thinks my diet is too extreme but it’s worked for me. Since August I haven’t worked out much except in the month of November before the holidays when I was running every morning. Got lazy around holiday time but my diets stayed the same (I dont cheat on holidays, I prepared my own low carb meals). I also haven’t been pricking my self to check my fasting glucose or 2 hours after I eat. It’s just too anxiety inducing for me (I believe I suffer from stress and anxiety, undiagnosed but I’ve always been a wreck so self diagnosed lol).
 
 Last night however I decided to check my blood sugars before going to bed, the night before my 6 month check up. 4-5 hours after eating I was at nearly 300. I was shocked. I mentioned this to my doc this morning, he said it was concerning and said follow up test which I did this AM will tell us how to move forward. I’m still in shock. Last May my C Peptide was at 1.6. Could it had crashed significantly that quickly and I need insulin now? Could my lack of exercise these past 6 months, my stress, anxiety and lack of good consistent sleep I should add, really mess me up that bad if my diet is still good and I’m taking my meds? 
 
 I should also add I currently have an abscess, not too big though. Could an infection spike me that much even I don’t feel sick or have pain? 
 
 I’m honestly still in shock so any insight, stories you guys could share I’d really appreciate it. BTW 2 hours afters after eating breakfast this morning I was at 300. Shocked and disheartened.

title: Breaking Free from Food: A Mindset Shift for Diabetics
post text:
I’ve been thinking a lot lately about what it really takes to manage diabetes successfully, and I’ve come to a realization:

The hardest part isn’t avoiding carbs—it’s changing your relationship with food itself.

For most of our lives, food has been about pleasure, routine, habit, and social connection. We eat because it’s mealtime, because we’re bored, because food is enjoyable. But as diabetics, we don’t have the luxury of mindless eating.

We’re constantly told:
 “You can’t eat that.”
 “You have to give up the foods you love.”
 “You’ll always be tempted.”

At first, it feels like deprivation. It feels unfair—like everyone else gets to enjoy food freely, while we have to struggle every day just to stay healthy.

But what if the real answer isn’t about finding replacements for carbs or constantly resisting temptation?

What if the real answer is changing how we think about food entirely?

The Modern Food Trap

The truth is, most people today:
 Eat constantly, whether they’re hungry or not.
 Chase pleasure in food instead of eating for fuel.
 Are surrounded by hyper-palatable, addictive foods engineered to make them crave more.

For diabetics, this modern way of eating is a losing battle—because we’re fighting against a food environment designed to make us overeat.

We don’t just have to resist cravings—we have to rewire our mindset.

The Shift: Eating for Function, Not Emotion

Instead of thinking:
 “I miss eating bread, pasta, and sweets like everyone else.”
Shift to:
 “I eat in a way that keeps me strong, in control, and free from disease.”

Instead of:
 “What’s a low-carb alternative for this food I love?”
Think:
 “What foods make me feel my best without triggering cravings?”

Instead of:
 “I have to eat because it’s lunchtime.”
Ask:
 “Am I actually hungry, or just eating out of habit?”

Fasting & Hunger: Reprogramming Your Brain

One of the biggest mindset shifts for me has been realizing:

 Hunger is not an emergency. 

Most people panic at the first sign of hunger. But true hunger isn’t painful. It’s just a signal. It rises, fades, and comes in waves.

When I started eating only when I was truly hungry, I noticed:
 My cravings weakened.
 I felt more in control of my eating.
 I stopped constantly thinking about food.

The End Goal: Food as Fuel, Not as a Master

This is the real key to long-term success with diabetes:

 Eating when your body actually needs food.
 Not chasing food for pleasure or emotional reasons.
 Breaking free from the constant cycle of cravings, overeating, and restriction.

At first, it feels impossible. But when you stop seeing food as entertainment and start seeing it as a tool for survival and well-being, everything changes.

You no longer feel deprived—because you no longer crave the foods that once controlled you.

And that’s real food freedom.
label: yes stigma
