title: Supporting my parents with diabetes but need help
post text:
Hello! My parents are almost 50 (my mom) and almost 70 (my dad) and they both have diabetes. But I am only 15 and I don't really know how to help them.

I didn't know it had so many kinds of diabetes! They have the kind that's caused by not having a good diet, so your pancreas gets overworked and can't make insulin any more so you can't process sugars. I think that's Type 2.

My dad is very overweight, and his doctor gave him insulin and told me to starve him (jokingly ofc) but he stopped using insulin because it was too expensive. But I am confused, because my dad is a farmer and does a lot of physical labour, and I thought exercise is supposed to make you healthy? But he takes these pills, called Metformin, to keep his sugar down.

My mom had gestational diabetes when she was pregnant with me and then developed pre-diabetes when I was younger so I think it's kinda my fault. She started feeling really sick around 2021 or 2022, I can't remember well. It turns out she has diabetes now and her blood sugar was very high, around 200-300 sometimes. I thought blood sugar was supposed to be between 100-250 if you are normal but I learned in Biology yesterday that actually it's supposed to be between 100-150 which is surprising.

Anyways, I get very scared when my mom is sick because she can't do anything, like she feels weak and sometimes she collapses. Sometimes she is very confused and talks to people who aren't there. I think maybe her diabetes is affecting her mental health because her dad also suffered from depression and anxiety and I think schizophrenia but I'm not fully sure about that one. And my mom has a lot of trauma from when she was little. I get scared because I don't know how to help and my dad gets very frustrated because he does not have lots of patience.

Sometimes I help around the house like washing the dishes, cooking, mopping the floor and washing the clothes. But sometimes I get tired and pretend I have school work to so I don't have to help as much, but then I just go to sleep. But I feel really bad because I know they need my help but I am always so tired and worried and often I do have school work to do, I just end up sleeping instead of doing anything. I am scared that I am a horrible person because sometimes I get angry when my mother calls me to help her. But that is because I have trauma from when i was younger but I know it's not right for me to be angry at someone who needs my help.

We live in the Caribbean, where it is quite normal to eat lots of carbohydrates like rice, bread, roti (a type of flatbread), potatoes, flour and things like that. Our meals always have rice or roti to eat with a meat or vegetable. I think the carbohydrates are the main part of the meal really. I know that is not good for diabetes because carbohydrates are broken down into sugars and their bodies cannot process it. But I do not know what is the alternative for them. I don't know how to help. 

We have foods called 'provisons' (yams, cassava, eddoes, taro, unripe bananas) that I think are supposed to be good for diabetics because they have complex sugars that are broken down over a longer period of time so your blood sugar doesn't spike as much, but my dad does not buy these often. What should I suggest they eat, given the typical diet in the Caribbean? I want them to feel better and not just eat whatever and use a pill to bring their blood sugar down, because that doesn't sound healthy.

Also, how do I prevent myself from getting diabetes? I do not want to be sick like that when I grow up. I do not exercise often anymore even though I like it, because it is hard when I am so tired and anxious. But I like to climb trees and run around in the grass in my school.

I don't eat much, because I learnt that diabetes is a lifestyle disease so maybe if I don't eat a lot, i will not get it. But sometimes I get very hungry, especially if I haven't eaten a full meal in a couple of days, and I end up eating a meal. Usually i survive off snacks and things like sausages and toast or two hamburger patties or fruits. I am often very hungry but I learnt to ignore it. I get scared that maybe I'm not really hungry and my blood sugar is high, because I read that you can feel  hungry if your blood sugar is high. I often have a compulsion to test my blood sugar but I am scared to use the needle pen thingy especially after my parents have used it because I do not like to touch things they have used. 

Can anyone please help me with these questions? I am very confused. Thank you in advance

Tl;dr: Both my parents have diabetes, I want to help them but I don't know how to change their diets, and I am scared I will get it too. What should I do?
label: yes stigma

title: Rapid Weight Loss?
post text:
I’m a newly diagnosed diabetic with a 7.1 A1c as of 1/16. I started walking for 45-65 minutes per day and lowering my carb intake to less than 130g total carbs per day. Most days, I will be significantly under 130g. I know the recommended carb intake for everyone and insulin intolerance is different for everybody, so I have just been going on the 30-45g per meal like my diabetic counselor recommended.


I have been tracking my macros and shooting for a target of approximately 1,200 calories per day. I am a 5’3” woman in my early 40s. I was very sedentary before I got the diagnosis, so the exercise is the most I’ve done a decade. I did used to be very active in high school and my early 20s, but much less since I entered the workforce.

Since I started this journey, I have lost approximately 15 pounds. Tbh, I was scared to get on the scale when I started. My last weight was recorded at the doctor’s office like 2 years ago. I feel like I may have gained a little or stayed similar in size, given that all of my clothing was too tight and I even sized up so I wasn’t bursting out of my work pants. I finally bought a scale today and saw that I was 15 pounds down from what I thought I weighed when I started. I know some of this is water weight. However, I do notice a difference in my whole body. My belt size has changed by two notches and all of my clothing is looser. I am not taking any medications to manage my diabetes, so that’s not a factor. 

I know that there are so many factors that could have caused the rapid loss. This is not a result I was looking for, although I did expect a little weight loss, given that I started counting my macros and weighing everything I put in my mouth. There are some days when I don’t get my target of 1,200 calories and few when I’ve gone over a little. I wasn’t watching my diet before I got the diagnosis, but by no means was I eating something like 5,000 calories a day. I also know that not having a starting weight measured on a scale could mean that I haven’t lost as much as I think I have. I did weigh myself at the gym last Sunday and have lost 11 pounds since then. Of course, that was using a different scale, which also is a factor. Now that I have a scale at home, I’m hoping that it’ll standardize the readings. 

Weight loss is obviously a target of mine to help hopefully improve insulin sensitivity, lose visceral fat, and lower my bp and cholesterol. However, I’m concerned that I’m losing a little too fast. Should I up my calories a little? Or should I not be concerned? I just don’t want to crash and burn or break my body anymore than it already is. Obviously, I will mention this to my doctor if I really am concerned. I just wanted to know if I should be a little alarmed.

FYI, I have like 45 pounds to lose to get into the ideal weight range for my height. I also got a CGM, which is unrelated to the weight loss, but I just wanted to put that out there in case anybody suggests it!

Thanks!

title: Dear family members of diabetics posting in here
post text:
I love that you’re coming in here for advice. That’s wonderful! Just know when you describe your loved one in really stigmatizing ways like “there’s no medical reason behind this, he’s just stubborn” or “she doesn’t care about her health” and you wonder why they don’t like all of your suggestions, know that we have lived experience with this condition and we may be telling you things you don’t want to hear, but you might wanna listen. For example, a lot of the time when you mention how “he’s turning his nose up at everything and doesn’t want to take aaaany of my advice. What can I do to control his diet further and force him?” I know you don’t want to hear that your loved one craves the agency we all do as adults, but…they do. Would you like to feel controlled like that?
 
 Just check your language when you describe diabetics. It’s not a character flaw or a sin and the thing is, a significant amount of the time, there’s a valid medical reason behind it, not them personally being diabetic to annoy you. 
 
 For example, if we tell you that your question really needs to be addressed by a doctor or a dietitian, there might be a reason for it that we’ve gone through. 
 
 We actually have to live with this condition. We are the family members everyone complains about because we “just don’t care about our health” but we do. 
 
 When you’re coming into this space it wouldn’t hurt you to have an open mind to our lived experience and not use stigmatizing language that hurts diabetics. It’s frustrating to offer help in goodwill and get “nah, my loved one is just being annoying and could easily do better.”
label: yes stigma

title: Jardiance and stomach pain?
post text:
Hi there, I've been flirting with being full-on diabetic and pre-diabetic for about three years now. In November my sugars were at 6.5 and my doctor put me on once daily Jardiance, 10mg since my stomach could not tolerate Metformin. 

Prior to starting Jardiance, I would have a kind of dull, stomach ache type pain in my upper abdomen off and on during an increase of stress or anxiety. I would have a full/bloated feeling and although I was hungry, I could only tolerate a few bites since I started to feel fuller faster. It would come and go and didn't neccessarily hinder my day to day, but it was annoying. 

However since starting Jardiance, I have noticed an increase in this type of stomach pain, along with some changes in bowel habits - some days I'm constipated, other days I have looser bowel movements, sometimes they are in the shape of ribbons. The pain still comes and goes, but when it's a bad day, it's a baddddddd day. I have also noticed that I have lost a little bit of weight in the eight weeks I've been taking Jardiance - maybe about 5-7 pounds. I do pee a lot and I have been eating less on the days where my pain is more persistent to just avoid that full feeling. 

All this to say... could Jardiance be making this worse? I have an appointment in a week with my doctor, but obviously when you google things it gives you the big C.

title: Angry over disease. What helped you?
post text:
Family has a history of type 2 on my dad's side. Issue is they were unaware for the longest time. I inherited this from them. What I mean to say is I had good habits, stayed away from sugary drinks and candy, etc. I did all this specifically because I saw the stresses and pains my dad had to go through and never wanted to go through any of this. 

Then I got my diagnosis and I felt like I got knocked on my ass. I had a lot of misconceptions on how type 2 worked. Like that it was inheritance. So it completely destroyed my world.

Before. It felt like a fair deal. I gave up things I liked, the sodas, candy, baguettes, etc. In return, I would avoid everything I saw happen to my family. 
Now, everything feels unfair. I'm angry all the time about it. And scared. Even with all the information I've learned about this disease, and the fact my glucose has been perfect since the day of the diagnosis without meds or any life style changes other than altering my diet .
(This whole thing started cause I tried bulking, way too much meat and eggs, plus energy drinks, and a few months of bad sleep= A1C of 7)

I'm just defeated over this. Like I thought I did what I had to already to never wind up making this post and I got railroaded in. I already made an appointment with a therapist to talk about this and try and deal but, just the rage I feel at the world is just unending. At life, my family, even my job. I cant even enjoy my hobbies anymore, nor do I want to do anything in regards to making plans. Just sleep and work.

Did anyone else go through something like this, where you did everything you were supposed to do and life still kicked your teeth in? Just wondering how I'll ever get my life back, or what's left of it.
label: yes stigma

title: Made a free Android app for diabetes care reminders - feedback?
post text:
Hi everyone

  
I have been a type 2 diabetic for about 7 years now, and I recently made an app in my free time to help with my treatment and to remind me to take care of myself. My numbers are okay, and I do try to take care of myself consistently, but there are some days where I struggle, and I think this app serves as a bit of a nudge to help me with those days.

  
Essentially, you can set daily reminders to receive a preloaded quote/fact from one of several categories that will inspire or motivate you to take care of yourself. You can also set your own reminders if you are not a fan of the pre-defined ones.



I would appreciate any feedback! For the record this app is only on Android, but if enough people like it, I would happily port to iOS. 

**Some features are only accessible behind a paywall. Please shoot me a private message if you like the app and I will happily give you a promo code.**

title: Newishly Diagnosed - My Blood Glucose Readings are Very Confusing
post text:
Recently diagnosed 3.5 months ago. A1c almost a 12. Metformin titrated up to max dose and put ozempic. Just recently titrated to 0.5mg. 

I traveled to Europe and had a full cheat day. Pasta, chocolate, wine. Blood glucose never went above 160. Granted I was walking 20k plus steps a day, and only had moderate portions and limited carbs/sugar the rest of the trip. 

Once I got back and my does of ozempic increased to 0.5, my average fasting reading is 110, and post “compliant” meals 130. But randomly it will be very off. 

Tuesday, my readings were 110 all day. I had approximately 40 carbs at dinner time, which included mainly salad but also a small piece of bread with garlic and cheese, and chicken, and my reading two hours later was 200. And hour later 150. 

I woke up the next morning (Christmas) with a reading of 80. Lowest I have ever tested since being diagnosed. (I tested twice because I thought it was a mistake). 

Reading throughout the day were between 80-110. Had carbs and small amount of sweets at dinner for Christmas. Less than 50 carbs. 2 hours post, my reading was 170. 3 hours later, when I went to sleep, my reading was 112. Woke up this am with a fasting glucose of 145. 

I am very confused. Any insight into whether inconsistent responses to carbs and inconsistent fasting glucose readings are normal, please let me know.
label: no stigma

title: A small victory...
post text: Hey all, thanks for being such a.supportive community. I have learned a lot from you! I was dx w/T2 at the end of August without a lot of direction from my medical provider (I got a very long email from the NP). At the time, my A1c was 6.7. I have been more diligent with meals, reading labels and portion sizes, got inro nutrition counseling, got into regular therapy for other issues, and reduced my work stress. I went in for a lab draw to check my A1c, and it was down to 6.5!  I needed that encouragement so much. I have  many family members who have died due to complications, and it's scary to me. I do feel like I'm trying to keep my head just above water in the middle of the ocean sometimes.  The amount of questionable ingredients in food that cause spiking is crazy! I just learned about the dangers of maltodextrin yesterday, and to know it's used ALOT in sugar-free stuff almost gave me an anxiety attack.  But anyway, thank you all again. I'm getting up for another 3 months of incremental changes to make sure this is all sustainable and will be focusing on getting more movement and amping up weight loss efforts. I feel I've accepted reality, and now I can make changes for a more positive outcome later in life.
label: no stigma

title: I know we HAVE to put in the work but I’m exhausted.  /rant
post text:
I'm just discouraged and need to rant. I'll hop back on the horse eventually. But I have no hunger cues and I'm exhausted and cranky. 

I've been diagnosed diabetic for 4 years now. I'm only 32, I have dieted my entire life but also have BED. I've done it all from classes on nutrition, cookbooks, working with counselors and doctors, and idk, I've done it all. 

Right now I'm in the process of finding a new doc (or maybe figuring out why mine sucks). I started out with 11.1 a1c, 5'6 171 lbs (185 at highest), with really intense diet and exercise I got it down with metformin until it wasn't working. Then I got on Mounjaro- the absolutely best thing I've ever had happen. It changed my entire life and I dropped to 117/120lbs and 5.1 a1c, I felt incredible I kept my glucose between 60 and 130 at all times! and maintained for almost a year on the lowest dose because I thought "HEY I'm not that big, I need to maintain this drug forever, I'm going to stay on the lowest dose as long as I can spreading it out" 

Lmao so my insurance said "sounds like you don't need it at all" for whatever reasons and took it away.  My doc said "well we did a peer to peer talk and yeah I just agreed with them"

I've been fighting for months and am still fighting to get my glp1 back. Man FORGET about feeling ignored and not taken seriously for being overweight (something I didn't really even have to deal with... I've been a visually "healthy" looking weight my whole life) but if you get really trim and fit they don't care about you either turns out!!!!! Lose lose. 

Because I was smaller they didn't think the drug was doing much. I don't give a flying thing about my weight. I'm 32, I put effort in my appearance, my vanity and my health are separate and maybe I lumped it all together because being thin was like a joke I talked to my doc about. Like "wow it worked. I feel like a kardashian" I should not have made the joke, i don't know the kardashians health history? I just said it because I'm nervous and weird and awkward. I have body dysmorphia like the next guy but like health trumps that by a long shot. I'll deal with my insecurities some other time if I'm not feeling good. 

Anyway. I'm sitting here. Typing. Off the drug that made me feel like a normal person for once in my life. I've gained 13lbs in about 8 weeks... my CGM keeps going off and I have so much work to do, but after weeks of doing weights and running 3mi/day I'm back at my 8-6 and can't find time, I also don't have the energy. I'm fasting at 130... 

And it all feels like my fault. It's my fault I'm gaining weight and overeating and my glucose is messed up again. But is it my fault because a) I'm a failure of a human being for getting t2 so young and fighting my BED like a bitch? Or b) I fought really hard to get the meds, researched so hard, pushed myself to the lifestyle it allowed me to achieve that I could not achieve before... then because I'm a super responder I didn't play the game right and now insurance companies don't believe me...?

I feel like a failure. I wouldn't extend that sentiment to ANY of you here... but why do I only want to be so hard on myself? 

I am a failure. I'll figure it out I guess I always have. But man, those 18 months of Mounjaro were the best time of my life. I will not ever look back and say I took it for granted. I used that time to really set myself up for ultimate health. But once I was off, right back to feeling worse and worse every day. And the worse I feel the less I can manage it. I got my first UTI since starting Mounjaro. There's no way I'm doing my post snack walk. I'm in tears...! 

Anyway, screw the insurance company. I'm exhausted seeking other docs, life sucksssss. But it'll be fine. It'll get better. Thanks for hearing me out. I guess I'm not a quitter but I worry I am.

title: Is Metformin necessary when on Mounjaro?
post text:
I was on Metformin for years and my A1C in May was 10.9 even when trying low carb.  After 4 months on Mounjaro (2 months in 2.5 and 2 months on 5) it was 5.4.  My dr said I could go up to 10 and skip 7.5 but I decided on 7.5 mg.  I had a lot of stomach issues even on 2.5 mg and after 6 months I stopped Mounjaro.  

My appetite came back and was bloated so I asked my dr to start again but without Metformin.  He told me the 2 together should not cause stomach issues and I can start the Mounjaro again at 2.5 mg.  I decided on my own to stop the Metformin and start 2.5 mg of Mounjaro on my own.  My first go on 2.5 mg I had a lot of diarrhea.  This time without Metformin (stopped 3 days before I started Mounjaro again) I didn’t have any.

title: Disease progression: optimal eating vs acceptable eating?
post text:
Let's say a Type-2 diabetic is able, through weight loss and meds, to control their blood glucose so that it is under 100 mg/dL all day and night when they eat a healthy, low-carb diet. I'll call this 'optimal eating.' Then, let's say the same person can eat a meal with 30 g of carbs (e.g., modest piece of lasagna or a couple breaded chicken tenders) and their blood glucose rises to a peak of 140 mg/dL in about an hour, but is down to 100 mg/dL at the two hour mark. I'll call this 'acceptable eating.'

The acceptable eating certainly makes the pancreas work harder than the optimal eating, but does this stress accelerate disease progression in an appreciable way? In other words, is any insulin production in a Type-2 diabetic depleting a finite resource that they should be conserving? Non-diabetics eat whatever they want and assume that their pancreas will last effectively forever. Does a diabetic with 'quasi-normal' behavior have the same assumption?

Not looking for any medical advice here. Just curious if people have thoughts or have had conversations with their endos, etc.

title: Tandem App!!
post text:
So the Tandem tSlim app flipped out on me today. It's not too big of a deal since I know what causes my pump to go from 100% charge to 15% in under eight hours. 
 
 But it was during training as a USPS rural route associate. Fuck me right? I made sure I was at 100% when I left my house this morning. Everything is cool until about 12:00, thirty minutes until lunch time. All the sudden I get the three buzzes. I keep my pump muted as much as possible and know the difference between a high (buzz buzz) and other shit is going on (buzz buzz buzz). I take a look at it and we're down to 15% at noon. Now I usually don't carry around a car charger or USB battery pack with me, but now on the job I absolutely will have both in my LLV (Long Life Vehicle).
 
 I know that the tSlim app has issues sometimes draining batteries on the pump. So I immediately uninstall the app and go for my second ride around the obstacle course. Doing good delivering fake mail. Ok it's about 1:45 and time to go out on the streets. Down to 12%. Let's fucking go! We drive around town, learning how to not hit mail boxes and kids while keeping a right hand drive vehicle on the streets in the US. Kill the driving test. It's 2:45, down to 10%. Yep it's that fucking app! Go back inside and take the written test. Cool, cool only miss one and I know it's because I don't want to loose my settings in my pump. I'm stressed. Passed the test, now I have to drive to my main office to turn in paperwork. Luckily it's only 10-15 min away. Get there, find a supervisor to turn in my paperwork saying I passed the driver's course and out to my vehicle. 5% left. I head straight home. Get through the door, give my puppers a whole bunch of love and sit down, plug in and save my settings. 
 
 I almost lost all my pump settings ( I know them in my head but still). Even though I was stressed I still was able to keep things in control, I never bounced over 150 and was cool calm and collected. 
 
 I hope some newbies can get a little calm from this. I know things can be hard. But you can get through this.

title: Been a long time
post text:
It's been a almost 5 years since I've been on this sub-reddit. I was on a completely different account, weighed over 420 lbs, A1C was 14, bg was 700 back in Jan 2020.

I got back into managing my diabetes (first diagnosed in 2016, stopped managing in 2018 due to no insurance at 26), went on heavy meds (2000mg of metformin, jardiance and trulicity)truly city), exercised regularly, went low-carb. By Sept. of 2021 I was 225 lbs. Most of that loss was the drugs, and when I realized that after I got off of them, I slowly started creeping up to 310 lbs, due in no small part to getting into a relationship (first in 13 years) and breaking up...twice.

But it's 2025, and although I'm still around 289, my bg is at a constant 94-105, still doing low-carb religiously, meds have fluctuated to none to just taking jardiance and trulicity, and A1C has been consistently at 5.7-6.1 on and off meds. I've been hitting the gym, so I know some of my weight is muscle, I'm trimming and toning and I had my chest surgery in 2021 to celebrate my massive improvement.

I wanted to say this as a thank you to those who gave me advice to build better habits so I could get off the meds and feel healthier. This is also meant to encourage others: this is a marathon, not a race. It's for life, but it doesn't have to be the end of the world. Stay strong, get support and those who will lift you up and encourage you, and stick with it, you'll love the changes you see!!

title: Recently diagnosed and would some help
post text:
I was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes on, of all days, Halloween. My A1C was 11.3, and my fasting glucose was 335. I had ignored all the signs: extreme thirst, a constant need to urinate, a sweet taste in my mouth, tingling in my feet, a family history of diabetes, and unexplained weight loss.

I went for my annual eye appointment, and while the doctor didn’t see signs of diabetes in my eyes, he was concerned about the changes in my left eye over just one year. That prompted me to visit my primary care physician, where I was officially diagnosed.

Since then, I’ve taken my diagnosis very seriously. I consistently monitor my glucose levels, which are now around 99 before dinner and 130 in the mornings. I’ve heard that type 2 diabetes is something that can be reversed or beaten, but is that accurate?

It feels more like it doesn’t truly go away but instead goes into “remission.” To me, it’s more about learning to make the right decisions and keeping it under control. What are your thoughts?

post text: I was diagnosed prediabetic like a month ago. My HbA1c was 54 mmol/mol. I dont even know how bad this is. They didnt really explain anything or tell me what to do or not to do. They just said to eat normally and that i can even eat candy if i like which is odd. Im not on any meds, i just measure twice a week in the morning and after a meal. Right now my morning readings have been about 7-8 and after meal 9-12. I have been doing extra measurements because every now and then i get weirdly shaky and weak, and when this happens im at 3.9-4.5. This usually happened 3-4 hours after a meal. I told my doctor and she said its completely fine and i dont need to change my eating times or anything and that the awful feeling i get is propably from something else and my body will fix it on its own. Huh? I literally just told her i get the shakes, measure, im at like 4, then eat and it goes away. How would it be from something else? I tried to just eat normally but i would get so nervous of getting the shakes that now i eat something every 3 hours. But the problem is that sometimes 3 hours after a meal im at 12, sometimes 4. I never know, i dont have the money to keep using so many testing strips to check. Im almost every meal scared to wait a little longer if i dont feel like eating yet because what if i get the shakes, but im also scared to eat because what if im high and go even higher... I also get scared when leaving the house incase i get low or something... The anxiety of not knowing my levels all the time has been so bad, i was adviced to lessen my measurements as doing them just feeds my anxiety and im aware of that but then im just scared and trying to check how i feel all the time, and i mean like sometimes every few minutes i check of my hands are shaky, try to grab something to see if im weak etc. Im neurodivergent and dont feel hunger so i cant go based off of that, so i have set times to eat but sometimes i have gotten the shakes before that time, sometimes im high and feel sick and cant eat. Idk what to do. Sometimes for days i have no appetite and eat barely anything, so im constantly scared of going low. I cant even rely on how im feeling because i struggle with 24/7 anxiety and i get shaky, sweaty or weak randomly from that. I asked her that what if i get the sensor so i could manage this better, but she said theres no need and i dont need to adjust my eating based on my levels. Idk if this info is correct and im just stressing or have i been given bad advice? I feel so hopeless and scared all the time i cry almost daily because of all of this :( i already struggled with eating, my diet is very bad and a psychiatrist has suspected i have arfid. When i got diagnosed with this, i lost all my comfort foods because they were all sugary. I was barely a normal weight and have been scared of the fact that i will lose weight because i eat way less calories now that i stopped all sweet stuff. I have tried so hard to try new foods but i cant, i sometimes almost gag on even my favourite foods. title: Rampant A1C numbers, yeast infection, and weight loss.
post text:
Welcome to my chaos. I have written other posts concerning my vaginal health asking for help, but after 4 months of having my life upended from it, a Doctor finally suggested that my diabetes and blood sugars could be to blame for the recurring yeast infection. I am curious if anyone else has experienced this? 

Bit of a back story. I was diagnosed with T2D in Aug of 2024. My A1C then was 6.7. I was in denial so put off accepting it till October. My numbers then blasted up to 9.7! Doc gave me a run down of what it meant, said it was a new diagnosis and no need to test my sugars or monitor other than regular blood tests. I by no means have a perfect diet, but after having to undergo a FODMAP diet for a different issue, my diet is drastically better than it was. So I'm thinking that's going great.

Yeast issues started in Sept. They never went away and resisted ever intervention we tried, and there were many. Meanwhile, I was dazzled by the way Metformin seemed to clear up the fluid retention I had battled in my legs and hands and saw pounds falling off the scale.  So I'm thinking that's going great! 

Meanwhile, 4 months into the yeast and contact dermatitis issue of my vulva, and 2 months away from seeing a specialist, a doctor I was able to get on the phone was like, How is your blood sugar? Of course I have no idea because I was told I didn't need to monitor. She thinks my blood sugar is high and my blood is literally feeding the yeast and making it impossible to clear up! Frustrated, I'm fairly pissed this is JUST NOW being suggested. 

So I get blood work and a urinalysis. Not only is my glucose and keytones in my urine abnormal, but my A1C has shot up all the way to 13.4! After making significant changes in diet, even over the holidays.

As far as my weight, I have lost 40 lbs in the past 11 mos, 13 of those in the last 27 days. I feel weird being worried about this, as I really need to lose weight, but this is a lot for the changes I have made. 

So. All that said.... Has anyone with a vulva had experience with a resistant yeast infection? Do you have any advice or know what helped you turn it around? 

Also, I am totally diabetes illiterate. Has anyone else experienced these crazy and drastic (to me) spikes in their numbers? How do you get them back down and control this shit? title: Taking more insulin isn't cheating
post text:
When I started a few months ago I was in this mindset that I had a carb ratio of 1:10, and that was it; unless you my sugar went above 10 I couldn't take more insulin than that. I had a lot of mental blocks about how I would manage my blood glucose and insulin use.

I started going to the gym and my diabetes team reduced my basal dose. I was scared that if I couldn't make it to the gym I would be stuck with Hugh sugars. Then when that eventually happened I went from 92% in range to  72% in range.

And then I just raised my basal again, and I'm doing even better. I just broke through this idea that I need anybody else's permission or blessing to change things. I have a less aggressive carb ratio during the day, and a very aggressive approach in the morning.

I see people talking about being sick or stressed, or going through something that makes them more insulin resistant; guys and gals, you can take more insulin if you need it. I heard someone on a well known podcast say "It isn't stacking if you need it."

I know some of us have some pride in having a "good" insulin to carb ratio, but if taking 30 units of Lantus and a ratio of 1:3.5 gets you under control, do it.

And if you need to take less to stop hypos, take less.

"Rage bolusing" isn't wrong either. There seems to be a real phenomenon that many of us have noticed where high blood sugar makes us more insulin resistant (and I'd love for somebody to study it). If you need more insulin to get movement against that 15/300 than you do against that 11/200, then take more (and if you don't, then don't). Of course once you break through that resistance you have a whole lot of insulin in your system, so you can have a snack to give yourself a smoother landing; plan it in advance if you want.

I guess what I'm saying is that your diabetes is YOUR diabetes. How you manage it is up to you, and you shouldn't be fixated on rules like correction factors and insulin: carb ratios which working pancreases don't know and don't care about. You didn't have an insulin: carb ratio before you were diabetic; your pancreas just saw excess glucose, and fixed it; if it wasn't enough, it made more insulin. If it was too much, it held back with the insulin and secreted glucagon.

title: High Fasting Blood Sugar Despite Healthy Habits - Anyone Else Experiencing This?
post text:
Hi everyone,

I've been diligently tracking my blood sugar levels for a while now, and I'm noticing a concerning pattern.

Post-meal: My blood sugar typically peaks around 140-150 mg/dL, even after larger meals.
 
Random: Throughout the day, my random checks are usually in the 110-120 mg/dL range.

Fasting: This is where things get strange. My fasting blood sugar (first thing in the morning) consistently hovers around 170-180 mg/dL.

Here's the kicker: 

I consider myself to have a pretty healthy lifestyle:
I work out regularly. I try to take a walk after most meals, especially dinner. I generally eat a balanced diet.

The weird part: Even on days when I forget to take my medication or skip my post-meal walk, my post-meal sugar levels remain relatively stable.

Has anyone else experienced this? I'm scheduled for a doctor's appointment soon, but I'm curious to hear if anyone else has encountered similar issues and what lifestyle changes (if any) helped improve their fasting blood sugar.

title: Diabetes, Kidney Disease, and etc...
post text:
I was approached by someone in my chat about a post I did awhile back about Dr. Richard K. Bernstein.  He is one of my heroes, also a 90 year old type 1 diabetic (one of the last of his generation), an endocrinologist, and the author of the book 'Diabetes Solution'.  The person who approached me is dealing with pre diabetes and it looks like some kidney damage.  Dr. Bernstein claims in some of his YouTube videos that he reversed his kidney disease.  I've spent a good amount of time watching his videos and taking notes.  I decided I ought to put up those notes on a site I maintain and have the person search for "kidney" to see where Dr. Bernstein talks about diabetes and kidney disease in his videos - and at what minute.  I thought while I was sharing with one person, possibly I ought to share with more?  So here I am, and here are those notes:  You can search for "kidney", or whatever else you are dealing with.

title: Just diagnosed today
post text:
I (43m) have been obese my whole life, but my labs have always been in the normal range. I have always figured it would eventually catch up with me. 

The last few months I’ve put some more weight on. I had my annual physical a week or so ago, and over the weekend my Dr left a voicemail telling me that my labs “overall look good, but we need to talk about your blood sugar.”

I figured it’s finally caught up; I’m probably pre-diabetic. We finally connected today, and I’ve skipped over pre-diabetic and right into a diagnosis. I was at work and wrote down the numbers he gave me (but forgot to bring them home), but I didn’t really have the context to know what they meant or how “bad” they were (or even what units they were and what exactly was even being measured). If I remember right, I believe the numbers were 140 and 6.6 (this was fasting). 

My head has been spinning. I’m disappointed in myself (more than usual), because my immediate and louder thought is “failure” as opposed to the more rational/accurate “disease.”

What advice can you give to someone JUST finding this out. I understand I’ll need to actually get serious about diet and activity (and I can read more about what I specifically need to do), but I’m looking for maybe the less obvious advice. Things like dealing with the psychological side; how to determine target blood glucose ranges (I bought an inexpensive monitor off amazon; is that going to be sufficient, at least at first?), what should I be educating myself on beyond diet/activity?

Frankly this is scaring me. When I was 7 I watched my 40 year old, obese and diabetic mother die of a heart attack, so that background doesn’t exactly ease my anxiety. 

Thanks in advance. 

Edited to add: I’ll be starting metformin as soon as the pharmacy fills the Rx. No insulin at this time.

title: 12.1 Hba1c to 5.8
post text:
I(M27) was diagnosed late August with T2D and had Hba1c of 12.1% . Doctor started 1000mg Metmorfin and Gliclazide 60mg . I gradually cutoff carbs and started keto diet . Did weight training every day and atleast 30min cardio. Even on rest days they were active rest days and I did walk usually after dinner . Lost 27lbs till now . Had hba1c test today and result was 5.8% . Happy with the progress and will continue to do so . 
Sometimes I did eat Pasta and burgers but I did alot of workout the same day . I had a modest amount of protein intake which helped in controlling my spikes. 

Fasting glucose usually between 80-100 
After food its usually around 110-130 


Hard work , dedication and good lifestyle can keep T2D under control. We shouldn’t lose hope if we have high hba1c , it can come down for all us if we just learn to control our bad eating habits

title: Finally taking my BS! Have a question tho.
post text:
I’ve posted on here before about being fearful of taking my BS testing every day.  I was afraid if it was too high or too low, what that meant and what I should do.  (I have a history of medical trauma with PTSD).  But this kind, compassionate community was so supportive and I worked through my fears and my kit arrived today.  

I experienced a pretty bad low this morning before the kit arrived.  I was shakey and anxious and unsteady.  But I read up on the 15-15 rule and ate half a banana and drank a Glucerna then waited awhile and had the rest of the banana with some tuna and a couple crackers.  Took a nap, the kit arrived and it’s 137.  Just good to know I can now have some actual data to know what to do. 

Question:  how do people track their foods and BS readings?  I use Carb Manager which gives me a weekly summary and my test kit (Metene TD 4116) probably has a printout but I was thinking of doing an Excel spreadsheet to have all the data in one place.  I’m relatively new to all this although I was diagnosed a few years ago. Did a complete 180 as far as care and management when my feet started being tingly and numb a couple months ago.  Hey, some of us are just slow learners. 

Thoughts on how to track it all?

title: Eye exam next week!!
post text:
I’m a little nervous, as it’s been 3 years since my last exam, and my diabetes has not been well-managed for most of that. I go on Tuesday to see an ophthalmologist (used to just do the LensCrafters-associated exams with the added retina scan or whatever they do). I am genetically predisposed to macular degeneration, and I have always had a funny feeling I’ll end up impaired somehow. I don’t *want* it to happen, but the last couple years have been a struggle, mentally, which has made it harder to do the things I need to do. So we shall see if I’ve screwed myself. 

The good news is that I’m doing a million times better as far as mental health, so whatever the result is, I’m prepared to deal with it in a positive, progressive manner.

title: When did you stop seeing a dietitian?
post text:
I (49m) was first diagnosed about 3 years ago. I went on metformin and saw my doctor’s in-house dietitian. I lost 40 pounds, cut down on carbs, and tried to add more vegetables.

My sessions with the in-house dietitian seemed repetitive after a few months  and I was somewhat frustrated that she was an older lady who didn’t seem very data driven. After I got my A1C down from 12.1 to 5.9% in 6 months I stopped scheduling follow-ups with the dietitian.

Six months ago I noticed my A1C was creeping up to 6.3% and gained weight again. I found a new dietitian who knew how to track my diet with MyFitnessPal and I used CGM for a while. I lost 30 pounds and am due for my 6-month labs this month to see if any of this was effective. 

I guess I should wait for labs to see if I’m going in the right direction or need more intensive efforts. If A1C is reduced, should I cancel with the dietitian?

The dietitian sessions seem repetitive at this point. She is more about accountability now — which is helpful and she is more data-driven than my first dietitian. I still need to lose more weight — am losing about 1 pound a week now, which isn’t great but still in the right direction. I could be more strict with my diet, but it’s the holidays so...

Are dietitian sessions eventually just about accountability? I mean how many ways can someone say lower calories, less carbs, more vegetables?

title: Fasting blood glucose levels still high despite weight loss and lifestyle changes
post text:
Hi everyone,

A few months ago, I was diagnosed with prediabetes (A1c of 5.8). Since then, I’ve made some serious changes:

* I’ve lost 17 pounds (I’m a 24-year-old male).
* I’ve cleaned up my diet, cutting out most processed foods and focusing on whole, nutrient-dense meals.
* I’ve been doing intermittent fasting because it fits my lifestyle, helps with calorie control, and I actually enjoy it.

Despite these efforts, my **fasting blood glucose levels aren’t improving.** My readings are typically between 126–140, and the lowest I’ve seen is 109. To better manage things, I recently got a continuous glucose monitor (CGM).

Today, though, I’m feeling pretty discouraged. My CGM showed my glucose at **160** around noon, which I confirmed with a fingerstick (145). The confusing part? I woke up at **6:30 AM** and haven’t eaten a thing since. Where is this glucose coming from?

I understand the liver can release glucose through **gluconeogenesis** and **glycogenolysis**, but if the goal of managing or reversing diabetes is to keep glucose levels stable, what can I do? I’m already eating clean, losing weight, and fasting. Physically, I feel like I’ve made progress, but metabolically, it seems like I’m stuck.

Does anyone know:

1. How long it typically takes for **insulin sensitivity** to improve after making lifestyle changes?
2. If fasting glucose is this high even without eating, is there something else I should focus on?
3. Any tips or advice for someone in my situation?

Feeling a little frustrated and could really use some insights. Thanks in advance for your help!
label: no stigma

title: Newly diagnosed T2 Diabetic. Due to start Metformin next week.
post text:
I was prompted to have my bloods taken due to feeling off last September, fatigue, strange tingly sensation in my hands, migraines, blurred vision, thirst… you name it. At my first request my Gp turned me away and simply stated I don’t need one unless they ask for one. So I plodded along, symptoms still there on and off, then rolls around January this year… symptoms STILL persisting and this time I had to call an ambulance because I felt like I was dying. Fast heart rate, confusion, extreme exhaustion, cold sweats and a very strange metallic sort of taste in my mouth that intensified (only way I can describe it)… anyway, paramedics arrived, told I was having a panic/anxiety attack and left. That’s when I thought I really need to get checked out as my wife is already diagnosed pre diabetic. So I walk into my GP pretty much demanding I have a blood test because I think I’m either pre diabetic or diabetic. Cue 2 blood tests later due to a HbA1c reading of 53mmol average, I’m confirmed T2 diabetic as of yesterday. I had already made changes to my diet prior to being diagnosed as I was constantly drinking Pepsi Max Cherry and ordering fast food and eating rubbish, now I eat very little, cut out all sugars, stopped all junk food and I drink only bottled water. I know it’s more for T1 diabetics but I slapped on a Dexcom G7 to track my BG and well… here we are. 

Yesterday I felt awful all day, BG was 7.5 literally all day but my wife did a “healthy” bolognese thing with chopped tomatoes, kale, a small portion of lean mince and whole grain blue dragon noodles for tea the night previous… apparently all good to diabetics. Apparently not. Those symptoms lasted from the night previous, all through yesterday and seemed to have now disappeared. I have woken up today feeling refreshed and most important my migraine has gone! I’ll be staying away from red meat as I was fine with chicken. 

Here’s to me trying to reverse this with weight loss and eating healthy I guess.
label: no stigma

title: The hard work is paying off.
post text:
With my new job I got some pretty great insurance and reconnected with my PCP in December. Got on insulin, along with Mounjaro. I am also taking metformin, and when I met with my diabetes educator we talked about a CGM. The CGM has been a huge help personally in seeing what affects me in real time vs the finger poking. 

I’ve kept up with my injections and medicine and today I got my updated A1C of 8.3! I know that’s still high but that’s a huge improvement considering in December I was at 12.8! 

The goal is to eventually get off insulin, Mounjaro, and just take metformin or better yet have it so manageable I don’t need to continue with medications. I know this disease can be overwhelming but we can do it, trust that it will pay off!

title: DKA: How I got into this mess
post text:
55M, 175 lbs.  I've got a great job with great benefits, yet like an idiot I went 10 years between doctor visits.  Didn't even have a primary.  Then March 2020 happened.

After 2.5 days of vomiting I checked into a hospital.  Docs quickly determined I was in DKA with an A1C at 13.7.  They resolved it but the hurling continued.  They thought it might be flu or covid (it was early March 2020, after all), but I was neg for all that.  Turned out my gall bladder had gone necrotic and I had not felt a thing, most likely because my undiagnosed diabetes had killed the nerves around the gb.  

The good news is the experience scared me straight, so to speak, and I reformed my dietary ways.  Since then the A1C is checked twice/year and has ranged between 5.5 and 6.5.  As mid-life health crises go, I got off easy.

title: Recently diagnosed, full of questions and unsure what to do
post text:
Quick backstory, I've had a family crisis going on for an extended time, and thought stress from it plus work burnout were causing my fatigue and digestive issues. In the past, I've kept my weight and stress in check with a lot of walking and weight lifting, but since Covid, I had fallen off, plus my diet had gone to hell and I was drinking a ton of diet soda every day.

I went in for UTI treatment at an urgent care on November 25th and my glucose was 500. A tech told me this was a concern but I wasn't given any additional information. I'd been in in September for a blood screen, but never received any information about any problems. A blood screen taken December 2nd revealed my blood glucose was 240. After the high urinary glucose read, I'd gotten a glucometer, read about low glycemic index and ultra-low carb diets and had been trying to eat fewer than 50 carbs a day.

I finally managed to get in to be seen by a pcp, who prescribed Metformin, I currently take 500 mg twice a day. Last week, I was fitted with a CGM.

I've lost 15 lbs so far (175 to 160 lbs) through careful food choices, portion control, and walking. The Metformin helps a lot, but my numbers still are higher than I'd like. In the AM I usually wake up anywhere from 110 to 125 and it tends to rise to 150 when I exercise, then drop off to around 125-136. In the afternoons, it slowly ramps down and by bedtime it's usually 90-100. During the night I see it can gets down around 70-80.

I've asked the nutritionist, pcp, and diabetic counselor a lot of questions, they just keep telling me with an A1c as high as mine was (13.8) it will take time to correct, then keep telling me they're going to help me 'reverse' this (while also telling me how horrible diabetes is and what terrible painful things will happen to me). They also want me on Muonjaro, but I'm concerned about some of the side effects.

I'm in a rural area with a severe doctor/healthcare shortage and it took me over a year to get in with the pcp.

Any information about what numbers I should be striving for, better ways to manage my eating habits (I am focusing a lot on protein and leafy veg with occasional other veg, and fats like natural peanut butter, tracking calories, I don't go over 1200 a day), or in general be more effective would be so appreciated.
label: yes stigma

title: Struggling with motivation—why do you keep going?
post text:
Hey everyone,

Lately, I’ve been feeling really drained by the constant effort it takes to manage diabetes. Some days, it just feels like too much—the numbers, the planning, the never-ending decisions. I know I *should* stay on top of it, but honestly, I’m struggling to remember *why* it matters so much.

So I wanted to ask: *Why do you keep going?* What makes you push through the tough days? Is it about feeling better in the moment, avoiding complications, showing up for the people you love—or something else?

I guess I’m just looking for a little inspiration. If you’ve ever felt like this, how did you find your way back to caring? I’d really appreciate hearing your thoughts.

Thanks for reading. Hope you're all doing okay.

title: I have nothing left that makes me happy. I hate existing.
post text:
Diagnosed a bit more than a month ago. In that month I gave up carbs and focused on exercising and diet control. I lost 20 Lbs already and I lose more by the day. 

My life is a living hell. I was always relatively healthy. What I mean to say is I didn't become diabetic due to my weight or habits. In this case it was mainly genetic, even found out while in the process of bulking for the gym...

My point is that I rarely gave myself a treat, maybe on a cheat day, or if I was out with company. But now everything fucking sucks. I cant eat anything I enjoy. It's been a month straight like this and I've started to dread eating. I don't get any cheat days anymore...

 I've made it my mission to stay in remission for as long as possible. I hate my family for passing this disease on to me that Ill have to live with the rest of my life, but Ill be damned if I'm just going to sit here and take it. 

Same goes for my hobbies. I used to enjoy very sedentary hobbies apart from the gym. And I only used to do the gym to avoid catching Type 2 Diabetes. (The fucking good it did me)
Now I can't do any of them with any peace of mind. Can't paint my miniatures. Can't watch tv or a movie without getting antsy and walking around a bit. Can't play games, or build models etc. 


Suffice it to say that if it gave me joy, diabetes took whatever it was out back and shot it in the head. I literally have nothing left that gives me an ounce of joy. I just go to work, eat some healthy nutritional slop, go home, eat more nutritional slop, and then go walk on a treadmill like a hamster to wrap my day up. I hate every second of it. My blood sugar doesn't even break 110 after eating. I'm never hungry anymore either, not that it was a problem before mind you, I physcially don't get hungry anymore, probably cause I know the shit Ill have to eat, so I guess that's an upside.

I've tried going out to do my walking in public spaces to clear my head and be amongst others but I just get angry, bitter, and jealous of the people around me. They get to be unhealthy and overweight, but they get to enjoy their youth and their lives however they please, with at worst hypertension or something easy to treat with diet and exercise. 
And the smell of the food shops and stands make it all hurt that much more. All those places I had been to one time or another, and now I cant even look at them. I'd rather burn the world to the ground at this point than keep going. I just have nothing left anymore. 

If anyone else has been in a similar position. Please let me know what's made it better. Please...

title: Need some advice
post text:
Type 2 here. I take 1000mg Metformin 2x daily right now. My sugars have been really high lately. Been working on diet and exercise, but I’ve been dealing with some chronic dizziness (high blood sugar def contributing to that I’m sure) which has made it harder for me to stay as active as I would like. 

I was taking 25mg of Januvia with Metformin for a while and it helped decently with my readings but didn’t help with the dizziness or made it worse. Also had strange GI affects, felt like shit when I ate most things, headaches, had to piss non stop. So I stopped taking it. 

I’ve been having some scary readings in the morning of 250’s, down to 150-180 a few hours after eating. 

My doctor prescribed me *5mg GLIPIZIDE. I took it for the first time today and it very suddenly brought me down to like 86 after few hours after taking it, and I did NOT feel good, like way worse than the Januvia. Had a tiny spoon of honey and went up to like 110 and felt a little better. Im going to try it for a while before stopping , and make sure I have some kind of sugar on me in case I feel like that again. 

But I’m wondering if I should just try something like ozempic. I’m scared to take that shit because it seems to have a lot of side effects. I’m also stressed about the longterm use of it, but it also seems easier than worrying about timing a med like glipizide cause I can’t really test super easily while at work most days and can’t afford a CGM right now. 

I just really want to get my BG in check. My doctor thinks it will help my dizziness. I feel kinda stupid for being resistant to meds like ozempic but I’ve read stuff about it and it’s made me nervous to say the least. 

*Edited for spelling and stuff. I’m tired lol.
label: no stigma

title: T1D College Scholarships 2025
post text:
I gotta vent. Let me start by saying I know this sounds like I'm feeling sorry for myself. I'm a HS Senior with T1D who's been looking **everywhere** for scholarships. My family is middle class and can afford some of the schools I've been accepted to with the money they've given me, but the problem is that I have a sibling in school and the FASFA doesn't take that into account when giving aid, so they gave us basically nothing. I don't know how much my parents make but with both of us going to school, my dad has already said no to schools I really like.

There are quite literally no T1D scholarships I qualify for this year. I've seen "Athletes with T1D" or "T1D students with financial need" but neither fully apply to me. There are disability scholarships, but past year winners have cancer, paralyzed legs, prosthetics, etc. I don't think I have any shot for those.

It just sucks because T1D has seriously affected my life (plus I was diagnosed during covid, two months before high school) and I everyone kept telling me about how they got scholarships in past years that really helped them. This year no one is offering any.

Idk I feel link this sounds like complaining but I tried so hard to bring my grades up from freshman year. I was hospitalized frequently because of my mental health and my lack of care for my body after being diagnosed. I'm so proud of all I've accomplished, but it feels like my struggles were bad enough to completely change my life, but not big enough that I "get something good from it." Idk how to explain it, I sound so petty but yeah.

title: Dexcom Support Disappointed Me Today
post text:
Really frustrated. Had an issue with a sensor. Called Dexcom for support, which has been a breeze everytime. They have always been so kind, and so quick to solve the problem, even overnighting sensors to me when I let them know I was low on them.

Today? Not so much. The person I talked to was very blunt, as well as not really helpful. Made me answer questions they have never ever asked me before, and then to top it off, said that they now only do 3 good will replacements a year… and that I used 2 up. 

What the f*ck is going on? 

I’m so upset just because they have been so incredibly helpful and such a good company to get support from since I started the sensors two years ago. Always willing, always ready to help, understanding that something you wear on your body 24/7 is bound to fail from time to time.

I’m just kind of floored. Really concerned about that new policy too. If your piece of medical equipment doesn’t work, replace it. It is that simple, and they’ve always treated it as such.

What changed? Am I the only one who has experienced something like this? Or is this a growing problem with Dexcom? From what I see in this sub, kind of seems like they’re starting to go downhill.

Incredibly disappointing. Really sucked some joy out of my day, and blindsided me.  

I guess companies are going to do what they do best at the end of the day. Should’ve known Dexcom could turn too.

Please let me know if this was truly a one off experience, or if any of yall have had issues too. I’m truly curious. 

TL:DR: called Dexcom for replacement and was very disappointed in service. Feeling sad about it lol.

title: Not Sure What To Title This So Just Read The Post
post text:
For quite some time I have wanted a simple but versatile application/software for keeping track of diabetic information.  Things like dates and times that I put on and take off sensors/sets, as well as other criteria.  Maybe even for keeping track of shipments/deliveries from the supplier.  Up until now, Ive just manually logged all the information in an Excel document.  Doing it all manually is tedious and can be time consuming.  

Well, lucky me, OpenAI released a new model this weekend and Ive been playing with it.  Im by no means a programmer, I have fundamental knowledge of coding, but not enough to build anything.  I have tried using various AI models in the past to create the perfect piece of software to streamline as much of this process as possible, but none of them have been able to even break the ice in terms of making something thats even somewhat functional.  

After less than 2 hours of going back and forth with this new ChatGPT o3 model, Ive got a working application that is almost exactly what I want.  All I need to do is make more changes to get it perfect, which this bot is able to do without issue.  I just need to get some sleep for the night so Im pausing my progress.  

TLDR:  ChatGPT makes software for diabetics





THE IMPORTANT STUFF:

My reason for posting this is because I want suggestions for features you all would want in this application.  I fully intend on releasing the final product, free and open-source, to you all when it’s done.  So please, if anyone has any suggestions, feel free to comment them.  I know there has to be others out there that would benefit from this.

EDIT: I really appreciate whoever sees this and is instantly downvoting both the post and comments, you sure are a good person and deserve a community of fellow diabetics who are thoughtful and willing to help out everyone here.

The parents of a type 1 diabetic go into how they have to check their child's blood sugar every 3 hours and especially at night time. Is this normal for all type 1s? 

I have a rarer type, (pancreatogenic diabetes or type 3c which occurs when you produce little or no insulin due to external damage to the pancreas as a result of a different condition rather then an autoimmune response). in my case I've been lucky and my endos want to take me off insulin (my last hbA1c was 5). title: My CGM and prick tests are a different.
post text:
I am currently using two Blood Glucose monitoring devices.

The CGM, A Sinocare iCan i3 has for the last few days, had my fasting levels at around 7 (after having a cookie), which is higher than normal for me.

  
However, my prick test, a GlucoRx Q consistently has my fasting levels at 5.2 to 5.6 and only goes above 6 after eating.

  
The CGM seems more sensitive and obviously polls more frequently, but the prick test with lancets was supplied to me by my GP.

  
Should I be worried about the difference? Is one more accurate than the other?

title: Travel
post text:
I need some advice. Since my diagnosis the one thing I’ve been concerned about is work travel. My first 3 day work event is coming up in less than a month, and I am wondering how everyone handles this. 

When I was diagnosed my A1C was 11, about a month and a half later it was down to 8.2. I’ve been making really good progress and I’ve made even more changes since then, and I’m hoping when they test my A1C next to be at or below 7. 

Back to my question, how does everyone handle travel? I’ll be at a work site for 3 or 4 days where our meals are catered or we have executive dinners at fancy restaurants. I’ll be staying in a hotel and I can bring some snacks with me, but how do I handle meal times? I don’t wanna be the only one saying no to restaurants or being questioned on why I’m not eating (this happened before my diagnosis when I was on ozempic and had no appetite.) To add a layer to this I also largely eat meat and dairy free, so my usual pasta go tos are now out the window, which is fine when I’m at home. I just feel like if I don’t think too much about it then I’ll make bad decisions and the habits I’ve been trying to build up over the last 3 months will fall apart. I’m going to have to do this every few months indefinitely, so what are your go tos when eating out or during travel?

title: Food choice wins
post text:
I was diagnosed 4-5ish months ago at 7.9, brought it down to 5.5 ish in 3 months with lifestyle change and medication. By far the hardest part of this for me is diet - I'm an emotional eater and a binge eater, so changing and keeping my diet under control has been hard. And I've had slip ups of course, especially this time of year - holidays are very stressful.

Anyhow, I had bought a case of Mtn. Dew for a work potluck and almost no one drank it, so now the case is sitting in my house until I can take it to my sisters. It's just sitting there taunting me. I want to cave and have one, but I truthfully can't imagine what that delicious green liquid would do to my sugars. But I knew that if I didn't get a "taste" of soda, the craving would get worse and I'd eventually go nuts with it. So I just got a coke zero from the work fridge and idk I'm so proud. I didn't cave and have something very bad but I fed my craving enough that it went away. I also resisted the banana bread and no-bake cookies at the checkout :)

title: Fingerstick false high reading?
post text:
Today I had an issue with my new sensor (Libre 2) - it wouldn't start and I had to place a new one. I did so slightly above where my previous one was (after cleaning the site). As soon as it started working it set off the low bg alarm. I checked with a fingerstick to confirm. Libre was 68 fingerstick was 153. imo big difference. I wasn't feeling unwell so I assumed the fingerstick was correct.

Bolused for dinner as usual, ate and about 3 hours later checked sensor readings (which was in the range of 100-80 trending slightly, but trending noticeably downwards). Fingerstick said 140.

The issue is, soon after I get a CGM reading of 53. Probably a lower-than-actual reading, but I did start feeling unwell. Did a fingerstick to confirm and it said 128 (which wasn't super easy, considering my hands were shaking and sweating and I was feeling the typical mix of anxiety and confusion I do when I go low).

My question is this: Have you ever had a false high reading on a fingerstick? And is there anything that could cause this. I couldn't find much info on-line.

I use Accu Chek Instant, make sure to clean my finger with alcohol prep pads and dry it before sticking my finger. I used this batch of test strips for a couple of weeks (I don't check very often), but even yesterday - with the old sensor - they seemed to be accurate (almost 1:1)

EDIT: It seems like the issue was with the sensor. It died 4 hours after application.
label: no stigma

title: 3 months after first diagnosis
post text:
A bit of an update from my [last 

Was first diagnosed 3 months ago during a medical check up at work, bs was 23-24 mmol/l.

Got sent to a diabetologist, diagnosed with t2, from my previous bloodworks they found out that I already had it in 2022 but was never diagnosed with it before.

Started 500mg metformin twice a day and insulin (12 in the morning and 18 in the evening), then after a month no more insulin just 750gr metformin twice a day.



My bloodwork on 10.07. was like this:

*fructosamine* **441 umol/l**

*hemoglobin A1c* **11,7%**

*IFCC hemoglobin A1c* **104 mmol/mol**

*average calculated bloodsugar level* **16,01 mmol/l**



Bloodwork from yesterday:

*fructosamine* **209 umol/l**

*hemoglobin A1c* **5,5%**

*IFCC hemoglobin A1c* **37 mmol/mol**

*average calculated bloodsugar level* **6,16 mmol/l**



Got some cakes during christmas and was expecting the average calculated bs to be around 7, but at first glance this numbers seem ok to me.

Cut out sugar completely (except for 1 daily mini sweet) and no more than 1 slice of bread per day (it was 2 until last week).

Will talk about them with my doctor next wednesday.

Still find my morning bs before breakfast a bit high (around 6,5), but the doctor told me in december that we'll deal with that later on.

Stopped measuring my bs regularly for the past month and a half.

Loosing weight is still a bit of a challenge, and not always do I have time for a daily workout while being with my two kids.

So now I guess I just have to keep up with with I have been doing in the past 3 months for the rest of my life?

After my numbers go down does it ever change somehow in any way?

title: Premier Protein Pudding!
post text:
I know I’ve commented on other posts about this, but wanted to start a post in case others have flavor combinations or add-ins to suggest! 

If you want a tasty snack/dessert with protein, mix one bottle of Premier Protein shake with a box of sugar-free Jello instant pudding mix plus a half-cup of milk. (I top it with a couple Tbsp of Cool Whip.)

All of the flavor combos I've done come out to roughly the same nutritional values per 1/2 cup serving: 80 cal, 7.5 carbs, 1.5 fat, 8.5 protein. 

Note: That’s just for the pudding, with 2% milk. The Cool Whip adds another gram of carbs per Tbsp. 

IT IS SO GOOD. 

I have done the following combos: 
* pistachio pudding + vanilla shake + cinnamon
* butterscotch pudding + caramel shake + Penzey’s cake spice
* banana pudding + banana shake
* chocolate fudge pudding + chocolate shake
* white chocolate pudding + white chocolate raspberry shake
* chocolate pudding + café latte shake + cinnamon

01/14/25: Added more combos to keep them all in one place. I used 1% milk for these, but the macros come out to be pretty much the same as noted above. 

Any ideas for other combos? Low carb add-ins?

title: Eversense 365 14 days post insertion
post text:
14 days post insertion and things are going relatively well.

Trying out my 3rd attachment method.  An Etsy seller has this band for the Eversense 365.  I just put it on and it’s reasonably comfortable.  I don’t think I would wear this if I was doing something like yard work because it covers the reader but doesn’t snap in.  Still afraid I might lose it.

I’ve now started the once a week calibration step, so at least one blood stick a week for the next 50 weeks. My largest calibration difference was 12 and my smallest was 2.  If this keeps up, I can use my CGM for dosing decisions which I couldn’t do with my Libre 2.

I’ve also set up the prediction mode which tells me that I will probably go low or high in the next 20 minutes.

Slight issue with the app losing the reader.  This has happened twice but plugging the the reader into the power supply fixes it.

Have a great day!

title: Good going to- and in-bed food?
post text:
I've had a couple of times when my CGM warned me that my blood sugar was low when I was in bed and it was too early to get up. The first time this happened, I fortunately had the kit I have for taking with me when I go out nearby, so I got up, grabbed it, and got back into bed. Then, the other day, I noticed before going to bed that my blood sugar was a little low. I didn't want to spend any time making something, so I grabbed the last two pieces of bread in a loaf and ate halfway through them. Then I tucked the rest of it next to me in bed. I did this because I figured that preventing it from drying out was worth a few crumbs. Then, when the CGM alarm went off in the morning, I finished the bread and it did the trick.

So my questions are:

1. Is there a good food to eat just before bed if I notice my blood sugar is a little low and I want to stay in range through the night?
2. Is there a food I should have within arm's reach if my blood sugar is low in the morning and I'm not ready to get up and have breakfast?

title: Longtime Lantus user...
post text:
I have a Tresiba question. Lantus user for about 10 years. It's been pretty much rock solid from one day to the next, unless I went far above normal with my activity level. 
 
 Not so with Tresiba. I've used it for about a month, and have had several days where I ran low (<70), once rather persistently. Granted these instances were days that I was more active than normal, but it seems like Lantus has more stable glucose levels. 
 
 Tresiba maintains glucose quite steady overnight, ~90 - 105 mg/dl. I'm supposing that's due to the long half life compared to Lantus' 18ish hour half life. 
 
 So, is my short experience with Tresiba normal? I'll keep using it, as it should enable me to have my A1C to be below 5.4% next cycle.

title: Sugar levels after Flu A
post text:
T2 diabetic that's normally well controlled here with insulin and a Dexcom G7. 
 
 My wife & I had the type A Flu at the end of January and it's been about 3 weeks since we recovered. She took antibiotics and I didn't, symptoms and length of time sick were about the same for both of us. 
 
 Our problem is that *since then*, we can't keep our sugars down. Today is typical of the last 3 weeks. 110 when I woke up, I've eaten nothing and I climbed up to 196. Took enough fast-acting to bring me to 0 about an hour ago. Still haven't eaten and I'm now 205. 
 
 It's like this every day for both of us since being sick. No changes to foods, drinks, meds, routines, etc. Just a clear change since having the flu. 
 
 Thoughts? 
 
 [Edit] I should add that taking a 2nd dose of insulin does bring it down. I can also wait and not eat for around 4 hours and it will come down some but not as much as it should. It's like we both now need 2x-4x our normal amount of insulin.

title: I Think I'm Addicted To Eating Sweets
post text: I (21 y/o F) had bloodwork done in **January** 2024 that showed that my **A1C was 5.9**. The doctor told me I was pre-diabetic and that I should watch my diet and work out more. I was already very active and went to the gym about 5 times a week almost every week . Hearing that I was **pre-diabetic** scared me and I started having trouble eating. I didn't want my A1C to rise and I didn't know what foods to eat to stop that from happening so I decided the best thing to do was to stop eating entirely. Sometimes, after I did forced myself to eat, I would make myself throw it up as it started to give me *anxiety* and I began to overthink the food that had just entered my body.I went from 150lbs to 122 in 2 months. I got my bloodwork done again in **March** 2024 and my **A1C had gone down to 5.8**. I decided to try Factor meals pre-made food delivery service, as I am not a chef and didn't know what to cook myself, this felt like a safe healthy option. I also cut out rice and pasta from my diet completely, I only allowed myself one sweet treat a day, tried not to eat too much bread, and stopped making myself vomit after eating. Everything was going okay, I was eating the Factor meals and going to the gym regularly, until Summer came around. I got ringworm from the gym and it spread all over my chest, my back, my stomach, my thighs, and my biceps. I was appalled by my body and was scared to go outside or work out at all, in fear I would start to sweat and cause the ringworm to worsen or spread. The ringworm took the entire 3 months of the Summer to go away, ruined my gym progress, and discouraged my cleaner eating habits. In **October 2024 my levels were 5.7** so I guess progress had still been made. However, after that reading in October, I think my brain convinced itself that I'm fine now, even though I still am pre-diabetic, and I have reverted to my old eating habits and still haven't gone back to the gym. I still don't eat rice or pasta, but I have sweets pretty regularly, they're all I crave. I have about 3-4 sweet treats daily and definitely not enough of an other foods. I am terrified that my levels have spiked in the past two months since my last reading, but I can't bring myself to stop eating sweets or go back to the gym. I need advice badly. I also have just recently been diagnosed with *ADHD* and *high* *anxiety* as well, so I believe I'm stuck in a repetitive pattern and can't get out of the cycle of doing these same things daily as it become like a schedule. I NEED to get a donut from Dunkin for breakfast every. single. morning. I can't stop myself even though I know it's bad for me. I feel guilty afterwords and beat myself up about it, but a few hours later I find myself just having another sweet treat again and again and again. I should be getting my bloodwork done again in January 2025 and I am absolutely terrified to the results to come. I'm freaking out.
label: yes stigma

title: One more Diabetes success post
post text:
Has been a long 2 years since being diagnosed but i have been able to keep my last 3 A1c levels at 5.2-5.3 with lifestyle changes and coming off of insulin.
 
 I won't go as far as saying i am in remission just because my BG will still spike high if i eat the wrong foods or over eat.
 
 I don't think it's just one thing i did to control the DM well but, is multiple approaches that seems to work for me.
 
 CGM has been a game changer for sure as i can see how i responded to foods and have made the right adjustments in my diet and has worked. I did also lose a last stubborn 10 lbs and feel better with the weight loss.
 
 I also changed jobs at work an no longer sit on my butt in a cube for 8-12 hours a day. Be surprised how getting active helps.
 
 Hopefully this encourages other folks to keep after it controlling their diabetes.

title: Omad improved my t2 (for time being)
post text:
This new year i finally decided to take charge and shifted to one meal a day. 

I am not tracking my calories . Just take care not to eat too much carb . Focus is on foods which provide fat and protein. I workout every other day and walk half hour.

Results:-

Before
(2 hr after meal / fasted)
300 / 250
