title: Bun/creatinine ratio question
post text:
Tl;dr: started thyroid meds (methimazole) 1.5 years ago and ever since creatinine is creeping up and bun is creeping down, to point where ratio now is lower than it should be, even though both values are well within normal range still. Also, cholesterol almost doubling with no change to diet, weight, or activity. Any clues?

Long version: 
T1 for looong time (33+ years). Had quite a few years of not the best control but a1c has been 6.2-6.7 (usually 6.2-6.4) with 80%+ TIR since I got cgm 4 years ago.

My bun:creatinine ratio was always normal, with creatinine between 0.66 and 0.85. I had trace protein in urine few times, but its usually one test and then next several years all tests are clear. 

My cholesterol was also always in normal range (120-145 usually)

After I had covid in early 2023, I got thyroiditis that required methimazole treatment. My hormones went to normal within 3mos but I have some antibodies so my endo insists on keeping me on minimal dose of methimazole until those are gone (they are steadily dropping)

However ever since after 6mos on it or so, without ANY changes in my diet or activity, my BUN has been slowly dropping, my creatinine increasing and my cholesterol went from 124 to 204. My metabolism is slowed down some 200-300cal a day as well (I eat bit less so after initial 2-3lbs no more weight was gained). 

My bun:creatinine ratio in 2023 was 17 (bun 11.2, creatinine 0.66), then early 2024 was 13 (bun 10.4 creatinine 0.85), then mid 2024 11.6 (bun 10.4 creatinine 0.89) and then on last test a month ago its 8.6 (bun 8.1 creatinine 0.94). Egfr is not standard test here but I will ask for it next time. Albumin and creatinine in urine are also not standard test but will ask. My doctor didn't say anything regarding this, I was looking something else up and found that my bun:creatinine now is too low.

Increasing creatinine could indicate kidney function decline but... in diabetic kidney disease bun also usually increases rather than decreases so its bit confusing as mine is going down. My liver function is great, and I eat adequate protein.

I am gonna find a nephrologist to talk with, but does anyone have any clue what could be going on? As my bun and creatinine by themselves are in normal ranges (just ratio is too low) I KNOW I will be brushed off unless I come with very pointed questions demanding answers. Unfortunately I have to be my own doctor a lot and know things before pointing them out, and I am at complete loss with this so I am asking for help if anyone could provide any :)

title: A year after being diagnosed!
post text:
Had my physical today and I’m so happy! A year ago, I was diagnosed with diabetes and my life was shaken up! I was pre-diabetic for the longest time and didn’t really care. But when I got my test results back last year, I thought the world was ending! My A1C was 10.8 and I had no idea what to do and how to manage it. Luckily through my doctor and my nutritionist (and the great people on subreddits like this one) I was able to control and manage my diabetes! There were a lot of ups and downs this past year but I’m definitely doing a lot better than last year. 

Overall, I’ve been eating healthier, lost over 40 lbs, and my a1c is now at 5.1!!! Still working to lower it but I’m so happy with the success from the past year! 

Truly, when I was diagnosed, I was so depressed and couldn’t see things getting better. A year later and I’ve grown so much! And I’m so much healthier than I was! I still have a long ways to go, but I’m very proud of the steps I’ve taken this year and just wanted to share my happiness!

title: Critical folks with diabetes
post text:
I posted earlier today about being 4.7 and feeling terribly low, I had some really lovely people get back to me and thanks for that! 

A lot of comments were pretty critical about why I’d “feel terrible” at 4.7- I am aware 4.7 is not technically low. I’ve had diabetes for almost 12 years now, but that doesn’t change the fear, being home alone, having an extensive history of seizures, running out of low supplies, just generally being an anxious person. I just reached out for some comfort, some support, just wondering if I’m completely alone in sometimes living with fear.

I appreciate that sometimes with anxiety, being met with some factual and objective thinking can actually be helpful, but quite a few replies kind of just came across as heartless.

It’s been 12 years, but I still struggle, so much of the time. I still find it so difficult, so scary, so depressing. One of the main comforts, I think, is speaking to someone else that also deals with the same situation. Please, just be kind, be gentle- in one way or another we are all in the same shite boat

title: Pregnancy and T2
post text:
Hi T2 community. 

I guess I am looking for some hope. 
I have been TTC via IVF for the past three years. I was diagnosed with T2 in my journey and I have been pregnant twice to which the first was a MMC at 9 weeks. That hit me pretty hard and 6 month s later was pregnant and had a chemical pregnancy. 

Before anyone judges, my infertility is “unknown” I’ve been to every specialist known to humans and I do have an endocrinologist who helps me monitor my T2. 

I kind of feel broken. No matter how hard I work on ensuring my sugars are in check, the doctors always blame my T2. I wasn’t getting pregnant when I didn’t have T2 and i managed to get pregnant when I had it. 

I’m planning to use an insulin pump to help control it even more but I feel helpless. 

Never in my life have I been treated like a subhuman saying my T2 is why I’m infertile and as though I am not deserving to be a mother. I get treated as if I’m some sloth, just eating garbage all day when I’m eating more cleaner than I was when I was fit. 

So I guess I am looking for hope in that there are other people out there in the world who are T2 and conceived naturally or via IVF. 

Please don’t judge me - I just feel absolutely gutted and like a POS… when I’ve been trying so hard and as we go into a new year I am still childless and I feel like it’s my own fault and the doctors always say that  it’s my T2. I can only be on insulin whilst doing IVF and that’s made me put on weight and it’s hard to drop off as well. 

I just want to hear those that conceived, what did you do differently, what was your HBA1C … I am just so lost and upset I can’t help but be hard on myself and blame myself. 

Thank you for listening.

title: Apple Cider Vinegar? I’m in shock
post text:
Ok I was told to drink apple cider vinegar before meals and I did it today and I haven’t had a high all day. AND I ATE POTATOES. 

Normally potatoes would send me well over 250. 

I was 136 after I ate potatoes for lunch. That’s a mind bogglingly low number for a post carb heavy meal. 

Has anyone else had this effect? 

UPDATE: It’s still too early to really know if it is working but this is what I found so far. 

Last night I did NOT drink the ACV and I ate a bowl of ramen and a slice of bread. Blood sugar rapidly spiked to 300. 

This morning I drank it again and had no real spike after breakfast. It only went up to 160. That was not a carb heavy meal but it had carbs. 

For lunch I had two grilled cheese sandwiches with whole wheat bread (for the sake of science of course ). 

I again drank the ACV. My blood sugar topped out at 190. I am aware that is high but four slices of whole wheat bread would spike me well over 300 normally - so I will take it. Also I wouldn’t normally eat two sandwiches but I did it this time because I was pretty hungry and I wanted to really test the ACV out again. So I assume if I ate one it would be a lower spike. 

How much?

I used 1 tablespoon of Bragg’s Apple Cider Vinegar diluted with half a glass of water (I’m guessing that would be about 1 cup if I measured it). 

I added Splenda so I could tolerate the vinegar. Normally I use stevia as my sweetener of choice but Splenda is all I have right now. 

I chugged it and ate immediately after. 

I’m not going to say this works but it appears to help a lot.

title: Tandem Mobi Insulin Pump Malfunction: A Dangerous Issue That Needs Attention
post text:
If you’re a Tandem Mobi insulin pump user, you need to be aware of a serious and potentially life-threatening malfunction that can leave you stranded without insulin. This is not just a minor glitch—it’s a critical failure that could put lives at risk.

The Malfunction: What Happens?

For seemingly no apparent reason, the pump will begin beeping and vibrating every five minutes, alerting you that something is wrong. The lights on the pump will flash red continuously or possibly stay solid, indicating a total system failure. When this happens:
   •   All insulin delivery stops immediately—both basal insulin and bolus doses cannot be administered.
   •   You cannot silence the alarms or dismiss the error.
   •   The pump will not restart without specific steps that require equipment you likely don’t have on you while away from home.

This means that if you are out for the day, at work, traveling, or even just a few hours from home without backup insulin, you’re in serious trouble. High blood sugar, diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), and hospitalization become real risks.

Restarting the Pump: A Frustrating Process

The only way to fix this issue is to:
        1.        Place the pump on its charging pad.
        2.        Hold the Quick Bolus button for about 30 seconds until it turns off.
        3.        Ensure the pump has a proper connection with the charger—it will vibrate slightly when aligned correctly.
        4.        Wait 1-2 minutes for it to restart.

But here’s the problem: Who carries a charging pad with them everywhere? When you’re out for the day, you don’t expect your life-saving medical device to completely shut down, requiring a bulky charger to restart. Tandem’s response? Just carry a backup insulin plan.

Tandem’s Response: Do They Even Care?

When reporting this issue to Tandem Diabetes Care, their response was shockingly dismissive. Their policy states:
   •   If the malfunction happens three times, they will replace the pump.
   •   However, the replacement is likely a refurbished unit, which could have its own issues.
   •   Customer service representatives offer no real solution beyond telling users to carry backup insulin.

This should not be happening with a life-sustaining medical device. The entire purpose of an insulin pump is to reduce the burden of diabetes management—not to leave users stranded without insulin in a dangerous                     medical emergency.

A Widespread Issue?

It’s unclear how many people are experiencing these failures, but reports are appearing across online communities. Reddit, diabetes forums, and social media are full of users encountering similar issues, receiving the same inadequate response from Tandem. If this is happening to you, it’s critical to report it so that regulators and advocacy groups take action.

What Can Be Done?

If you experience this issue, here’s what you should do:
        1.        Report the malfunction to Tandem—even if they don’t seem to care, having records of complaints matters.
        2.        File a complaint with the FDA’s MedWatch program (FDA MedWatch)—this is how device failures get officially documented.
        3.        Contact diabetes advocacy groups like JDRF, the American Diabetes Association, or Beyond Type 1 to raise awareness.
        4.        Warn others in the diabetes community about this issue so they can be prepared.
        5.        Consider switching to a different insulin pump brand—companies with better reliability and customer service exist.

Final Thoughts: This Should NOT Be Happening

Tandem’s insulin pumps are marketed as advanced, reliable medical devices, yet they are failing at a basic level—providing uninterrupted insulin delivery. When failure occurs, users are left without immediate solutions and a customer service team that downplays the seriousness of the issue.

If you rely on a Tandem Mobi insulin pump, be prepared. Bring a backup insulin plan, carry a charging pad if possible, and document any malfunctions. Most importantly, demand better from Tandem and the FDA—because no one with diabetes should have to fight for their safety against the very devices meant to keep them alive.

title: Please excuse my self indulgence
post text:
In September, I 56M was diagnosed with Type 2 DM. It wasn’t a surprise: my weight was at its all time max, I had been measuring as pre-diabetic, and I was eating about as poorly as I could. I had been avoiding blood work for 2 years. I weighed 230 pounds, my A1c came back at 7.9% with fasting glucose at 242 mg/dL. My liver enzymes and cholesterol were out of bounds.

So, I took it as a wake up call. In the last 3 months, I’ve been eating low carb with calorie restriction and I’ve been taking 500 mg Metformin ER once a day and 0.5 mg Ozempic per week. I do eat a ‘normal’ meal every now and then, like Thanksgiving, but my portion size is much smaller. I’ll also occasionally have a treat like one cookie (instead of a dozen cookies). I also have been wearing a CGM because I am a data nerd.

I’ve lost 52 pounds, and I just got my lab results back. My fasting glucose was 87 mg/dL and A1c was 5.2%. The cholesterol and liver values were back in line. So, I’ve appreciated this sub and I just wanted to share.

title: Taking Ozempic and easing off insulin
post text:
I was diagnosed in 2016 and managed with metformin (and some dietary changes) for a few years, but then it started wearing off. My doctor increased the dose, then prescribed glipizide, but my a1c kept going up. Last summer, it hit 12. I asked about Ozempic and was told that CareOregon wouldn't pay for it until I got my a1c below 10. At this point I had a different primary care physician, and he prescribed insulin and referred me to a pair of nurse managers who have been very helpful. They got me a G7 CGM and it's been teaching me what I need to do to keep my blood sugar low.
 
 Meanwhile, under the nurses' guidance, I gradually increased my insulin to 50u/day, which seemed like the right level to keep me from going over 180 too much. Finally, in December, my a1c was tested and it was 8.5. Soon after that I began taking Ozempic, 0.25mg each week for 4 weeks, and I just had my second 0.5mg dose a few days ago. I started leveling down the insulin last week and I'm down to 42u now. 
 
 The problem is that the Ozempic has really kicked in and has lowered my appetite, which is what I was hoping for. However, the insulin is still working too well and is lowering my BG at times when I don't want to eat. I have juice pouches which help in the short term, and I've ordered glucose tablets which should arrive in two days. 
 
 Does anyone have any advice for dealing with this transitional period?

title: Omeprazole raising my fasting BS, doc doesn't believe me.
post text:
I have GERD and have been on famotidine for years with no issue for management, recently I had a reoccurrence that caused another endoscopy.. same finding.. mild esophagitis.  Dr. Put me on Omeprazole which the last years ago did.. and it didn't work well, actually made my nighttime heartburn worst.

Well I was recently diagnosed with T2 about 2 years ago and now have a CGM..

My A1c very recently was 6.1 , after starting the Omep (OTC 20 mg) it came back 6.3, and the increasing to 40 mg A1c was 6.5.. now they have me on prescription 40mg XR twice a day and my fasting BS has gone from  95-110 to 150/160!!! i can't eat eggs and sausage without my BS hitting 190/200.

I asked my primary to taper me down and find an alternative and they said to talk to the Gastro, the gastro said " He has never heard of this happening and it's probably other factors".. I told him I have the graphs with timelines which line up to show it!. I have been working out more (nothing too intense) I have been limiting carbs more, and my readings are always 150/160.

I decided to test it myself and lower my morning dose from 50 XR to the OTC 20 mg... and my BS went down approx. 12 pts that morning, same thing the next morning.. in fact I saw it go below 130 for the first time in over a week.

Please, has anyone else had a similar experience.. I feel like I am going crazy. I don't have anyone else to talk to or ask questions about this. My primary is pretty hands off on my diabetes, she actually advised against the CGM saying "As long as your sugar isn't in the 300's and your A1c stays below 7.0 you are fine" I am not even 40 man, I don't want issues down the line.

EDIT:   
 

To be clear..

I eat low carb, both simple and complex.

I am on 2000mg of metformin .

I don't eat sugar, I don't do soda, I don't do bread (sometimes keto pitas).  
I don't do pasta, I eat a few small red potatoes from time to time. 

I exercise, eat lots of veg and protein, low sugar fruits, even do unsweetened soy milk for protein shakes to not get the sugar hit from milk.

The highest carb thing I eat is a low carb ice cream (4 net carbs), small portion from time to time and keto cereal with unsweetened soy milk. 

My daily carb in take is prob 50 ( including veg)

title: I want to give up
post text:
I was diagnosed 5 years ago. In that time, I tried really hard to get healthy. I eat healthy, exercise, everything they tell you to do. I was on Metformin up until a couple of months ago. It always made me sick, so I only took it when I knew I wasn't going anywhere that day. I told my doctor, but he never suggested anything else. The last time my A1C was 9.4, it had gone up from 8.4. 
I asked about ozempic, and he said I was a good candidate. I had insurance, and it would help cover it. Perfect! I started to lose weight, my sugar levels seemed to come down, and my cravings were almost gone. I was eating less. It was great. Then I lost my insurance, and now I can't afford it anymore, so I stopped it. Now my body is out of whack again.
My doctor prescribed Jardience. But I need to find money to pay for it. 
I am so sick of this. Even things I considered healthy aren't. I am sick of eggs, and eggs and oatmeal and cream of wheat were all I can stomach in the morning.
I try to mix things up for lunch and dinner, but unfortunately, I have a very simple pallet, and I hate fish and most veggies.
I know we need to eat well-balanced meals, and for the most part, I do. But a lot of these healthy foods are expensive, especially for a single person. Frozen meals are cheaper. I have thrown so much food away because I can't get through it fast enough before it spoils. I do freeze some things, but fresh produce is hard to keep. I try to buy minimally, but I still don't get through it fast enough.
I'm tired of trying, yet I know the ramifications if I don't eat healthy, etc.
When is enough enough? I'm finding my quality of life is being stomped out by trying to add to the quantity of life. This is not how I want to live, and I have tried so hard.
Add all that and how certain foods are good, and ones you always thought were healthy, aren't. I was told to eat every 2 hours to keep sugar levels stable, then told 4. Then I was told intermittent fasting is good, but if you do that, sugar levels spike. It's never ending.
Losing my leg or my life scares me, too. I am seeing a  counsellor, but I'm still frustrated.
Sorry this is such a downer, I just needed to get it out.

title: MDI - logging doses
post text:
EDIT:  Sorry I wasn’t clear. I should have specified that this question was geared toward people that have been doing MDI for a long time. Just curious if anyone ever stops logging. That’s all. 

For those of you on MDI, do you log all your insulin doses?

I was only diagnosed last year at age 46 and I’m still in my honeymoon period.  I log every insulin dose I take in my Dexcom app. It helps me keep track of WHEN I gave a dose and my Endo can also see all my data.  I also use my data to track how my insulin needs are increasing over time. 

However, I can’t imagine having to do this forever (if I stay MDI).  For now, it’s still helpful but I can see a time where I don’t log everything and just kind of chug along through life.

title: Traveling to Thailand next week
post text:
Was diagnosed with t2 a few months ago when a full blood panel revealed that my hb1ac was 7 (granted I was eating cake daily that month) and I’ve been trying to eat clean and incorporate more physical activity since December, and I’m now down 10kgs.

I currently wear a CGM (a Dex7) and on 500mg metformin twice a day (up from once a day from the first 2 weeks). My worst spikes were when I ate spicy chili noodles highest spike was 7.8 mmol/150mgdl- it was just meat and noodles with chili and the day after I ate 10 pieces of pork chive dumplings where I peaked at 7.9mmol/140 mgdl. Granted that weekend after that, my blood sugar was still hovering between 5.3mmol/95 mgdl to 6.3mmol /113 mgdl until Monday noon despite me eating those during the weekend. Weirdly flavored fries only spiked me at 6.5 mmol/117mgdl.

Ever since my diagnosis, my main eating had been veggies (lettuce, aragula, spinach) + protein (meat fish tofu) + dairy (feta cheese, Greek yogurt) + fats (olive/avocado oil, the occasional avocado) then usually just tossing it up in a bowl. I had two or three instances where I had some grapes or brown rice. Brown rice doesn’t spike me but I realize I stay above 5.0 mmol (90-100 mgdl) for a longer time when I have it.


Now my main worry is that I will be going to Thailand for 2 weeks and that country isn’t known for being the most diabetic friendly: rice, sauces, curries, noodles, rice deserts. Even the protein stuff like pad krapao (holy basil pork/chicken) or Kai Yang (grilled chicken) might have hidden sugars in the seasonings or the marinade. I am also traveling with friends so I don’t want to really impose on them as we are surely eating out a lot. One good option to eat is Khao mun gai without the rice (it’s like hainanese chicken rice) but I obviously can’t eat that everyday. 

Anyone who can give me tips??? I really want to enjoy this trip.
label: no stigma

title: Feeling unsupported in diabetes care
post text:
This is more so a vent/ rant post more then anything. I was diagnosed type 2 diabetic in Nov '23. When I diagnosed my A1C was 6.8 and I was prescribed metformin er 1 pill a day in the morning. I went back 3 months later and checked my A1C and had went down to 6.0. Fast forward 1 year and December '24 I went for my annual physical with my check on my A1C between March '23 and Dec '23 and my A1C has went up to 8.1. During this entire time my doctor has never told me anything about needing to check my blood sugar or prescribed me a lot to check my blood sugar and I was basically told I only needed to take the pill and nothing else. I was never given any pamphlet for diabetes management or recommended to go to any diabetes education classes. I also live with my Mom who is not diabetic and does not have to take any prescription meds and is skinny. No one on her side of the family is diabetic but almost everyone on my dad's side of the family is and I got my health from my dad's side. My Mom loves to constantly remind me how lucky she is that she doesn't have to take any meds by saying little things like "I'm in my 50s and don't have to take any prescriptions and I can eat whatever I want." I also am the one who buys all of the groceries and I never have had much of a sweet tooth even before being diagnosed or gaining weight but I spend roughly $150 on groceries for our house and my Mom also buys stuff for herself to eat such as Kool aids and donuts and soda and constantly bakes. She doesn't want to eat healthier even trying to fix frozen vegetables with our meals made her complain that she had to change her eating habits which I never asked her to do and since incorporating more vegetables she does not eat them. I don't have the money to buy groceries that are healthy for me and also buy groceries that she will eat. If I did that it would basically double my grocery budget since she refuses to eat healthier foods and does not buy any food besides snacks for herself and also does not do any cooking unless it's baking. 

Long story short I don't feel like I'm being supported by my family or by my doctor and I'm really at a loss for what to do. Any advice would be appreciated and thanks for listening to my long rant.
label: yes stigma

title: In Need of Guidance!
post text:
Hello all. 6 months ago my a1c was at 6.5. Now its jumped up to 10. A year ago my c peptide was at 1.6 now it’s at 0.9. In the past 6 months my medication intake has remained consistent and my low carb diet has remained the same. The only difference was reintroducing “keto” or “sugar free” products into my diet recently after stopping them last summer because a sugar free protein drink and a keto tortilla spiked me. Other than those 2 instances I never had an issue with keto or sugar free products spiking me so I just thought why not live a little and reintroduce these products (except for the 2 that spiked me). I hadn’t been checking my sugars in the past 6 months so I have no way of knowing if anything in my diet during that span of time has been spiking me. I stopped exercising altogether except for about 3 weeks in November. I have been anxious and stressed as well recently but idk. I’ve never been a believer in those two affecting my sugars since I’ve been stressed and anxious all my life. I honestly thought my low carb diet and meds alone would keep my a1c below 7.

I’m supposed to be discussing my test results and treatment options Monday with my Endo who left this vague message: the drop in c peptide may be partly why my a1c has drastically increased. What else could be it be? My lack of exercise? Not monitoring my sugars to test what my body reacts well to or doesn’t even though I’m fairly paranoid about my low carb diet and monitor ingredients closely? I’m honestly confused by current experience. I’ve had diabetes for 5 years now maybe i still don’t know as much as I think I do or underestimate it? Is there something else wrong with me? 

I want to have a calm conversation on Monday and instead of just ranting questions like I did above. I would appreciate if anyone could share similar stories or give some advice. What questions should I ask my Endo and what should I ask for?

title: Jardiance? Farxiga?
post text:
When I was diagnosed three months ago, I was put on 16 units of insulin (Tresiba) since my a1c was super high. I'm now down to 8 units, and since my latest a1c test was much lower (5.6) the doctor offered to take me off insulin and suggested jardiance or farxiga.

 I was pretty sure I didn't want to make the switch, but now that I'm looking into it more, it looks like the Fournier's side effect is extremely rare. Like, 55 cases in six years or something.

title: T2- is it lifestyle or genetics?
post text:
Is there a way to tell?
1.5 yrs ago I was A1C 6.4
I already ate well. Little to no fast food. Didn't drink alcohol or pop. Low end of normal BMI. Active lifestyle but not fit.
Taking thyroxine for hypothyroidism. 

Decided to make changes instead of taking meds. Thought I would try to "reverse" the t2. Implemented intermittent fasting to one meal a day. Ate meat and salads a lot. Drastically cut out carbs. Lost 25 lbs (now a size 4 at 5'5" tall). Play pickleball 3-5 x/week. Long walks with the dog. Retired so life isn't very stressful. I paint for enjoyment. 

My 1st repeat A1C was 6.2 (disappointing). My next was 5.8. My latest, though, was 6.1. 

I'm still very disappointed with the results. I wore a CGM and aimed to keep my levels at first under 10 then under 8. I was in my target zone well over 90% of the time and rarely over 11.

What am I missing here? Although my family doctor is satisfied with the results, I am very disappointed and confused. 

Can anyone offer insight...? 
All I can think of is that my case is genetics related and my ability to control this is therefore limited. I am so jealous hearing about people who are not on meds yet get A1C results in the low 5's and even in the 4 range.

title: 55 year old diagnosed with type 2 a month ago
post text:
Hey everyone, wanted to share my progress since diagnosis about a month ago. Stats:

Starting numbers:

* A1C: 9.7
* Fasting glucose: 180
* Weight: 232 (was 244 in May)
* Height: 6'5"
* Discussed with doctor and decided no medication

Current numbers after 1 month:

* Fasting glucose: typically between 110 and 120
* Weight: 220
* Can reach 94 during fasting
* Evening numbers usually 105-110

What I'm doing:

* Very low carb (<20g daily)
* Two 36-hour fasts per week
* Careful glucose monitoring
* Working with therapist for stress management

Notable observations:

* Can really feel when blood sugar rises (warmth in face, racing thoughts)
* Coffee raises glucose about 8 points for an hour
* Body handles extended fasts well (just hit 107 at 29 hours fasting)
* Had one reading of 98 before bed

Goals:

* Get fasting under 110 by January 6th follow-up
* Weight goal of 205 by March
* After 3 months strict keto, plan to test higher carb tolerance
* Eventually transition to one 36-hour fast/week plus 16:8 IF

Thank you to everyone here for the stories, ideas and inspiration! I appreciate you.

title: Newly diagnosed and I’m feeling things

Hi, so I 21f have officially been diagnosed.  The worst part is i can’t even call my family to tell them without ruining a big moment for my sister.  So I’m posting here instead of calling my mama crying.   Anyway so a couple weeks ago I had a doctors appointment to talk about some stuff.   She ran a GAD65 because the side effects of my wegovy are in fact not side effects.    I have three out of the five antibodies that the endocrinologist tested for.    So I mentioned the extreme hunger, fatigue and being crazy thirsty.  Also that I was living in like a haze for a while.   

Well it turns out I am a type one diabetic just in the honeymoon phase.   Which I wish it would end.  I am tired of feeling like crap.  No matter what I eat, I’m nauseated, and exhausted.  I also can’t shake the headaches.   Oh and to top it off I crave sugar like all I want to eat is sugar.    My endo is waiting to put me on insulin because my numbers are still okay for right now.  Whatever that means.  


I am freaking out right now.   I’m just so tired of feeling crappy, I kind of wish this would just end so I could figure out all the other stuff that comes along with being diabetic.    The ironic thing is I absolutely suck at math… like it’s bad and now I’m dependent on math to survive.  I’m laughing so I don’t cry.    Anyway I named my diabetes Jeff because why not.  

Did you name your diabetes?

title: My husband has diabetes type 1 and I can't bear it anymore
post text:
Hi,
 
 First post ever on reddit for me, I must admit I created an account especially to ask the reddit community for some advice. (sorry for potato English, it is my second language)
 
 My husband has had diabetes type 1 for 11 years now. We have been together 8 years and married 2 years. He always managed his diabetes rather okay, but since 10-8 months it is getting very difficult, especially at night. Nights were always a problem, he would have to wake up to take care of hypoglycemia, ruining his sleep and sometimes mine too. But globally, he would manage rather okay. But since 10-8 months, it is very difficult. A lot of hypoglycemia, a lot of hyperglycemia, a lot of sleep issues, a lot of eating issues. Evenings are most painful as he can't go to sleep until his glucose level is stable, which was easy before but hard as hell since 8-10 months. Nights are terrible as he has to wake up regularly to check his glycemia, nearly 3 times per night, waking me up and wrecking my sleep too. Mornings are painful too: as I check on him when he wakes up, he always tells me how bad his night went. We were both very tired, and it felt like it was never going to get better. So we asked for the automatic pump solution (we live in France, it is not easy to get one here).
 
 3 weeks ago, he finally got the automatic pump Omnipod 5 with sensor Dexcom in closed loop, which was supposed to solve everything and take over, especially at night. But something went wrong, he is saying that it doesn't work properly, that it makes mistakes and that he can't trust it. He is saying that the pump's code doesn't understand the fluctuation caused by his workout sessions and he is constantly in hyperglycemia. I am not sure I understood the problem properly. He switched back to "manual" mode on the onmipod 5 pump a week ago.
 
 Three days ago, when we were both working from home, he injected himself 10 times the required amount of insulin by mistake. It nearly killed him. I stayed by his side during the whole crisis, bringing sugary food to him, cuddling with him, trying to be supportive. I fear I have failed to be supportive in a way that truly helps him. I suspect that this "accident" was not a mistake. Our relationship has been difficult for 6 months, with a lot of tension around the new house we just bought. I fear this insulin injection "accident" was a suicide attempt, but I know he is quite sensible on this topic. So I didn't dare ask him directly. I tried to tell him that I love him, that I will always be here for him. But it is true that his diabetes issues are weighing on me. It is hard for me to see him suffer and to listen to him complain about it every day. I used to be able to hear his complaints, but now it just makes me cry or makes me wanna dissociate and ignore it. I don't want that. I love him, and I wanna support him, but I am clearly failing to do so. We had so much hope with the automatic Omnipod 5, but it seems to not be working. He is trying to get an opensource solution to replace the "faulty" Omnipod 5 code, but he told me it will take a long time. I fear I can't hold for long and I fear he can't either. I try to be supportive, but his issues are slowly killing me. I am so scared for him, so tired. I feel shitty for not being able to support him with his health issues. I go to therapy, but he doesn't want to go himself...
 
 Does someone have issues with the Omnipod 5 automatic closed loop system? What can I do to help my husband and preserve my own mental health?
label: yes stigma

title: Newly diagnosed- lowering A1c and remission questions.
post text:
Hi, I am a newly diagnosed T2. At the start of October my A1c was at 10.8, I recently had my number run again and I am down to 6.5. woohoo! 
I was on 500mg Metformin 2x a day(so 1000 mg total/day :one pill with breakfast and one with dinner) for the first 60 days. For the last 30 days, after visiting a specialist, I am on 1000mg Metformin 2x a day(so 2000mg total/day) and switched to the  extended release tablets  with the upped dosage. 

I have been working on lowering/being more mindful of my carb intake, eating more balanced meals, and increasing my exercise along with the medication.   I have always been active in sport and don't eat fast food so was quite surprised with this diagnosis, though looking at family genetics I should not have been. 

I am cautiously optimistic with my improvement thus far, but I am curious if I will be seeing a plateau  in my improvement  soon. When you were first diagnosed
and started to see improvement, did this hit a plateau early on? I know I need to worry about my complacency as my numbers improve and keep on top of things to keep seeing improvement and to keep that improvement in place. 

I am hoping for remission of my T2,  For those who have been able to do this what was your timeline? I know all bodies/medication/results will vary, but I am trying to understand the steps I need to take to accomplish this goal. Once A1c is stable at a "normal" level did you/can you lower your medication (in my case Metformin) intake?  My physician has mentioned perhaps combining the metformin with another medication if need be, but as of last appointment did not see a reason to. Should I question this further if it will help me reach my goal of remission?  

Cheers!
label: no stigma

title: Two years post diagnosis - 5.5% A1C without medication
post text:
Got diagnosed in Nov 2022 with an A1C of 8.8%. Brought it down to 5% with metformin. Stopped medication after consulting with my doctor. Since then A1C has gone up and down but stayed under 5.7% which has been my goal. 

Whats worked for me is just actively making healthier diet choices. I still splurge and overeat way more than i Should but I feel guilty about it atleast and try to recover. Also found out that weight training has been a game changer for me. 3-4 days a week with 45 min sessions has been enough. In the summer a single round of golf per week did the job as well. Also fasting is probably the best thing to control your diabetes. I fast 30 days in Ramadan and by the end I remember having 3 bowls of ramen at 2am and glucose didnt go above 180. Anyway I have realized this going to be a life long thing so have to do whatever is sustainable. One thing is for sure that this sub has been really helpful. Thank you all. 

See previous post:

title: T1D and Helicobacter pylori
post text:
I want to ask if anyone had something similar or is this a unique experience. So I m t1d for 15 years now, my hb1ac has always been between 7-8.5% and i always had what people call roller coasters. And I also can always sense my hypos and when I am in a hypo I have to eat until I feel like im not fainting or shaking anymore, so mostly more than what i shld eat and well this contributes further to the roller coaster. I should also note since i got diabetes i ve been consistently having 2 extra kilos each year. And i m pretty active but its like impossible to loose weight, i think this was also due to pcos and insulin resistance.

Ok thts a long intro, but basically this last 3 tears i have been having h pylori symptoms, but very sparsely distributed. So i didnt suspect it until My feritin went to 4 really low, and then one doctor was like ok maybe there is something in ur gut, and indeed I found h pylori. 

Now I took antibiotics, it was an aweful week, where i cldnt eat, i basically just had a but of protein shake with antibiotics. Now its 3 weeks after that treatment. MY SUGAR CONTROL HAS NEVER BEEN THIS GOOD, my insulin sensitivity is so good, and I have 0 craving for food in general, and Im never hungry, I literally eat just I know I should, also that weird hunger when im low went away, now I can definitely eat just a little bit. I should also note I noticed it doesn’t wake me anymore at night if i go low, like I can spend a whole night in 2.9 but it doesn’t go lower ( but maybe I should be concerned about this part), also my basal needs went down by 35% and im predicting it will go down more bcs im also loosing weight for the first time in forever at the rate of 1-2 kg per week. 

If someone experienced this, is it a temporary thing or it stays like this?

title: Diabetes at age 20
post text:
I’ve had bad eating habits since i was young and actually had insulin resistance symptoms since high school with always going to the bathroom, dark neck, thirsty, always hungry. I KNEW I HAD INSULIN RESISTANCE. It all started from the beginning of 2024, my nutritionist checked my a1c and it was 6.0 gave me a meal plan then i was dismissed. THEY NEVER TOLD ME IT WAS DANGEROUS AND COULD LEAD TO DIABETES?? 6 months later i was infact diagnosed with type 2 as my a1c was 9.2. I was shocked sobbing everyday. For the first 2-3 i didnt eat any carbs at all and was exercising/swimming often and my a1c dropped to 7.2 with also be prescribed glucophage 500mg twice a day. My weight didnt move tho (95 kilos). Went to the drs and told them i wanted monjaro and they gave it with jardiance (i refused to take it cuz my grandma and dad take it and didnt want to be like them always on meds). When i first took monjaro, for the first few weeks my sugar levels would drop to 70 after meals but adjusted a month later my body took monjaro so well and didnt really face any side effects. Fast forward to now checked my a1c DRUMROLL its 5.4! and i lost 10kg in the span of 6 months! Which was a bit discouraging cuz other ppl who used monjaro often lost way more weight but im kinda glad i did cuz even if i eat so bad for a week i would barely gain a kilo. My weight is 84-85 now and i just follow a low carb diet and try to consume protein as much as i can i still eat carbs/ order fast food but BUT not as much as i used to  (i used to eat fast food almost everyday and drink spanish lattes SO OFTEN). Honestly I think my fat percentage was the reason im diabetic as its 49% and im focusing on lowering it atm.

I have a couple of questions if anyone can help:
- Even though i lowered my a1c, my neck is still dark, how do i make it disappear
- how do i boost my weight loss/ fat loss, any specific diet plan that helped?
- I still face diabetes symptoms like blurry vision and go to the bathroom often, what do i do
- Advice that you wanted to know in the begging of your journey that can help me.
- (Stupid question) so i lowered my a1c am i still diabetic or insulin resistant
label: no stigma

title: Need to share with folks who understand!
post text:
Just got back results from my Doctor and in 3 months I went from an 10.8 to 5.0 a1c! 

It’s been really hard at times; especially with the holidays and everyone giving me sweets and treats. Even with a couple of days where I let myself enjoy some items that I used to consume all the time, it’s doable. 

I have to say having a cgm is probably one of the best things I’ve invested in when it comes to keeping the best track of my blood glucose levels. 

Thanks for letting me celebrate on here! There are few people in my life that understand how hard this disease can be. 

Edit: also, thank all of y’all for the support on this sub. It’s a great help and a community I needed!

title: Sweetest
post text:
I just wanted to say thanks to everyone who participated in the survey. We already have ~340 responses including my handful of type 1 friends! I tried to upvote every comment. 
The thing I can report immediately is that there are less penguins in this subreddit than I’d initially thought.
Unfortunately, today my miniature human was home sick from daycare. That’s what daycare is: it’s just a Petri dish you pay to infect your progeny, so you can’t send them back. And this kid is OBSESSED with me. He’s, like, my total STALKER. Taking a shower? He’s there. Putting in a pump site? He’s there and he’s HELPING. Going pee? Yep. HE’S THERE, eagerly waiting to flush the toilet for me. 
Anywhoozles, results coming soon! THANK YOU ALL SO MUCH! This has been so much fun and I’m actually really excited to see the fruits of our labors!
And it’s not too late to be a part of the MATHEMAGIC! I won’t be able to work on this over the weekend. (Damn miniature human! I keep telling him he’s not ENTITLED to my time OR my body.)

He just laughs and says, “Deedle leedle leedle! Dedo dedo dedo!” I blame the patriarchy. 

THANK YOU ALL AGAIN!!

title: Do doctors get kick-backs from certain companies?
post text:
Is it possible/likely that a doctor might push certain pharmaceuticals while discouraging others for any other reasons than good medical practice? It occurs to me that mine seems to really FAVOR the Ozempic line (Ozempic, Rybelsus). I tried Ozempic & HATED it -- if I ever try anything again, it would not be that -- but doc suggests Rybelsus (the pill form) over trying something slightly different like Mounjaro. I hate to think this way, but something seems amiss. 

Editing to add: I think that this thought came to me after I considered the recommendation 2 years ago that I do a ColoGuard test instead of colonoscopy, because "it takes so long to get an appointment". I'm 54. I started to wonder last night if there's a benefit \*to the doctor\* to recommend ColoGuard... and put that together with the recommendation of the pill form of Ozempic even though I had not tolerated the injection. I do actually really like my doc... but I began to wonder. Thanks for all the thoughtful responses.

I once fasted for 3 days to try to see what it was like to be really hungry but as it turns out, 3 days starving is just how I normally feel. title: Thanksgiving Carb Party Fallout
post text:
I’m suffering the consequences of going nuts during Thanksgiving. It was a small gathering of my family and my sister’s family, but we had a ton of delicious food. 

I’m only going to list the carb heavy dishes I indulged in. 

I taught my 9 year old son how to make my famous mashed potatoes (he made his own batch and I made mine, and his turned out better than mine ). My wife made cranberry sauce, sweet potatoes baked with brown sugar and spices, and apple crumb cake. 

My sister made butternut squash roasted with butter and brown sugar glaze, topped with kale, chickpea, quinoa, craisin topping. My niece made pastries filled with cream cheese, ginger, and cranberry sauce. 

I indulged in 4 glasses of wine plus all of the above (terribly high carb) deliciousness. And had such a wonderful time.

But oh boy. It’s been almost 48 hours since then and my BG is still all out of whack. My fasting BG is higher, even my “safe meals” raise my BG unusually high, BG stays elevated much longer after meal, and exercise doesn’t bring down my BG as easily as it usually does. I’m a little stressed out about how long and how badly that one (wonderfully delicious) meal is affecting my glucose control. Gonna have to work extra hard to get things under control quick. 

Thank goodness Thanksgiving is only once a year.
label: no stigma

title: a fun fact being T2
post text:
I am currently peeing outside in our large backyard because our restroom was under renovation. At first, I noticed that I was urinating frequently like about every 15 minutes per hour—which was unusual for me.
 
 As time passed, I realized that the spot where I had been peeing for a week had developed a large, sunken ANT NEST !!?
 
 That’s when I discovered that I was pre-diabetic or progressing towards Type 2 diabetes, and that my golden, sometimes white pee was apparently delicious or enough to help build an ant nest in just a week.
 
 Yep, that’s it.
 
 Thanks for coming to my TED Talk.

title: Looking for info / support about my neuropathy
post text:
Hello all. I got diagnosed with t2 back in January, my A1C was 10.6. My onset seemed really fast, less than 1 year earlier had no signs of pre-diabetes but I had gotten injured with a broken shoulder and possibly my change in lifestyle associated was a major contributor. Six months later I had it down to a 4.8 and my most recent blood work showed a 5.1 and I have not needed to take my metformin at all since \~June. 

However some days if feels like my neuropathy is worse than it was before and it is really hard to not slip into depression over it. I am in my mid 30's and the thought of living with this level of pain for the rest of my life really scares me. For a while it seemed like I was in less pain but had no feeling at all in the effected area. My symptoms are around the undersides of my toes back to about the bottom front half of my feet. I either have some mix of a constant burning pain and / or sparks where it feels like electricity shooting up my foot or I feel nothing at all. But even when I feel nothing it is a similar sensation to having a fat lip from the dentist around the toe joints and I cant find any shoes that consistently work anymore other than crocks. It feels like my foot swells up and is trying to break out of my shoes. Just last week I saw a podiatrist who said my blood flow to my feet looks good and started me on a supplement, Nerve Reverse, but said it could take up to 3 months before we know if it is helping at all. My insurance wont pay for diabetic shoes and orthopedics unless I have already hit my deductible for the year and I can't afford them on my own.

Recently it has felt like the burning feeling is more prevalent than just the numb feeling. I try to be hopeful that maybe I am having the burning feeling because it means that I am regaining some feeling in general and I just have to go through the pain part of feeling before getting back to not hurting but often I find myself thinking that it actually hurts so bad all the time because it is getting worse.

Anyways I don't really know what I am asking for here. Advice, maybe thoughts on my situation, experiences you all have been through. I appreciate your time.
label: no stigma

title: Q for my menstruating friends <3
post text:
do you experience a significant rise or fluctuation in your BG when you’re about to start your period/during your period?

i was diagnosed about a month ago and shortly after that my period started.  HOWEVER, bc my levels were really high and i had just started lowering them, i didn’t notice how it affected anything (i also didn’t even think of the possibility of my period affecting anything). 

so now almost a month since my last period and now that i’ve had my sugar more stable, i noticed about 4 days ago that my night time levels aren’t in the 80’s how they usually do (i’m now hanging at around 100 while i sleep), and all of my usual safe foods/meals are bumping me up an additional 15 points and i take even longer to come back down. so instead of sitting at around mid 80’s or 90’s during the day, my body is literally fighting to keep me at 100, and maybe 115-120 2-3 hours post meals. even after i do my usual gym work outs that i know can easily bring me back into the 70’s/80’s, at most i can get into 90’s territory for like 5 minutes before my body goes back up to 100? 

i started panicking thinking my diabetes was somehow getting worse or that i’d have to up my metformin dosage, only to start feeling some cramps on my daily walk on friday and then i remembered that i had also been feeling light headaches on wednesday & thursday, some lower back pain also a few days ago, feeling tired (BG wasn’t high either), and just more cramps yesterday. i track my period on an app and it says i was due 3 days ago but it’s NEVER been accurate since i’ve always had an irregular cycle since i was young but i’m thinking it might actually start soon bc of everything i’m feeling 

all of this to say: if it IS because of my period then that’s so rude of my own body to naturally just add an extra trigger to all of this

how do you tackle your diabetes while waiting for your period?

title: Question for Diabetics in Minnesota
post text:
I’m curious if anyone else with diabetes (or any other chronic illness) in Minnesota has actually experienced reduced cost of prescriptions this year with the new MN statue, 62Q.481, going into effect? 
 
For those unaware, [this  states that insurers can’t charge more than $25/prescription or $50 total/month for prescriptions used to treat chronic illnesses. My wife has type one diabetes and her prescriptions are all either still the same price, or more expensive this year. When the first one came through at something like $800 for a one month supply, she asked why it was that much and showed the pharmacist the statute. It wasn't insulin, it was the supplies needed for her continuous glucose monitor. They said they couldn't help, and recommended she ask her doctor. When she did that, and showed the statute, the doctor basically shrugged their shoulders and said to ask the insurance. When she did that, the rep said they hadn't ever heard of such a law and recommended she just submit a claim and wait. It has now been about a month and a half, and still nothing. Another round of prescriptions needs to get filled, and the pharmacist is saying its $800 again.
 
 The doctor, the pharmacy, and the insurance company are all basically just pointing the finger at someone else saying it’s their problem to fix. And it seems like her bringing the statute to their attention is the first anyone has heard of it, which seems crazy.
 
 Has anyone had success getting prescription costs to actually come down and reflect what’s stated in this law? Does anyone know the magic words or who to actually talk to to get it to actually process correctly? We're going crazy over here.
label: no stigma

title: Beta Bionics iLet First 90 Days
post text: So for my first 2 pumps I was on Medtronic. My last one was the 630G (the CGM sucked) once I switched to Dexcom I switched to the Tandem t slim X2. Good pump for the 4 years I had it no issues per say. But it's closed loop system is a bit behind looking back at it now. The iLet does 100% correction doses and there are no settings (basel rate, carb ratio, or correction factor) I mean I guess there are but the pump learns as you go and adjust all that you only input your weight. For meals you just choose breakfast lunch or dinner and the size normal less than or more than. Overall the pump has worked great. BG spikes are to a minimum and when it does it's for a much shorter amount of time since the pump is constantly learning how much correction to give and is giving it at 100%. My A1C dropped about 2% in the first 60 days. I'm my endos first patient on it and he was skeptical at first but had no issues with my trying it. Just had my follow up and he was instantly a believer.
label: no stigma

title: Alcohol and blood sugar
post text:
I'm recently diagnosed as T2 about 3 months ago. Some big adjustments but generally going pretty well.

One thing that confuses me a little still is alcohol, I am not a big drinker so haven't actually drunk since my diagnosis, but don't particularly miss it. Tonight I had a beer with dinner, and the dinner was a bit higher carb than usual as we're on holiday so trying to relax on my diet just a little. 

My BS is lower than normal post meal, which I understand is because my liver is prioritising processing the alcohol rather than carbs which turn into glucose.

I guess I'd like to understand if this is actually a good thing? Like if I am having a worse than usual meal, is having a small alcoholic drink with it actually helping keep my BS low? Or is it just keeping it low now and I'm likely to see it go crazy overnight or tomorrow? Is it better to skip the beer and let the spike happen and get it over with?

I'm wearing a CGM so will watch with interest but was hoping there might be some more knowledge someone could share.

title: Interpreting Dexcom G7 data?
post text:
Hi all. Two weeks ago my (65m) A1C test came back at 11.5. I saw my doctor a couple of days later and he set me up with Metformin, insulin, and Dexcom G7 sensors.

So for the past 10 days I’ve been taking my meds religiously and have made a lot of changes to my diet. I’ve been paying attention to the G7, and have been using their “Clarity” app to try to get a more detailed view of how things are going. The app reports have a lot of information - I am not certain what parts of it are significant. I plan to talk with my doctor about it when I can, but in the meantime, I’d be curious about any comments from those of you who are more experienced.

FWIW, my average blood glucose is now 130mg/dL w std dev 19mg/dL, and in the last week I’ve been 99% “in range” between 70 and 180. The attached picture shows my data for the last week - I wake up with circa 110, then my morning coffee/ light breakfast causes a rise, it goes back down, then dinner will make it rise again, then it falls. Again, any comments would be appreciated. Like: my blood glucose seems to ‘cycle’ over the course of 24 hours; does that look normal or problematic? Also: the 70-180 range is the default when I set up the G7; should I change that to (for instance) 70-160?

Thank you!

title: Supper: Curried Coconut Peas on Quinoa,  Dumplings and Spinach with Sesame Dressing
post text:
All home made. Pretty much whipped up with leftovers and veggies that were about to turn. **Estimated 45 g carbs for meal**

**Dumplings:**  
**dough**:  
about 1/2 c whole wheat flour w/ 1/2 c hot water, kneaded until smooth; take about a nickel-sized ball and roll into a thin disk  
**filling**:  
about 1/2 c diced carrots, cabbage, mushrooms sauteed with a little soy sauce, rice vinegar, siracha, and a touch of maple syrup. Let cool, then place about 1T into disk and fold. Steam for about 15 minutes.

**Coconut Curried Peas:**  
sauté cumin seeds, small diced onion, 2 minced garlic cloves, a diced green or red chili, and 1 tsp grated ginger, then add 1 T garam masala/curry powder and 1 c coconut milk. let simmer for about 10 minutes. Add 1 c frozen peas and 1/4 c grated coconut. Served over quinoa. Garnish with chopped cilantro and green onion.

**Spinach with Sesame Dressing:**

blanch a lot of spinach in boiling water for 30 seconds, then chop with a scissor. Add a tablespoon of tahini, a teaspoon of soy sauce, a teaspoon of maple syrup, and a teaspoon of rice vinegar. Mix. Sprinkle with Everything-but-the-bagel seasoning and diced red Thai chili pepper.

title: Can't lose weight because of glucose/insulin??
post text:
I have weighed and logged every bite that has gone into my mouth for two months. I set my goal to lose 1 lb per week and the recommended daily calorie intake is 1430, losing any faster would take me down to 1240 cals and that was too miserable. I am female, 5'4" 180 lbs, 52 y.o. and menopausal. My A1c was 6.8 last check, T2 (no trata done, just assumed), was prediabetic for like 15 years before that, gestational diabetes during two pregnancies 18 and 20 years ago. I'm not on any medication for T2. Strong family history, Father plus 2 grandparents and a couple of uncles. I exercise 3x per week, jogging and gentle strength training. The thing i want to emphasize is that I am 100% certain that I am calorie-restricting (no cheating, no counting errors). I emphasize that because it's hard to find help because people are super convinced that if you're calorie counting right you lose weight, period, so they immediately assume it's user error if it's not working. This mindset is leaving me without help because the advice us like "let's explore how you're doing it wrong" - I'm not. Not sounding cocky, just truly want that to be clear. t's also not a muscle gain thing. It's not a measurements thing, all my measurements are the same. 
 
 So my theory from reading around is that the weight is holding on tight because my glucose is not controlled and possibly the high glucose also means high insulin production? Is that a real thing? Would that really be so powerful as to counter significant calorie restriction to hold on to the weight? Where can i find the research on this? Have any of you gone through this? Were you able to lose weight once you lowered carbs? What amount of daily carbs should one aim for (approx)? I don't do well fasting and I hate keto (have tried both way back, 100% miserable for me). Then there's the menopause aspect that is probably a factor (In now on estrogen, can't due to a clotting disease, so just rawdogging menopause)
 
 I had opted to focus on calories for weight loss rather than control carbs directly, figuring once i lost some weight, the high glucose would take care of itself (that's what doctors tell us, isn't it) but if the weight loss is not possible without the glucose control, we are in this self-propelling problem and now it makes sense why I'm fat (I was glucose intolerant way before i was overweight). I'm so angry that doctors don't understand sometimes it's the glucose intolerance that makes you fat, not the other way around

title: What to do when you don’t have food and have no money to buy it?
post text:
Hello all, 
This is the first time I’ve run into this problem since being diagnosed a few years ago- I don’t have much food. And I don’t have money to get more food right now. 

Does anyone have any tips on how to stretch carbs out? Or any ideas for cheap, carb heavy foods, like rice?  What have others done in situations like this? Any helpful tips or information is greatly appreciated. 



I’ll include some information about my personal situation that make things a bit more complex for me- but I would love to see any tips or resources shared that may help anyone in a similar finical situation. Thanks in advance, guys! 



-

Some background about my situation: I do have EBT, but it’s been used for the month. I have a lot of money the government owes me coming, but I can’t follow up on that until mid February. Until then it’s a waiting game, hoping they call me to set up payments asap. I’ve borrowed hundreds already from family and have almost exhausted that resource. 
I have used the local food banks a few times, however, the closest one to me is currently closed for renovations. I’m disabled with no car, so going further is a struggle. 

I am also autistic with ARFID, so I have a very restricted diet. Most things the food bank supplies are things that my roommate can eat, but that I often cannot. 
Due to my restricted diet and other comorbidities, I do eat whatever I can in the moment and am not worried about the types of carbs, how many carbs I eat per day, etc. I’m not keto or low carb and don’t currently have to ability to adjust my diet in that way. I’m only interested in not going low and correcting when I go high. The act of eating is already a struggle for me, and I’m not trying to complicate it currently. 
 I’m working with an endo, nutritionist, ED specialist, psych, therapist, the whole 9 nine yards, and we’re working on creating a treatment plan that improves my relationship with food and imparts more nutrition.

I only say this to explain that some advice may not be viable for me- but I still want to see what others have done in the same situation! Even if something isn’t applicable to me, it may help someone else!0

title: Making Progress. Not where I want to be, but I've been struggling
post text:
I was diagnosed in 2022 with an A1C of 6.5.  Ozempic was brand new and relatively unheard of.  Oi was told to just lose weight and I'd be fine.  Ozempic made me very sick and is not something I want to do again.  I tried to educate myself and stumbled quite a bit.  The duet reccomended  by a standard nutritionist would have increased my carb intake.  I bought a glucometer but with little guidance the numbers didn't mean much to me.  Still, I managed to cut carbs,  reduce weight and get to 5.8.  

Then we bought a new home.  We ate out more as we were tired.  Worse yet after moving in, I got sick like I do every fall.  Still, I pushed through without seeing a doctor.  I woke up one morning in March with no hearing in my left ear.  This began a round of visits to doctors that has yet to end.  I had surgery in July for Chronic rhinosinusitis with polyps.  In August, the first of several mild, but lingering infections set in.

In September, I see my primary care doctor and my A1C is 7.5.  My BP is scary high, and my asthma is uncontrolled.  Meds updated and switched.  I am given a deadline to get my A1C down or additional meds will be added.  Seeing Ozempic or something that will make me equally sick, I know I need to make a change and quickly.  The directions given are to try the Mediterranean diet.  That's it no carb limit.  To me, this is as clear as mud.

I need help and specific guidance.  Through my husband's work, there is a paid program called Twin Health, that gives you access intermittently to CGM as they help you eliminate diabetes meds.  This has been really good for me.  I need the specific guidance, appreciate the app for tracking, feel grateful that help is a text message away.

I have still struggled, with difficult to treat asthma, that required oral and inhaled steroid use.  But my success here was keeping my blood sugar in a good healthy range despite being sick and the steroids increasing my BG.

I've been sick with tummy troubles and after 2 weeks of it.  Saw my BG rising, despite exercise and intermittent fasting.  I was at the point where I ate only because it was necessary to function.  I went to urgent care in part because of a BG rise.  It's kind of odd.  The rise wasn't particularly high, but it wasn't normal for me.  TBH I'm not sure why this was my breaking point but it was.  Urgent Care ran blood tests and found that my liver enzymes were in the thousands.  They sent me straight to the hospital as they were worried about liver failure.  That odd little BG spike may have saved my life (and a smart doctor).


I'm out of the hospital and working to heal.  I'm apx 2 months out from the 7.5 A1C.  They checked my A1C while in the hospital and it was 6.2.  So not normal but in the pre-diabetic range.  I'll have another A1C in apx 1 month.  I'm hoping it will be normal. 

At this point it's not usually my eating habits but illness and lack of sleep that increase my BG.  I've learned I can have much if what I enjoy eating.  But I have to be strategic when I do it.  Generally, I wait for days when I'm low or when I'm physically active.  

My advice to all is get a CGM if you can.  Don't ignore illness.  Get enough sleep.  Treating illness and sleeping represent an apx 20 point drop in BG for me.     Gentle exercise really helped keep my BG down while taking steroids.  Exercising while exhausted or ill just increases my BG.  Balance what you do.  

Thanks for listening to my ramble.  I just needed to be heard.  I hope this helps someone else.  I'm kind of wondering if I can get from an A1C of 6.2 to below 5.7 in a month.

title: A Sad Day
post text: So, I just saw the results of my tests, and even though I haven't gotten the final answer from the doctor yet, I know I already have diabetes. My A1C is 11.5%. I'm really young, and this has been hitting me hard, leaving me scared. After all, the disease will have more time to develop. My world feels like it's spinning. I know that with numbers this high, there’s probably already been some damage, but what affects me the most is the guilt and the feeling of not being normal. It feels like I’ll never be able to go out with my friends to eat again, and I’m also scared I won’t be able to have a relationship with anyone. It’s hard to find people who accept you. And on top of that, it feels like there’s not much information about type 2 diabetes. I’ve done a lot of research recently, and I see people with type 1 diabetes showing themselves to the world like warriors, but people with type 2 seem to hide. I don’t see influencers or anything like that talking about type 2 diabetes, unlike with type 1. Is having type 2 diabetes really that shameful?

title: I'm insulin dependent. Just getting this off my chest. I know I'm stupid and will die early
post text:
I'm not type 1 I'm just considered Insulin dependent at the moment. My body's immune system is not attacking my pancreas which were proven in blood tests. 

My pancreas beta cells which produce insulin have a severely decreased function. My doctors are now gonna check if it's genetics related e.g. MODY

Idk what to do or say or think. I guess i just wanted a place to write this out so I can move on. 

I suppose years of a fast food diet and no exercise were the main contributors. I'm in my mid twenties, my height is 6ft and I weigh 83kg and was diagnosed several months ago

I could eat a small 20 gram crisp (chips for americans) packet and my blood sugar goes from 5mmol/l to 10mmol/l+ if I don't take insulin. How does this make sense. It's like my pancreas is not making any insulin at all. 

Then my diet is shit and I don't exercise its hard to change these habits.

Then I'm supposed to learn carb counting but a lot of current foods I eat don't even have this information on them.

I know I'm a grown adult and supposed to figure this shit out for myself but I can't be asked. I rather just like a very short life being happy than a long life struggling.

And before people ask speak to my doctor I'm literally a patient at a diabetes specialist center filled with doctors and nurses who specialise in diabetes. Their advice is just generic stuff and they usually tailored for type 1 or type 2 but I'm not fitting in either of these categories. They telling me to learn carb counting start with 1 unit of insulin for 10 carbs. They recommend not to keep correcting with novorapid (fast acting insulin) because my blood sugar still spikes. And they say if i have to increase anything it's my basal long acting? I'm currently on 8 units on that each night because my pancreas still produces a little insulin. They said ideally they want me to get by just on basal. But if my basal gets much higher I'm gonna be in lows so often if I don't eat more than once a day. Then I have to keep topping up my sugar so the basal has something to work on. 

Idk man life doesn't seem worth living this way. I know I'm stupid and yes I know I'll die if i don't fix my habits. I heard it all...

title: I'm better, but concerned
post text:
First of all, I’d like to thank everyone who responded to my latest post. Without a doubt, you all helped me a lot.

As I had predicted, I was finally diagnosed with diabetes yesterday. My doctor now wants to figure out what type of diabetes I have, it’s probably type 2. My last test in 2020 showed I was completely healthy. I believe that if it were type 1, I wouldn't have survived this long without insulin (I’m 22 years old). Anyway, I talked a lot with her and opened up about my concerns. She cleared everything up for me, but I think she was really kind and wasn’t 100% truthful with me. That’s why I want to share a bit of what happened and ask for advice from those of you who are more experienced with this.

First of all, I’d like to share some of the responses I received

*  When I asked about the symptoms, she said that I’m young and that the body of young people is amazing. She told me that if I keep my numbers in the range of someone without diabetes, I will definitely pause the symptoms and won’t need to worry about them developing.
* When I asked about any damage that may have already occurred, she said that, in general, diabetes needs to be out of control for years before it really starts affecting the body.
* She told me to stop searching for information on Google. She said that everything posted there is often exaggerated and doesn’t reflect reality, and that it usually just makes people feel bad and have panic attacks
* "When I asked about my A1c (11.5%), she said that at this level, I should already be starting to introduce insulin as medication, but that she wants to try lowering it with oral medication, diet and exercise first. I’m taking 500mg of metformin three times a day, after each meal
* When I asked about complications, she said that in relation to vision and amputations, that only happens to people who discover the disease too late or to those who don’t control their diabetes. She told me that if I take care of myself from now on, I’ll never have to worry about that

Well, that’s what she told me, and it kind of made me skeptical. All the answers were very positive, so I have some questions for those of you who have been living with the disease for years. Do you agree with what she said about symptoms and concerns?

  
I’m asking this because the first advice I received on this Reddit was to be careful with doctors

I'm going to see an ophthalmologist and I hope everything is okay. I had a few episodes of blurry vision a few years ago, but I didn’t worry because I thought it was due to continuous screen use (the problems didn’t last, and right now I don’t feel like my vision is affected), but with the diagnosis, it’s impossible not to worry

title: Diabete of shock - My story
post text:
Hello everyone,
 
 
 I hope you are doing fine
 
 I am sorry in advance for my english as it is not my first language but i am going to do my best.
 
 I am here to share with you my story of how i developped what my doctor called diabetes of shock. This is a reminder that YOUR BODY HEAR AND FEEL EVERYTHING YOUR MIND SAYS. DO NOT UNDERESTIMATE YOUR MIND.
 
 Now some context, i am a 22years old female, normal weight and height, most of my childhood and teenage years i did daily sport and activities i was a very dynamic kid. Overall i was healthy, eating healthy nothing out of the ordinary.
 
 For family, we have no diabetes aside from one grandparent who had type 2 diabetes at 90yo which idk why idont count. As a prevention of my health, every year i get a bloodwork where i test for diabetes, vitamins, iron levels everything...
 I was healthy so far my a1c levels were always between 5.2 to 5.5%, i had no symptoms nothing , life was good.
 
 
 Now during 2024, it was a very traumatic year for me. In one random tuesday, i became homeless, lost all my money, lost my family and ihad alot of issues while being a full time student and had a job. I vividly remember how painful it was PHYSICALLY, i had this constant feeling of each bone of my body hurting as if it was broken when i was perfectly fine. I could remember how hurt i was emotionally to the point where id have daily panic attacks, burns out, id be screaming and crying like a hurt animal. I was just miserable. It was intense and i was just shocked, i was so shocked that something in me broke or snapped or idont even know.
 
 
 Barely a month into the crisis, i was peeing alot, thirsty, couldnt skip or delay a meal id be shaking dizzy and horrible. I remember hunger which was just from delaying a meal by 1 hour to be painful i couldnt think straight unless i ate EVERYTHING IN FRONT OF ME.
 
 
 Seeing the context, i assumed it was stress or anxiety that i was going through a rough period so ofc it would show a way or another on me.
 
 Now this kept going for months, id be shaky, cold dizzy many times during the day but the situation was bad and i was distressed so i just brushed it off.
 
 Until one day, i fainted randomly. Got blood work done, a1c was around 6.7% im now type 2 diabetic at 22yo with no family history , no weight problems nothing. My severe hypoglycemia issues were reactive hypoglycemia and ofc the stress did not make it any better.
 
 I went to the doctor, i live in a 3rd world country. CGMs are not sold in pharmacy so i import them for twice the price and absolutely not covered by insurance as it doesnt even exist here. I was put on Metformin 500mg twice a day but i couldnt stand the side effects AT ALL, apparently there is smtg called extended release metformin but idont think its available here. Mounjaro and Ozempic arent available ucan buy them imported by someone and not refunded by insurance.
 
 I went to a doctor, explained what happened and he said that sometimes a traumatic experience can be so bad you can develop diabetes from it and many others conditions.
 
 Please, be gentle on yourself. Your body hears what your mind says. 
 
 
 Have a nice day <3 and i hope everything goes well for you
label: no stigma

title: Allulose
post text: I just tried something and was impressed with the results. In the past, weight training would send my blood sugar up 75-90 mg/dl. Today I added a tsp of allulose to my PWO, as it is supposed to be a GLP-1 agonist. According to a google search, it will lower blood glucose, increase insulin sensitivity, increase insulin secretion and reduce appetite the same way as Wegovy etc. I have been using it in my morning coffee, with zero glucose spike, but never thought to try it to stop the mid workout spike. Long story short, instead of 75-90 mg rise, today I only had about 42 mg rise, which kept it below 180 mg, and even after my post workout meal with some carbs, it barely spiked about 180. I plan to add it for a week or so and see how it works long term.

title: Is this my life now?
post text:
It's 5am where I'm at. My 10 yo son, who just got diagnosed with T1d 4 days ago, after his regular annual checkup, just had his first alarm of hypoglycemia 30 minutes ago. I got up and brought him some honey. He is back asleep. I'm watching the darn monitor to make sure he's ok.
 
 It just dawned on me: that's his life now. He'll never be "carefree) again. He'll always have to use a CGM. He'll always have to count carbs. He'll always have to be on the lookout.
 
 And it's not fair to him. He's just a child and all that part of his life has been cut short. His chance to be carefree. To eat junk food with his friends. All of that.
 
 Sorry for the rant. I'm just a tired mom who's getting used to the new reality of things.

We already eat a lot of vegetables and plant-based protein, BUT we are also a carb-loving household  Any advice is appreciated!

title: First month of my new normal down!
post text:
....and I think I'm doing well. I still have to make an appointment with the endocrinologist and a dietician (blame work schedule for that, that was just not happening with my job in December), but hopefully I'll have that down in the next two weeks, along with a visit to my primary doc.

My doctor has me on a once-a-day blood test, so I usually take it in the morning around 7 AM, before I eat and take my metformin.

Beginning BS - 244

As of today - 110

There's been a few weird spikes (yesterday I was at 160) but it's been helping me to go "oh, I shouldn't have eaten that item/ eaten so dang late" and course correcting for the future.  I've been trying to narrow down my meals and have decent portion sizes so I don't feel the need to snack all day.

Since my diagnosis, I've \*really\* started eyeballing the ingredients in stuff. What's the carb content? Okay, I had X earlier which had this much....might want to not have that till tomorrow. Oooh, this has a nice amount of protein, gotta get that. The American Diabetes Association website's been a big help in helping me decide on much healthier options for my meals. 

 Blueberries and apples have been my snack of choice, and I'm noticing that now that I have a lot less processed sugar, I'm really noticing the sweetness in fruits and nuts now. So that's nice.  

I'm allowing myself one cup of coffee in the morning, and then It's pretty much flavored water for the rest of the day, I'm still working my way through trying to find a sweetener that works for me overall, but I've not found anything that I really like yet. I have time.

Exercise-wise, I'm trying to get in about 30 minutes on my stationary bike before work in the mornings, and right after I'm done (thank goodness I WFH)  And I've dropped about six pounds, which is another thing that makes me happy. My goal is ten total before my next doctor visit near the end of the month.

So, overall - y'all were right. It's not been as stressful changing as I thought it was, and I'm feeling WAY better; the difference is really surprising. My husband and friends have been very supportive. Some of them are diabetics as well, so I've been getting some advice from folks who've been in the trenches with this for years. :)
