title: I just wanted to share my A1C level. I'm ecstatic.
post text:
I had an A1C level of 9.4% back in September. It was down from 11% (!) the year before. This with 2000mg metformin a day.

I finally decided to take a drastic step and... take care of myself and my health. I switched to an 800-kcal a day diet and started walking a lot each day. I lost 30kg (66 pounds) in 2 months (from 121kg (266 pounds) down to 91kg (200 pounds)) and my A1C level is now 5.5%. I am ecstatic and overjoyed and just wanted to share this accomplishment with you.

My biggest problem was the lack of any proper nutritional education. I just ate everything without ever thinking (or knowing) what I put inside my body. At the beginning of December I thought I need to slow down with my weight loss and start eating more normally. I can now enjoy a burger (with a wholemeal bun) or even french fries without sending my glucose through the roof. My daily kcal target is now around 1500. The biggest challenge now is to not revert to my old ways - eating a lot and being inactive.

My blood pressure also went down from hypertension to low blood pressure. My GP had to take me off one of my meds as it was making me hypo with all the life-style changes. Here's hoping I can keep it up and not go back to my old ways.

title: Could there be a physiological reason my Libre 3 is so far off?
post text:
I’m so mad… the last couple weeks I thought my increased Ozempic dose was having an awesome impact on my BS. So much so that I was actually eating more to combat lows. For some reason, when I switched out my sensor as scheduled, I decided to check my BS with my meter. The meter was 45 points higher than the CGM. I chalked it up to a fluke due to it being a new sensor but have kept using my meter in the days after to verify and nope, this sensor is off by 50-60 points each reading. I’ve used a CGM for over a year. I place it on the back of my arm per directions. These low readings are not compression lows. I have a hard time believing I got two bad sensors in a row but I’m sure it happens… I guess??

I had a heart attack at the end of December. Keeping my BS is check is so important to me (A1C  6.5 on Ozempic only). And now I don’t feel like I can rely on my CGM. Hell, the data is already skewed for the last 2.5 weeks so now I have no idea where I stand and what’s actually going on. And as you can see, I’m spiraling. 

So can something physiologic throw CGMs off? Dehydration? Medications (not vitamin C)? I dunno, just trying to figure this out.

title: Diagnosed this week, having a real hard time with it.
post text:
I am the son of medical professionals and I’ve always had generally good health. I guess I took that for granted for a bit too long but I’m active, I work out, I do MMA training, I run and I have for the most part avoided a lot of the foods they say can lead to diabetes. High sugar foods, high carbohydrate foods. I drink crystal light and water, I don’t drink soda. I don’t eat cookies and cakes except on special occasions. 

Diabetes does run in my family through my father but I thought I was doing things right.

I had been far too long since my last physical so I found a new Doctor and did the blood work.

I discovered at that time that I was an uncontrolled diabetic. My world kinda fell apart a little bit. If I’ve been depriving myself of all these things I love for literal years and still ended up with diabetes what hope is there for me?

This has all been overwhelming to me. At least my diet has been kinda in line I thought but now I have found I’ll have to deprive myself of even more good tasting foods. 

I love vegetables and I’ll make it work but I’m having a real hard time not falling into depression over this. I’ve been put on Metformin, but GLP-1 is almost certainly out of the question because of pancreatic issues I had once a long time ago.

I know this is far from the worse diagnosis someone can get but as someone who really has worked hard to be healthy this felt like a gut punch and has nearly brought me to tears a couple times. 

Sorry, this was just a vent but I’ve got nobody with experience with this to talk to. I feel defeated.
label: yes stigma

title: A1C down 6.8-5.4 in 7 months
post text:
Hi everyone,
Just wanted to get on here and give some hope to anyone 
I got diagnosed in April with an a1c of 6.8 and started Mounjaro (currently at 7.5mg) and I’m down almost 50lbs, my a1c is healthy, I workout 1-2x a week, my binge eating has been controlled to 1x a week vs daily, and I just have a better relationship with food.  

My sugar intake is significantly reduced and more excitingly - I got my menstrual cycle back after nearly 2 years!

I appreciate this forum more than you know. My a1c drop or weight loss is as jaw dropping as some folks on here, but I am focusing on sustainability and mindful eating which can be a bit tough as a full time nurse and full time grad student in my rotations, which limits my time quite a bit. 

But to anyone out there dealing with PCOS, binge eating, fatty liver, and T2D - there is hope!

title: PSA short walks have been a game changer to deal with spikes
post text:

title: Update to seizure
post text:
Hi family,
Idk how to link my original post but I posted a couple days ago about a scary low that resulted in an ambulance ride because of a seizure.. or insulin shock as many pointed out. First of all, thank you all for the well wishes. I will look into the inhale-able glucagon and I’m kinda pissed my endo never told me about it. Granted, it takes me 8 months in advance to even talk to her so there’s that.  Im downtown Chicago so if anyone has an endo they love… honestly I’m open.  I am currently visiting my parents in Durango Colorado which is at a super high altitude and I learned that can greatly affect our BGs. 

Welp, I went and saw a cardiologist and he said he didn’t hear a heart murmur so that’s one less thing to worry about. I had passed out at work previous to the seizure and two different docs told me they heard a murmur so I had obvious anxiety about that. 

So now I’m just stuck paying a $600+ ambulance/ER visit. I remember even talking to the woman working on me in the ambulance about cost and of course she told me not to worry… 

Is it bad if I worry more about the cost of being helped then actually getting helped?  That was honestly my first thought when I came to. Im a flight attendant so I don’t make much and the job is rough on the body.  I probably could have made it through my episode slowly and saved myself half my rent.  With the cost of all of our supplies/insulin/accentental ER visits..  idk how to keep this up.

title: It’s not been a good two months, medicine denied, and slipping into bad habits… I don’t even remember how to cope.
post text:
Been diagnosed for maybe 4/5 years now. 11.1 to 5.1, but rising. I lost some weight on my own but mounjaro was my saving grace. 

I was a super responder dropping weight on the 2.5dose (considered a therapeutic dose for 98% of folks) so I never dosed up even when asked. I was too thin, I really get it. 

But here's the thing, that medicine was the boost I needed to: 1) have energy to exercise 2) control my glucose and reduce inflammation 3) have a hunger cue for the first time in my life. 

I feel incredibly depressed hoping to hop on ozempic, if I don't get denied again... my doctor has done the peer to peer and I've filed an appeal and he's filed an appeal. 

I've gained 7 lbs in 1.5 months, I've gone from 80fasting to like 120. I'm back on metformin which makes me so sick. 

But most of all I'm so depressed. I hated my sick body. On mounjaro I was the best version of me. I wasn't sick. I was so happy. I accomplished so much when I was on it. 

I don't know what to do, and I wondered if quitting the drug is really the cause behind me crying every few minutes of the day. 

It feels bleak y'all. And I literally feel worthless when I binge eat, which has happened twice for the first time in 18 months. 

It's so bleak. And I hate insurance companies.

title: Frustrated with lack of weight loss, energy, and progress.
post text:
It’s almost been a month (4weeks) since diagnosis and lifestyle changes. My first week I contacted my psych NP because I thought I was having another depressive episode (bipolar 2) but she talked me through what to expect with lifestyle changes. That I would be low energy while my body gets used to not having so much carbs. That I’m feeling down because I have no energy. Well it’s been another three weeks and I can’t do much of anything. I have so little energy. I can sleep for 12+ hours and still feel tired. 

I’ve gone from having takeout almost everyday, eating barely any veggies, stuffing myself with cakes, candies, and whatever else. I was not in a happy place and replacing cigarettes with food after I quit. 

I no longer have takeout 50-75% of my meals consist of only veggies. I have one slice of multigrain bread in the am with two eggs. And chicken and greens for lunch and dinner. I snack on either cheese or an apple with a tablespoon of peanut butter and I lost a whopping 10 lbs. I don’t understand what I’m doing wrong? I don’t have the energy to workout. I barely have the energy to work and take care of my kids. 

I’m going to reach out to my psych NP again to see if it’s just an episode and I need to up my meds. Doesn’t explain the lack of weight loss. My next doctors appointment is in March. Hopefully I get some answers. I’m on metformin 500mg twice a day. I just got a glucose monitor to see how low carb tortillas or a baby potato could affect me. Seems baby potato is fine but low carb tortillas are a no go. 

Can someone explain what I’m missing? I thought with such a huge difference in eating I’d be shedding the pounds and feeling so much better but all I feel is exhausted.

title: Does maintaining A1c and glucose actually help in T2 diabetes???
post text:
I know that this might sound stupid but I found a research article on Cochrane library that said the following:

"Fourteen studies involving 29,319 people with at risk of diabetes complications were included and 11 studies involving 29,141 people were included in our analyses.

Tighter blood glucose control generally didn't show any benefits for patients compared to less tight glucose control. There was no difference in the risks for patients on kidney failure, death, or heart disease complications. A very small number of patients (1 in every 1000 treated each year) might avoid a heart attack with more intense blood glucose management. Some patients would expect to have less protein leakage through kidney function although the clinical impact of this benefit is unclear in the long term. The potential problems with treatment, such as side effects and risks of very low blood glucose (hypoglycaemia) were not generally measured in the studies."

Edit: Link:



P.S. I think there was another article as well on HbA1c maintained below 7 vs above 7 and those groups didn't have a big difference with diabetic complications either. I’m just scared that there’s no way to beat this disease and I’m doomed genetically.

title: I’ve had multiple hypo seizures (explanation below)…. Advice?

I’ve had several circumstances including yesterday where my dexcom was so far off during my sleep that I had a seizure upon waking, but it was reading normally on my sensor. The seizures have happened mostly within the past two years. Almost always during sleep or upon waking. I’m now finger pricking before bed, eating a bedtime snack, downloaded Sugarmate, set my low alarm during sleep to 85, and never giving insulin to correct while I’m sleeping. I know this isn’t a common occurrence but my doctor isn’t really much help beyond just needing to keep my numbers a bit higher, which I will absolutely do. Maybe I’m just looking for comfort, advice or really anything? Have you had a seizure due to type 1 before? Let me know, thank you.

title: Jardiance vs Ozempic?
post text: So I'm a 31 year old male. 2 years ago I went for a physical(after a couple of years) and my blood sugars were through the roof. My A1C was 12.3(I know very bad). Part of it is genetic, but in my mid 20s I got really unhealthy. I ate and drank a lot and got up to 250 pounds. I'm only 5'8-5'9 by the way. What happened was the physical I got before I was 26, and I was prediabetic and the doctor said if I don't loose weight I'll be diabetic in a year. Which happened, I started getting symptoms about a year later but instead of getting a physical I kept denying it, and let it be undiagnosed for a few years until I went to finally get my physical when I was 29.  In that time I was peeing a lot, always thirsty, and losing weight without doing anything. I went down to like 210 pounds. Anyways, the doctor put me on jardiance and Janumet. For the first year, I was very careful about it and brought my A1c down 6.6-7.2 But in the last year I kind of fell of the wagon. My last A1c (2 months ago)it was high again, not as high as before, but it was 8.8 which isn't good I know. Last two months I've been serious and it is better but based on the  sensor I wear it isn't as low as it should be. I feel it's not as controlled due to it getting higher over the last year. Anyways, my doctor recommended ozempic every week, which I'm planning on getting to bring it down again. For those who have taken it as well as jardiance/janumet, would you say it's better?  The thing is now I'm really not heavy, my BMI is in the healthy range, I'm like 160-164. But I want to get it down more. I know I'll need to work on my diet and exercise, but will the shot help bring it down and then I can keep it low with my diet and exercise? The thing also is I don't eat much, which is my sugars are probably fluctuating still. I was planning on getting the lowest dose and all. Any advice/experiences would be appreciated. My insurance will cover it by the way it seems, and even then I have money, but I just want to make sure that this will bring it down, as I'm worried.

title: My A1C update!
post text:
Hey everyone!

I just wanted to share a major milestone in my journey. I’m a 28F who was diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes last September, and my A1C was a whopping 10.9. It was a huge wake-up call. But today, just a few months later, my A1C is down to 5.7! I can’t even believe it myself!

Here’s what worked for me:

Diet Changes: I started changing my diet back in August (even before the official diagnosis), and that was honestly a lifesaver. By the time I got diagnosed, I was already in the groove of eating healthier, which made it so much easier to commit 100%. I cut out processed carbs and sugar, focused on whole foods, and really paid attention to portion sizes.

The Gym: On January 2, I decided to take things to the next level and hit the gym consistently. I’ve been working out 5 days a week, mixing cardio and strength training. The routine has done wonders, not just for my blood sugar but also for my mental health and energy levels.

It hasn’t been easy, but seeing the progress keeps me going. If you’re struggling with your own health, know that small changes really do add up. And once you start seeing results, it gets a lot easier to stick with it!

If you’re on a similar journey, I’d love to hear your stories or any tips you might have. Thanks for letting me share!

title: This Exercise Can Reduce Sugar Level In 2 Minutes
post text:
Does anyone know about this? I found it accidently while looking for something else. Sounds too good to be true, but I Googled it & it's confirmed elsewhere. 

Can you check with your doctors to see why if true is not being told to patients? 

Google link too long. Enter "do heel raises lower blood sugar"

Summary - Yes, heel raises, also known as soleus push-ups, can help lower blood sugar. This exercise can be done while seated. 
How it works - Engages the soleus muscle in the calf, Improves insulin sensitivity, Stimulates blood flow, Increases oxidative metabolism, and Reduces triglycerides in the blood. 

Don't know if this is true -


EDIT - Thanks everybody!!! I know exercise is good for high sugar & other things, but this is specifically targeting the soleus muscle that works differently by drawing sugar out of the blood.

Heel raises, particularly when performed with a bent knee to target the soleus muscle in the calf, can help lower blood sugar by significantly increasing the muscle's uptake of glucose from the bloodstream, enhancing insulin sensitivity, and promoting better blood circulation due to the muscle's "pumping" action when contracted; essentially, the soleus muscle acts as a glucose sink, drawing sugar out of the blood when activated through exercises like heel raises.

title: What triggers “erratic” BG behavior in you?
post text:
I’d like to know what possible events and conditions trigger your BG to act out of the norm - for example: spiking on foods you usually do well on, unusual changes in fasting BG levels, etc..

So far, I’ve noticed cortisone shot completely throws my BG out of whack - higher fasting levels, random spikes, spikes even with usually safe foods, exercising won’t bring down my BG like it normally would. 

I also noticed when I had really bad carb filled meal on Thanksgiving, my BG was out of whack for 2 days - although not as bad as cortisone shot. 

Today, I had work problems. Had to start work early, so I skipped breakfast and my usual morning exercise. Drank 2 extra cups of black coffee (instead of my usual 1 cup), and was kinda stressed out. I woke up with BG at 108. Since then my BG has been, 146, 124, 134 - all on empty stomach (except black coffee). Finally sitting down to eat something and go for a walk after, so hopefully my BG gets back to behaving normally. 

So, what are some things that throw your body out of whack and make it harder to control your BG?

title: Diabetes Meds & Chronic Hives
post text:
Sorry this might be a bit long but I am really curious if anyone else has experienced possible allergic reactions to meds that have caused chronic hives.

I’ve had diabetes for the past 2 years, my A1C as of the beginning of November is at 7.3, not the best I know but it’s coming down from being higher up. Been very strict on dieting and exercise. 

At the end of September I started getting little hive splotches on my legs and abdomen. I immediately thought I had grown allergic to either my meds, my cat or some foods and asked my GP to go see an allergist. Instead the sent me to the clinic and I got steroid shot and steroid meds. The hives went away for a week and then came back. 

I call Kaiser again and tell them they came back and in short they give me the run around until I finally get to see my GP mid November. Yay healthcare. My doctor takes one look at the hives on my legs and goes “that’s not hives idk what that is. Let’s get your a dermatologist appointment.” Who I get to see tomorrow morning. 

Currently the hives are the worst they have ever been. They’ve now spread all over my body including my forehead, I’m pretty sure my scalp since it’s been itchy, and neck. They are big red welts at this point. 


Here’s what I’m currently on and have been for the past two years:
4 500 MG XL Metformin at night with 2.5 MG Glipizide XL and 20 MG Lipitor. 
And then the same 2.5 Glipizide in the morning as well. I’ve stopped taking the Glipizide and Lipitor yesterday to see if there’s any improvement 

I asked my GP if I possibly could have become allergic to one of these 3 medications and he really doesn’t seem to think so but I have read otherwise. I really hope the dermatologist has some answers tomorrow or can refer me to an allergist. I seriously hate Kaiser right now. 

Has anyone here ever grown allergic to their medication? I’m getting really tired of this and don’t know what to do.

title: Three heart attacks plus major surgery later I’m diagnosed with diabetes. Feeling very very miserable.
post text:
This is long and I’m feeling very low, so if it’s not your thing please just ignore it. I’m feeling very depressed about the whole thing. 
 
 I recently got home from hospital, having had three stents put in my heart at the age of 46. 
 
 Former rugby player, not a single previous visit to a doctor, absolutely 100% asymptomatic for diabetes, hypertension, and high cholesterol- all of which dominate the genetic map of my large Irish family. The only thing I’ve ever seen a doctor for is broken bones; lots of them. 
 
 Two initial, smaller, heart attacks coincided with a horrific shoulder spasm I occasionally get due to a rugby injury two decades ago. The pain felt strange this time but I ignored it. The third was unmistakable - an unimaginable pain across my chest and into my left arm and jaw. Did I immediately call an ambulance? Of course not- I waited 12 hours till a friend came and drove me to hospital, from where I was immediately dispatched to a specialist unit at another hospital. 
 
 After the first blood test the consultants immediately asked if I was diabetic. “No, but my mother’s family have it”. More tests, turns out I’m severely diabetic. Blood sugar was well over 25 mmol/L. So 450+ in the mg/dl system. 
 
 Had to phone home to Ireland to speak to my aunt. The diabetes situation among them is much more serious than they had ever told me. My cousin, male and my age, was diagnosed just last year, another cousin injects four times per day, an aunt has had her health utterly wrecked by it, etc etc etc. 
 
 I’m now in a situation where I have to simultaneously control cholesterol intake, keep carbohydrates down, and **still somehow get enough calories**. My weight is bang on average for a 6ft 1 male of my age. Trying to find calories while eating what feels like bird food is utterly demoralising. 
 
 The websites giving diabetic meal advice just don’t cater to people like me at all. I grew up on what you might call a farmer’s diet due to my parents’ backgrounds- I ate well all my life. Very little processed crap, I never drink sodas or buy fast food. Very plain diet - the “meat and two veg” world with occasional pastas and casseroles. 
 
 So when faced with all the quinoa and flaxseed salads, or sweet potato and phoenix feather roulades I just feel like jumping off a cliff. Right now I have zero interest in “making diabetic food interesting”, which is what 99% of these sites seem to be aiming for, at the expense of simple advice. 
 
 If anybody has any book ideas or food lists or sites etc that give **simple** meal ideas I’d appreciate it. Last night I had a plate of green beans and a lump of plain chicken breast, covered it in a weird tomato and low-fat yoghurt sauce I concocted and it was fine. 
 
 I don’t care about the injections, I don’t care about the finger pricking. It’s the food. 
 
 What I really want is just a list of stuff I can eat. One site will say x is fine, whereas another will tell me x is awful. 
 
 Finally, and I gather this is the most common emotional issue among diabetics: many of my friends don’t have a fucking clue. “Oh you’ll be fine”, “Oh my dad has diabetes, he’s fine”. This thing has overnight ruined my life but apparently I’m “fine”. It’s impacted every area of my life and taken away two of the biggest pleasures I had - a Guinness with my friends at the weekend, and a hearty meal at the close of day. 
 
 Excuse the ranting, and thanks for any and all advice/support/sympathy/encouragement. 
 
 Dave.
label: yes stigma

title: Stood up by nutritionist
post text:
How do you deal with all the frustrations of trying to live with diabetes? (I'm in Canada, not sure how much of a diffrence that makes)

I was diagnosed 6 weeks ago, and it feels like at every turn there's a complete lack of professional support.  

First the doctor who diagnosed me spent maybe 5 minutes telling me I needed to take my meds and BG readings, and then ran off (I was freshly out of surgery for something mostly unrelated, but that did require a full bloodworm panel that lead to my diabetes diagnosis, so i couldn't get up out of bed and run after her). I eventually had to spend half an hour on the phone with the hospital staff to even find out the doctor's name, so I could call their clinic, so I could ask when and how often they wanted me to take my BG readings. 

I'm told that there aren't any available nutritionists or dieticians, but i should go to a website to figure that out. The website gives the most generic advice about eating lots of plants and not too many carbs, and to ask your doctor for more details. 

Similarly when I asked for advice on exercising. 

Trying to follow the vague advice I found online got my average BG readings down from the 12 to 14 it was the first days I got back from the hospital, down to 7 to 10. My doctor hasn't told me anything about what I should be aiming for, but this internet tells me even 7 is still higher than it should be. 

So I finally cave and rearrange my budget for the next few months, to figure out how afford to hire a nutritionist privately for a single consultation. That was supposed to be today. I joined the call 5 minutes early, but the nutritionist never showed up, never answered the messages in chat. I call customer support and get told they can reschedule me for an hour later, I say yes. I got to join that call, and I see that I have the right time but the appointment is tomorrow. I call customer support again, and they hang up on me. 

I'm just so done with all this bullshit. Kinda curious to see how much sugar it would take me to guzzle before I hit a coma

title: Should I expect more?
post text:
Hiya, 

I am 36, 146kg and I was diagnosed with T2 diabetes on the 18th of November. I am based in the UK. 

My initial blood test showed a result of 94mmol/mol on the 28th of October and then 73mmol/mol on the 18th of November.  I started Mounjaro on the 28th of October, unrelated to my diabetes diagnosis, I was going it to lose weight.  When I recieved the test results back from the 18th of November my GP was overjoyed with the result. He had said to keep doing what I am doing as it was working and that I would have a health check in January since it is my birthday month and from there we could discuss options. 

I was given no advice on diabetes apart from to check out the diabetes UK website and another website. I haven't been offered any other support or advice. I have mostly lurked on here and read some books and been following a not quite keto diet.  Since October I have lost over 15kg and feeling a lot better about myself. I have bought my self a bgm and have used it to test my fasting blood sugar twice now. 

I was due to have my health check yesterday. I attended the session, he took my blood pressure and looked at my feet and sent me on my way. He didn't need a urine sample because he had my test result from November, and no recomendation to get my Hba1c level checked again, which I had thought would be the case. Feeling confused, I asked if this was part one of my appointment and he assured me that it was complete and I could go home. 

I just feel a bit let down. Surely this can't have been the health check that my GP was referring to? I was hoping that finally I would actually discuss my health with someone. I have been working so hard on making better choices and dealing with the stress of hoping that it is working for me.  Obviously I need to reach out to my GP and check with them as only they can really tell me, but I struggle with avocating for myself at the best of times due to my Audhd and I am also a full time carer.  

Is this what I should expect?

title: A1c down 9.4-5.3 three months.
post text:
47m In late July I wound up in the ER which lead to two findings. 1. I have kidney cancer and 2. I have Diabetes. I was told I would be losing my right kidney and that I needed to get my diabetes under control or I would loose the other one. Since then I have lost about 40 lbs and this week I found out I got my a1c down to 5.3 without medications.

**Meds:** None for diabetes. Started Lipitor on my doctors advice. I have since switched Crestor.

**Monitoring:** I have been using a OneTouch Verio Flex to monitor blood sugar with a finger prick. I was only prescribed to test twice a day but I was testing 5-6 to better understand how different foods affected my blood sugar. This means I ran out of supplies quicker but I was able to convince kaiser to give me more. My care team recommended I use a CGM but Kaiser wouldn't cover it unless I was on insulin. I used my FSA to pay for 6 months of FreeStyle Libre 3 sensors. Don't use these as a replacement for finger pricks. The numbers always trend lower or higher depending on the individual sensor. They **DO** help me follow the trends of my glucose levels without stressing about bringing my OneTouch Verio Flex everywhere.

**Food:** This is a tricky one. After my operation I learned I needed to limit my daily protein intake. So I modified my diet to one with low carbs and low protein. I eat heaps of non starchy vegetables with each meal and take a fiber supplement. I limit myself to about 4-6 oz of lean meat or fish in a meal and snack on nuts with fresh and or dried fruit. I have eaten very well. I still eat things I like. Vietnamese wraps. Healthy tacos. lots and lots of salads. I'm not a fan of salads and I DO HEAVILY DRESS those salads with creamy dressings  and yet I am still loosing weight over all. I also had to cut anything with phosphorus additives so that means no diet sodas. I wasn't a soda drinker so this wasn't a big deal for me. My go to drink is "Celestial Seasonings Black Cherry Berry Herbal Tea" this is like drinking Kool-aid without the sugar.

**Workout:** Not much of one at all. The operation to remove my kidney devastated me. I'm just now getting to a point where I don't feel limited in my range of movement. Only just this last week started on the stationary cycle. I do force myself to take walks when I have indulged on special occasions with a higher carb load than normal.

My resting hart rate has reaped the benefits of these changes. My Resting heart rate had peaked in the high 70s some time in March of this year. It has since fallen to the Lowe 50s and even the high 40s while sleeping. I directly attribute this to loosing about 15% of my body weight.

**FYI:** After 3 months there are no signs the cancer has spread. I'll be under observation for the next 5 years.

Happy to fill in any deals. Answer any questions or even discuss meals in more detail

title: Denial from my father after 3 years
post text:
I've had t1d for 3 whole years, I'm super positive, litterally laughted when I got the diagnosis. My father went through a spiral! First he acted like this was a huge secret, no one had to know, once he said that he had shared it with one person as it's something to hide, it's personal etc...
Then I showed off my devices, I'm a proud woman, I'm a feminist, I'm queer, I've always been strong and loud, wasn't gonna stop now.
He asked if I was approached by guys, or if my devices put people off (cause oh the sacrality of appearance!).
He constantly says things like "what's that sound?" "What was that?" At my alarms as in "why is your phone doing that weird and loud noise?!" To the point that I hope it doesn't ring cause wdym you don't know? What do you mean you don't get it? Do I really have to explain it to you? (Again)
He offered me a banana when I was high, doesn't know (or pretends but I don't think so) how this works.
And the last crazy thing is that he litterally said "I don't know what diabetics are supposed to look out for" (as in what do I bolus for).
He doesn't know, blames my grandad for my t1d (he didn't have diabetes but died of cancer at 92 or something but he blames my mom's family for everything), is ashamed, stresses me out and pretends it doesn't exist. When he used to cook I had to call my brother in advance so I would know if we were having carbs.
I wonder if I wasn't so confident about it, if I had fallen into a deep depression for it.
Idk was just thinking about it and got passed off, I could go on forever but best not to.
Anyways! Off we go with our lives! I'm loved by many and by me, I'm like and appreciated, I like and appreciate myself, am proud and able, fuck them.

title: LADA/Type 1.5
post text:
Hello everybody,
I was recently diagnosed with LADA (F54, healthy eating, doing different sports on regular basis, 5-7 times per week, since I was 28, marathon runner). I  always had a normal weight and very good health, until the menopause kicked in, 3 years ago, when I developed psoriasis and I put 10 kg out of the blue. In november 2024 at a routine check (I check everything twice a year), my BG was 145 and A1C was 7. My GP told me that I have type 2 diabetes and prescribed metformin. I didn't take it because was prescribed just before I was going on a 8 weeks long vacation in Asia and I was afraid of the side effects. What I did - I bought a glucometer, I tested my BG few times per day, every day, I restricted my carb intake at max 40g/day, doing same amount of sports. In January 2025 when I came back I retested everything, also checked the C Peptide and GAD. Last week I got a call from my doctor (endo) and she gave me the NEWS - I have LADA!!!  GAD65 WAS 87. I was completely gutted!!! My pancreas is still producing some insulin, my A1C is now 6.2, I already hace a CGM, since last week. My BG values are quite Ok, apart from a couple of hypos during the night, which may be related to the sensor. I'm not on insulin yet, but my doctor said to me that this not my fault and now is the honeymoon period and very soon I'll end up needing insulin. Are there similar situations, how long could you manage to preserve your beta cells and delaying the insulin? If you are on insulin, can you travel safe for longer periods?

title: Honeymoon phase
post text:
Hello all, 34 m. I got diagnosed as type 1 back in April 2024, I was hospitalized with DKA. When I first got out of the hospital I was taking 1 dose of insulin to every 5 carbs with 20 units of long acting insulin at night. I was constantly waking up in the 180s so my endocrinologist bumped me up to 26 units of long acting insulin. For the past 6+ years I was addicted to snorting fentanyl. I’m clean now, but it seems back when I was a junkie I was able to control my numbers better. I have a very active work life and I walk 15-23k steps a day. I never had a problem with low glucose numbers averaging from 130-220 throughout the day. Back in August I finally got clean. Around mid October my pancreas started producing insulin, I now only take 7 units of long acting insulin at night and roughly 1 unit for every 25-40 carbs. My endo says I’m in the honeymoon phase and that it won’t last forever so I need to be mindful If it’s my day off from work I’ll take 1 unit for 15-20 carbs and be fine but due to all my walking at work, I constantly drop. I go from 180s down to 100 in the matter of 10-12 minutes and I feel the low glucose symptoms. I have a problem just maintaining normal numbers. Outside of work I can maintain in the 130s but at work I feel like I have to eat so much to keep my numbers up. Somedays i don’t take any fast acting insulin if I don’t eat much cause I know with all my walking my numbers are gonna drop.
My question is how long did your honeymoon last? How long did you go without taking any insulin? I don’t like when I don’t take any fast acting, I’m scared of going back into dka. That was an experience I never want to go through again. 
My A1C was 11.7 when I was diagnosed, last time I got checked back in September I was down to 6.5
label: no stigma

title: Synjardy XR
post text: Longtime type 2 diabetic here, age 63 with a strong family history of T2DM.  I'd been struggling with disease progression (its year 15) while taking the max metformin dose of 2000 mg.  Doc added Jardiance 10 mg, which worked well in getting my numbers down, especially after returning to carb counting and intermittent fasting.  However, my stomach reacted poorly to the combination of metformin and Jardiance in the morning....and no, I cannot give up my morning coffee.  I realized I could switch to extended release, reduce my daily pill count and lower cost by switching to Synjardy XR so it seemed a win-win.  The thing is, doc prescribed the 5/1000 version of Synjardy XR and told me to take one pill once a day.  Her logic is that the second 1000 mg metformin pill I had been taking was because regular metformin only lasts 12 hours.  She acknowledged the lowering of Jardiance but I suspect she wants me to manage my diet better as I have been successful in doing so over the years. Three days in though and my evening sugars are rocketing to 250 after my dinner and snack.  I can get back into range if I eat on a 18:6 fasting schedule and eat less than 50 carbs a day but that's a challenge to do 7 days a week.  It was easier to do in the early years but its harder and harder as life goes on.  Anyone else switch from regular metformin/Jardiance to the Synjardy XR, and if so, how were you dosed? title: Discussion: Is a Low A1C Dangerous for Type 1 Diabetes? Doctor’s Warning vs. Good Control
post text:
I recently saw my primary doctor, and my A1C dropped from 6.6% to 5.6%. He said it was good but warned me not to go too low, which confused me. I know the goal is under 7%, and I understand the risks of going over 7%, but what are the dangers of having an A1C in the mid-5s with type 1?

For context, I was using insulin pens (not a pump) at the time and relied on my Dexcom G7 to track my glucose. My Time in Range was 91%, and I didn’t have frequent or severe lows. I went below 70 mg/dL about 2–3 times per week, but some weeks I was at 100% TIR with no lows. I still felt symptoms when I did go low. My lower A1C came from things like pre-bolusing, adjusting doses, and paying closer attention to trends.

I’ve since switched to a pump, but I’m curious—was my A1C at the time actually too low, or was my doctor just giving a general warning? Anyone else have a similar experience?

title: A Mixed Bag
post text:
I saw my endo today for my 3-month check. I'd say the news is MOSTLY good?

The main thing I'm excited about is my a1c. Loads of progress there - it went from 15+ at diagnosis to 5.6 three months later. I'm very proud of that. The doctor is also optimistic I will be able to get off insulin soon; we're going to taper down. I've also lost 15KG in those three months which I know isn't a lot compared to some, but it's been hard to do while on insulin and I'm pleased about it. I got the results back from my EMG/NCV test, and I do not have neuropathy. I also don't have retinopathy, according to the ophthalmologist.

The bad news is my uric acid and creatine are up. Which means I'm going to need to cut back on red meat, poultry and seafood. The doctor and nutritionist say the main issue is probably not enough water, which I am aware of and will try to be better about. I also still have high blood pressure, so I'm pretty concerned about my kidneys.  My SIL is currently dying of kidney failure at age 39, so it's on my mind a lot.

I really want to celebrate the good news, but the bad is putting a damper on things. I'd love some words of advice, or just reminders that there's a lot to celebrate here.
label: no stigma

title: Why does my hair get really dry and frizzy after cold water showering?
post text:
Male here. I've had naturally soft hair for most of my life—over three decades—until a few years ago. I could use any shampoo, any water temperature, and wash my hair in any country without issues. However, about four years ago, I was diagnosed as prediabetic (HbA1c 6.3) and was prescribed Metformin. I vaguely recall my hair texture changing around the same time. Currently my HbA1c is 6.6. 

Back then, my B12, B9, and Vitamin D levels were on the lower end, but I corrected them. Now, in my early 40s, I initially assumed this was just part of aging. For the first time, I started paying attention to shampoos and conditioners, but no matter what I use, the difference is minimal. Conditioners keep my hair soft for maybe an hour, but after that, it becomes dry and frizzy very quickly.

People often ask if I’m using a styling product because my hair stands up on its own immediately after a shower and stays that way all day—without any product. Despite this change in texture, I have no hair fall and my hair growth is unaffected. I've been taking daily head showers for almost 30 years and rarely used conditioner—maybe 25 times in my life, most of that since this issue began.

Does anyone know if this is related to metformin and me turning diabetic? Does metformin affect vitamin absorption and can cause a deficiency?   Any thoughts or feedback would be greatly appreciated!

title: Carb replacements and struggle to eat
post text:
I was diagnosed diabetic around summer of this year at a1c of 11. Last test it was at 6.3. I've had a lot of struggles with food and under-eating. I have some food/texture sensitivities that have made it particularly hard to adjust to this lifestyle and I guess I'm looking for carb substitutes/replacements. I used to eat a lot of foods with noodles or rice for a "base". So for example, a bowl of rice with meat, veg, and a sauce on top. I know there's whole grain carbs, but I still can't have a bowl of those. I feel like I can't eat things like curries anymore because I have nothing to put the curry on, if that makes sense. Lentils are the most common replacement but they actually send me pretty high and keep me there so I can't rely on them.

I'm also struggling with meeting caloric goals and just wanting to eat. I would say I have a total aversion to food nowadays unless it's food I'm not supposed to be eating, which just means I either don't eat or I force myself to eat and feel sick for the rest of the night. I'm at a complete loss on what to do anymore. Its been months. People told me it was most likely the metformin and it would go away but it hasn't. I didn't really think it was the metformin anyways. My diet is pretty much a protein shake and a cheesestick for lunch and then for dinner it's a meat (chicken or fish) and broccoli or green beans. That's...about it, honestly. I've tried keto friendly snacks and to be honest, they're just kind of terrible. I've thrown away probably $100 by now because I buy an expensive "safe" snack, have one bite, and it's just disgusting. I've been eating just yogurt and cheese and meat and broccoli for months, aside from a short bout of cheating after a high period of stress. After cheating/eating carbs, I actually had energy for the first time since the lifestyle change. I actually felt normal again. Now that I'm back to eating diabetic friendly, I'm once again exhausted and miserable. 

I'm at a loss. I don't know what to do anymore. I feel like I'm not functioning like a normal person anymore. I'm exhausted and depressed literally all the time. Thinking of eating makes me feel sick and at points just makes me cry. There were times I went days without eating anything other than protein shakes. It's been almost six months at this point and I just don't expect it to get better anymore. I don't want to live like this anymore. I go to therapy but my therapist doesn't know how to help me with this since there's just not really anything to do about it. Has anyone else gone through this for so long? Is there even any point hoping it gets better?
label: yes stigma

title: Exercise-Driven Glucose Peaks
post text:
First post here. I'm a 57M, diagnosed 6 years ago w/T2 and A1C at 10.7. I successfully brought it down to the mid-6s since first diagnosis by cutting carbs, continuing a pre-existing exercise regimen (mostly running 2.5-3.5 miles several days per week), and Metformin + Jardiance. 

I recently strayed a bit from my diet and wanted to get back on track, so I bought an over-the-counter CGM (Lingo) and have been monitoring my glucose levels over the last two weeks. I drop into the 80s/90s overnight and peak above 140 after some meals (rarely above 160), but am trying to keep my levels below 140 when possible based on many conservative recommendations I've seen.

The one major exception though, which I can't seem to control, is my peak levels after exercise. Sometimes they approach 200 within about 30 minutes after my run, and I am often 160+ with a span of around an hour where levels exceed 140. I run in the mornings before work.

Three questions for anyone with similar experience or knowledge in this area:

1. Can I or should I attempt to control highs from exercise?
2. Are exercise-driven highs as harmful as those that result from consuming food?
3. Should I wait until my levels fall back to my daytime baseline before consuming food that could push them back up?

Thank you,
kwc
label: no stigma

title: Feeling dizzy with on/off headaches
post text: Diagnosed type 2 diabetic in Nov '23 and my doctor increased me from 1 pill of metformin er 750mg to now 2 pills due to my A1C going up from 6.0 to 8.1. Just started the new dosage the last week of Dec. I started my period Sunday 12/29 first 2 days were heavy but has lightened up to now just spotting and currently on the last few days of my cycle. I started feeling like my throat was sore but only when I yawned really wide and I've been slightly stuffed up, this started Tues 12/31. I think I just have a minor cold. However I keep getting this dizzy spells and a minor headache that comes and goes away throughout the day. Today I started having this feeling in my head almost like a wave going from the front to the back of my head, this has also been on/off. I am planning on going to the urgent care in the morning but I was wondering if anyone else has had similar dizziness and on/off headache and wave/pulsing feeling while being sick?? I checked my blood sugar levels before taking any meds or anything this morning and was at 188mg/dl then are cereal and an apple and checked roughly 2 hours later and was 168mg/dl.

title: Regression
post text:
I’ve had a1c in the 5.7-5.9 range for a couple of years now. I’m off medication but I’m pretty sure my numbers are regressing despite sticking to the same diet. I’m guessing this is just the progressive part of the disease despite being under control. 

I’m guessing my a1c is 6.2 now but I’ll find out later this month. I haven’t gained any weight. I could probably tighten my diet some but it’s frustrating what was working before isn’t working as well and I’m guessing I’ll have to go on some sort of medicine to help. Metformin XR gave me gi issues so I might want to look at something else.

title: 11.7 to 6.3 in 4 months
post text:
I couldn’t ask for a better Christmas gift!
Definitely felt the world crumble around me when I was diagnosed, but with the help of medication, a diet change and more exercise I was able to bring my numbers down to a much better number. 

I’ve been seeing lots of posts from other young people getting diagnosed and wanted to share my success story. Don’t stress about everything you eat, it really is about moderation and taking care of yourself in other ways. 

Wishing everyone a very healthy, and happy new year!  Enjoy your treats, a few days won’t hurt you in the long run. My cookie quota for the year was certainly hit this past week. 

Feel free to DM me if you ever want to chat or need some encouragement

title: t2d, what qas your diagnosis like?
post text:
asking because i fear ive been misdiagnosed.

my a1c was 11.7% when i was diagnosed, blood sugar above 350. ever since i was diagnosed i just always felt like type 2 wasnt correct.

i have seen an endocrinologist. i tested negative for antibodies, my c-peptide was on the low end of normal at 1.6. i dont see endocrine again until may.

when i spoke to endocrinology about my test results, i asked her some questions and explained some things.

i asked her about onset of type 2 diabetes. she told me that type 2 diabetes is usually slow progressing and has a lot of warning signs before youre full blown diabetic, but that didnt happen with me. i felt like i was very sick very suddenly.

i asked her if type 2 diabetics typically gain or lose weight before diagnosis and she told me its different for everybody but usually you gain weight because of the insulin overproduction. i told her that didnt happen to me either, and i lost loads of weight and despite eating 4 times the amount of food, i gained no weight at all.

i explained to her i didnt have many of the usual symptoms and the ones i did have i believed were dietary and i was eating very clean at the time of diagnosis and exercising regularly and i never felt better at all. i didnt pee a lot, did have fatigue which i thought was long covid, i wasnt thirsty, nothing.

she told me, while it is possible to be t1d with no antibodies, and yes my c-peptide is low, but its not low enough to be considered type 1 diabetes, so i am type 2. this conversation was because they wanted to start me on new meds, but im not fully convinced im type 2 and would rather not be on meds. they wanted me on ozempic or trulicity, but i keep declining because the lantus is working. they just say "well it'll always be effective" which i know that, but my diabetes has been essentially under control since i started and im eating worse than i did at diagnosis. i just dont get it. i was told "well if you happen to be type 1, trulicity wont hurt you" and i understand that but if you dont think im type 1 why is it a concern?

my blood tests have been pristine my entire life, and even now with diabetes they are, which led the np at my primary care to say he didnt think i was type 2 at first.

diabetes is in my family on my moms side. her father had it, she has it, i have 2 siblings with diabetes, one type 1 and the other is type 2. one sibling was prediabetic. the other so far doesnt seem impacted.

i have been on lantus since august and my numbers have been very good, i have a cgm so i can see everything and so can my clinic. i think the biggest indicators that i have been misdx'ed are the lantus working so well and the fact that it was so sudden. i dont even think my body responded to being prediabetic at all. that plus my cholesterol was pristine and my c-peptide is low.

so im asking, when you were diagnosed, what was your symptoms? did you gain weight? lose weight? not at all? which symptoms did you have?

  
edit: i forgot to mention, one of the big things for me was dietary and lifestyle changes didnt help me. if i ate a salad, i felt just as fatigued and "off" as if i had ice cream. now i can eat whatever and feel fine. i just really want to know whats going on with me, whether im right or wrong. id rather be wrong and know for sure, than wrong and wondering.

title: Help with diabetic mother who won't make changes.
post text:
I need help with my mid 60s diabetic mother. She let undiagnosed diabetes get so far she ended up in the ER and hospitalized summer 2023. 
 
 Me, my 3 siblings, and father haven't had success with getting through to her about lifestyle changes. Or her best friend who's in the medical field. When we bring up the seriousness of her condition and managing it, she downplays it and quickly changes the subject. 
 
 Her diet and lifestyle hasn't changed at all. Inconsistent with medication use. My older sister, who's a nurse, lives nearby and has repeatedly tried to get her to understand how important it is to maintain consistency with lifestyle and medication. It doesn't stick. 
 
 I'm looking for advice about how to get through to somebody who's in denial about their condition. I know we can only do so much, but we don't want to give up end up with her having a stroke or worse.

title: Ideas
post text:
Hello, I tried posting this yesterday but I needed to reword it.

I brought a juicer but NOT for fruit. I got it for vegetables. I have already juiced broccoli, Kale, and spinach. I wanted to know what are some other combinations I can do to HELP keep my a1c numbers down.

I'm not going on a juicing diet. I was told to watch my carb intake and eat 6 smaller meals through the day. I have 5 kids and eating smaller meals is not convent at all. We have a lot going on personally. 

I don't like sweets, not a fruit fan either. My issue was bread and potatoes. My a1c was 7.0 a few months ago. My new lab work showed 6.7 which I know is not a difference. Like I said I already juiced broccoli, Kale, and spinach. Which helped me surprisingly not feel hungry. My dinner consisted of tuna lettuce wraps. I think I'm doing better with carbs and have started exercising more

I just want to know, what are some vegetables that have lower carbs or that hasn't spiked your a1c

title: Confused on A1C reading at home vs doctors

The appointment was not diabetic related however he did ask that maybe we go ahead and look at that again because I haven’t been checked in a while and let’s see how we’re progressing so I let them do it. They did a finger prick blood draw.
